episodeScores.episodeTitle: Radio and Your Calls

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is a radio nostalgia and career retrospective show with no substantive connection to current news or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode flows as a loosely organized conversation with anecdotes strung together thematically but without clear segments or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: A few self-contained anecdotes (the bathroom break debut, the George Jones format-flip disaster) are quotable but require some setup to land fully.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Supreme Court and Columbus City Counsel Update

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages a specific Supreme Court ruling on transgender issues and related state-vs-federal jurisdiction questions, making it substantively on-topic for News/Politics, though the opening segment is off-topic celebrity chatter.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode meanders from John Cena's hair transplant into Supreme Court commentary without a clear transition, and the discussion drifts loosely between transgender sports, gay marriage history, and bathroom policy with no discernible segment structure.

clipAbility.rationale: The slippery-slope argument and the 'we have to differentiate' passage have some standalone potential, but most moments require surrounding context or are embedded in rambling tangents that undercut clean extraction.

card.tagline: The accidental on-air debut that launched a radio career nobody planned
card.description: Chuck Douglas recounts stumbling into his first broadcast, a disastrous country station launch, and what Desert Shield taught him about showing up.
card.reason: Because this episode offers an insider look at the radio business

scored.feedTitle: The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg
scored.description:
Greetings and Welcome to The 405 Coffee Break with O.K. Solberg

New episodes tend to air over the local KMMR radio station @ 5 minutes past 4PM each M-F. And have been doing so, nearly every week since Sept 2018.

I'm D.J. Rasmussen, O.K.s friend since junior high, possibly your neighbor & this websites maintainer, whom strives to get each episode's show notes written, the transcript proofed and the audio posted to the internet within a few hours of that days KMMR air time. NOTE: recently been publishing most new releases by 4:30PM.

Thanks for visiting and I hope you enjoy the time we can spend together.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The 405 Aired July 17th, 2026

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode covers commodity spot prices and a motivational parable about neighborly kindness — no political or news content of any kind.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves from a brief commodity price rundown into an extended parable with a closing benediction, but the segments feel loosely stitched together rather than deliberately structured.

clipAbility.rationale: The Walter parable has a self-contained moral payoff, but the folksy, slow-burn delivery requires patience and context to land as a standalone clip.

transcript.scoringWindow: WEBVTT 00:00:00.000 --> 00:00:09.040 I want to again welcome you to The 405 Coffee Break. Guys, friday weekend's coming. Get you a cup of coffee. Glass iced tea or bottled water. Let's see what's happening. 00:00:09.040 --> 00:00:29.310 Spring wheat $6 and 3 pennies, $6.03. 550lb steer calf. You can contract them now, top end, $5.45 a pound. And a 100lb fat lamb in Billings, a range, $3.30 to $3.35. But guys, there's more, much more. 00:00:29.310 --> 00:00:52.755 Oh, oh, oh, do I like stories, and I found one for you. If you have a moment, lend me your ear, and I'll share with you the story that I found, and it's titled the neighbor who never kept score. Are you ready? Are you listening? If I give you a quiz afterwards, can you tell me what's in the story? 00:00:52.755 --> 00:01:19.755 Or Jude just listened to me as background noise. Listen. The neighbor who never kept score. Every town seems to have one, not the richest person, not the loudest, not the one whose name is on the biggest building, just the one everybody knows. In one small Midwestern town, there lived a man, and his name was Walter. 00:01:20.475 --> 00:01:43.485 If your car wouldn't start on a cold January morning, Walter somehow appeared with jumper cables. The young couple, moving into their 1st home. There he was carrying boxes before anyone else even asked if an elderly widow needed a light bulb changed. Walter had already climbed the ladder. 00:01:44.605 --> 00:02:12.805 He wasn't trying to become important. He simply noticed people. He had an unusual habit. When someone was talking, he listened. Not to answer, but to understand. People left conversations with Walter feeling lighter than when they arrived. He remembered birthdays. He learned children's name. He celebrated other people's victories as though they were his own. 00:02:14.245 --> 00:02:36.510 He quietly cried with those who mourned. Years passed. Business owners trusted him because he had never stretched the truth. Neighbors recommended him because he never took advantage of anyone. When jobs became available, someone always said, have you talked to Walter? 00:02:38.275 --> 00:03:06.070 When a committee needed a chairman, they look for Walter. When disagreement surfaces surfaced, both sides wanted Walter in the room. His life became something money could never purchase. Trust, friendship, respect, well, peace. One afternoon, a young man finally asked him, Walter, how did you get so many people to like you? 00:03:06.630 --> 00:03:24.860 Walter smiled. I never tried to make people like me, he said. I just tried to make sure they knew I liked them. Funny thing about kindness, it has a way of traveling in circles. The smile you give today may return years later when you need one yourself. 00:03:25.340 --> 00:03:53.480 The hand you lend today may become the hand that lifts you tomorrow. The encouragement you offer may echo through generations that you'll never meet. Walter never became famous. He never expected applause. Yet when he passed away, the church could hardly hold the crowd. Mechanics stood beside lawyers, teachers beside farmers, children beside grandparents. 00:03:54.040 --> 00:04:10.145 Each one carried a story that began with the simple words, Walter helped me. Perhaps that's what Jesus had in mind all along when he said, thou shalt love your neighbor as yourself. Matthew 22:39 00:04:12.145 --> 00:04:25.566 I guess Jesus wasn't doling out a punishment when he said love your neighbor as yourself. It would seem he was providing you with a means of a blessing, as would Walter have told you. 00:04:25.886 --> 00:04:33.806 So until next time, go out and have a good weekend. As you go out there, remember now, don't be bitter.

card.tagline: Commodity prices, a parable about Walter, and a Friday send-off
card.description: O.K. Solberg opens with spring wheat and lamb prices before sharing a story about a small-town man whose quiet kindness outlasted his fame.
card.reason: Because this episode offers a reflective Friday wind-down for rural and agricultural listeners

scored.description: TribCast is a weekly political podcast hosted by Matthew Watkins and Eleanor Klibanoff, and featuring a rotating cast of editors, reporters and other guests. The TribCast is returning as a limited-run weekly series during the 2025 legislative session.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: An ICE shooting roils Houston

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on a specific, timely ICE shooting incident in Houston, examining immigration enforcement policy, local vs. federal jurisdiction, and the Trump administration's deportation crackdown with concrete data and on-the-ground reporting.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline moving from the legal context of deportation vulnerability to community reaction to investigative prospects, though the transcript shows some meandering transitions and mid-thought interruptions between speakers.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments stand out — particularly the explanation that people in the process of legalizing status can still be deported, the statistic that community arrests jumped from 14% to 36% under Trump, and the vivid description of silent Houston neighborhoods with residents peeking through windows.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Texas Dems host their own unity fest

topicRelevance.rationale: Directly covers the Texas Democratic Party convention, a live intraparty unity fight between Talerico and Crockett, and a contested party chair re-election — all timely, specific Texas political developments.

contentStructure.rationale: Conversational but follows a clear throughline moving from convention energy to Black voter outreach to Crockett's absence to party chair dynamics, though transitions are loose and pacing is uneven.

clipAbility.rationale: The Crockett-as-afterthought exchange and the Cornyn/Crockett 'sour grapes' parallel are quotable standalone moments, but most insights require the surrounding conversation to land fully.

card.tagline: ICE shoots a Houston man — and a community goes silent in fear
card.description: Texas Tribune reporters break down the legal vulnerabilities, rising community arrests, and chilling neighborhood fear following a deadly ICE encounter in Houston.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a breaking immigration enforcement story reshaping Texas communities

scored.description:
The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs is a raw, unflinching look at the dramatic collapse of one of hip-hop’s biggest moguls. For decades, Sean 'Diddy' Combs seemed untouchable—a figure who rose from the streets to become a titan of the music, fashion, and entertainment industries. His Bad Boy Entertainment label defined a generation of hip-hop, and his entrepreneurial spirit made him a household name. But beneath the surface of this glittering success lay darker currents—currents that have now surged into the spotlight as the empire he built threatens to crumble beneath the weight of scandal and serious criminal allegations.

This podcast dives deep into the allegations that have sent shockwaves through the entertainment world. At the center of it all are the accusers—those who have come forward with shocking claims of misconduct, manipulation, and abuse. These aren’t just sensational headlines—they are stories that expose a side of Sean Combs the public never saw. The Diddy Diaries explores how these accusations began to surface, what drove his accusers to finally speak out, and how the legal system has responded.

But this isn’t just about Diddy’s public image taking a hit—The Diddy Diaries zeroes in on the high-stakes battle that Diddy now faces to stay out of jail. Each episode unpacks the legal drama as it unfolds in real-time: the charges, the court proceedings, and the fight for his freedom. Once a man who had it all—fame, fortune, and influence—Diddy now stands at the center of a legal storm that threatens to strip him of everything, including his freedom.


We trace the events that led up to this moment, looking at how Diddy’s larger-than-life persona masked a pattern of behavior that would ultimately bring him to this point. Listeners will get a front-row seat to the high-stakes legal battles, where the possibility of incarceration looms large. The tension is palpable as Diddy’s legal team works to combat the mounting evidence against him, while his accusers push for justice.

Through riveting storytelling, The Diddy Diaries paints a full picture of Diddy’s downfall, from the heights of his stardom to the depths of his legal fight for survival. This is not a story about maintaining power or influence—it’s a fight to avoid the ultimate consequence: prison. As the allegations mount and the legal drama intensifies, The Diddy Diaries delivers a compelling narrative of a man grappling with the very real possibility of losing everything he once held dear.

With every episode, listeners are drawn deeper into this unfolding saga of celebrity, power, and justice. The Diddy Diaries not only explores the high-profile case but also delves into the human cost behind the headlines. As Diddy’s world unravels, so too does the carefully constructed image he spent decades building. This podcast is a must-listen for anyone who wants to understand the full scope of Sean Combs’ downfall, where the stakes couldn’t be higher and the outcome remains uncertain.

Prepare for a rollercoaster ride through the most shocking and consequential scandal in recent entertainment history. Tune in to The Diddy Diaries: The Downfall of Sean Combs to witness the dramatic fall of a man who once ruled the music world but now fights for his freedom in the face of life-altering allegations.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Mega Edition:  Todd Blanche And The Mechanics Of The Epstein Coverup (7/20/26)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on the Epstein files political fallout — Pam Bondi's DOJ handling, Susie Wiles' Vanity Fair disclosures, Melania Trump's White House remarks, and calls for a Special Counsel — all timely, specific political developments.

contentStructure.rationale: There's a clear throughline moving from Melania's statement to the Susie Wiles/Vanity Fair story to the Bondi 'binders' debacle, but the pacing is loose and commentary frequently overtakes the narrative thread.

clipAbility.rationale: Several punchy, self-contained moments — the 'binders full of nothingness' breakdown, the influencer rant, and the whataboutism takedown — would work as standalone previews with minimal setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Mega Edition:  The Virginia Roberts Deposition That Helped Expose Epstein And Maxwell (Part 7-9) (7/11/23)

topicRelevance.rationale: This is celebrity crime/true crime content centered on the Epstein-Maxwell scandal read as deposition transcript, not substantive news or political analysis — it scores in the 0-20 range despite touching on figures of public interest.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode is a long deposition read-through with minimal editorial framing or segmentation visible in the excerpt, resulting in loose, repetitive Q&A pacing without clear navigational signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: A few exchanges — particularly Virginia's statement about 'a blur of so many girls' and the confirmation that both Epstein and Maxwell asked her to return — have standalone emotional weight, but most moments require surrounding context to land.

scored.description:
A daily briefing on the AI systems, products, companies, and policy shifts that are just becoming possible.

Want a podcast for your own topics? Join early access: https://www.barelypossible.to/waitlist/?source_path=public_feed&feed_source=rss

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Barely Possible - 2026-07-20

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode substantively covers a live Apple vs. OpenAI trade-secrets lawsuit with IPO implications, plus current AI governance and ownership debates — real policy-adjacent news hooks, though the Nolan/Odyssey segment drifts toward cultural commentary.

contentStructure.rationale: The host explicitly signals transitions between segments and articulates a through-line connecting all three stories, though the pacing within each segment is dense and the Nolan detour slightly disrupts momentum.

clipAbility.rationale: Several genuinely standalone moments — the Nolan 'transparent horse' quote, the missionary Bible training-data passage, and the 'lawsuit is the weapon' framing — would each work as a 30-45 second preview with minimal setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Barely Possible - 2026-07-13

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers substantive policy-adjacent topics — BLM grazing regulation rewrites, RFK Jr.'s vaccine schedule changes, and AI regulation — but is primarily a tech/builder briefing that treats political developments as secondary signals rather than its core subject.

contentStructure.rationale: The host uses a clear throughline (who controls shared resources) to connect disparate stories, with deliberate transitions and explicit signposting, though the pacing loosens in the OpenAI cluster where multiple items are deferred or declined.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments exist — the toll booth metaphor, the slushie machine builder-brain riff, and the 'process is where the power lives' line — each of which could stand alone as a 30-45 second preview.

latestEpisode.title: Barely Possible - 2026-07-20

clip.text: other direction. Let me shift from who owns the technology to who trusts the technology. Christopher Nolan. The director. His new version of The Odyssey is currently tearing up the box office, and he sat down for an interview with Hugo Travers, who publishes on YouTube under the name HugoDécrypte. And Travers asks him this beautiful question. The Trojan horse plays a big role in the film, obviously, it's The Odyssey. And Travers wonders out loud, is AI maybe a Trojan horse? Something you welcome into your daily life, and then one day it becomes something darker, something else? And Nolan, laughing, gives what I think is the best one-liner

transcript.scoringWindow: flip the whole board over. Because there's a recent piece from TechCrunch, written by Kate Park, about a nonprofit called Current AI, and the whole premise is: what if none of these guys should own it? Not OpenAI, not Google, not Anthropic, not Moonshot. What if AI is supposed to be a public thing, like the water main, like the road out front, like the World Wide Web itself? Now I want to be straight with you about the timing here, because that matters. The centerpiece of this story, a device called Suno Sutra, that goes back to February, at the India AI Summit. This is not a thing that just happened this morning. But the reporting is recent, and the reason I'm leading with it is that it's the cleanest counter-argument I've seen to the entire "who owns the model" conversation we've been having for two weeks straight. Here's the setup, and it's a good one. Picture a farmer in rural India. She takes a photo of a plant that's dying in her field. She wants to look it up, figure out what's wrong. But she doesn't speak English. And the CEO of Current AI, a woman named Ayah Bdeir, she makes this point that stuck with me. She says, quote, "In India, there are hundreds of different languages and dialects, and right now AI is not representing them." So they teamed up with Bhashini, which is the Indian government's AI language division, and they built this thing called Suno Sutra. It's Hindi for "listening chronicles." It's a pocket-sized device, it runs AI in twenty-two Indian languages, and here's the kicker, it works offline. No internet required. And it's open-sourced so developer communities can build on top of it. Now, why does a founder care about a pocket translator for farmers in India? Stick with me, because the business logic underneath this is sharper than it looks. Bdeir came out of Mozilla, where she led their AI strategy, and before that she founded littleBits, that STEM education company that reached a lot of kids before it sold to Sphero back in 2019. So this isn't some naive do-gooder who wandered in off the street. She frames Current AI as a public-private partnership. The French government seeded it with a hundred million dollars. Then the Ford Foundation, the MacArthur Foundation, DeepMind, and Salesforce all piled in, and the total committed funding is now around four hundred million. And she draws a hard line on what those people are. She says, quote, "They're not investors; they're funders." That's a real distinction. Nobody's expecting a return. Nobody owns equity in the outcome. And here's the part that should make every builder sit up. She draws a distinction between what she's doing and what the big companies do when they go multilingual. She says, quote, "Big tech builds multilingual models to expand their market, regardless of consent or context." And then she gives you the concrete version, which is chilling if you actually think about it. She says for Indigenous languages, missionary Bible translations become the training data before those communities have set any rules at all. Think about that. The first digital representation of your entire culture, your entire language, gets scraped from a document some missionary wrote a hundred years ago, and now that's the ground truth the machine learns from. You don't get a vote. You don't even get a phone call. Her line on data ownership is about as blunt as it gets. She says, quote, "There are different models and proposals for who owns data in various communities, but one thing is sure: it shouldn't be a company in Silicon Valley trying to make a select few thousand people wealthier." Now, here's where I put on my skeptic hat, because that's my job and you'd fire me if I didn't. This is a four-hundred-million-dollar organization, and the actual money going out the door to build things is small. Their first grant round was three point two million dollars, split across four organizations. Kenya, Lebanon, and the Brazilian Amazon. And I asked myself the obvious question, which is: what can you actually do with three point two million split four ways when OpenAI and Google are lighting billions on fire every quarter? And Bdeir has an answer ready, which tells me she's heard the question before. She says, quote, "Scale is not always the measure. That is the Big Tech paradigm." Her example is an Indigenous elder in the Brazilian Amazon using a tool built in Kenya to pass down ecological knowledge in their own language. Now, is that a business? No. Is it charming? Yes. But here's why I think a founder should actually track this, and it's not the charity angle. It's the architecture angle. Look at what they shipped in Geneva earlier this month. A chatbot called Alpha Chat, assembled in seven weeks by a coalition of ten organizations, including Hugging Face, Mozilla, and MIT Media Lab. Each one brought a piece. One brought a language model, one brought safety tooling, one brought the compute. That's a modular, assembled-from-parts stack. And they also struck a deal with Sakana AI, the Tokyo startup that does what it calls Sovereign AI, to build a shared open-source stack for Japanese and for the Global South. So the thing to watch here isn't the feel-good story about the farmer. It's whether a loose coalition can bolt together a working product in seven weeks out of donated parts. Because if that model works, if you can assemble a serviceable stack from open pieces without owning any of them, that's a real threat to the "the model is the whole company" thesis. And it's a real opportunity for anyone building on top who doesn't want to be a permanent tenant in OpenAI's building. That's the piece I'd file away. Now, funny enough, the guy who runs the biggest AI blockbuster of the summer is thinking about the exact same thing from the other direction. Let me shift from who owns the technology to who trusts the technology. Christopher Nolan. The director. His new version of The Odyssey is currently tearing up the box office, and he sat down for an interview with Hugo Travers, who publishes on YouTube under the name HugoDécrypte. And Travers asks him this beautiful question. The Trojan horse plays a big role in the film, obviously, it's The Odyssey. And Travers wonders out loud, is AI maybe a Trojan horse? Something you welcome into your daily life, and then one day it becomes something darker, something else? And Nolan, laughing, gives what I think is the best one-liner about AI I've heard all year. He says, quote, "I think AI is a Trojan horse that everybody knows the Greeks are inside." And then he pushes it further, he says it's "a transparent horse, it's made of glass." Now sit with that for a second, because it's genuinely sharp. The whole trick of the original Trojan horse was that nobody knew what was inside. It was a gift. The betrayal only works because of the surprise. Nolan's point is that with AI, the surprise is gone. Everybody already suspects. He says, quote, "I've never seen a technology advancing so rapidly that's been so completely rejected by the public. Everybody's suspicion of it is so extreme, particularly young people." And he singles out the kids. He talks about people his children's age immediately looking at AI videos online and calling it "AI slop." Coining that term, he says, and just putting it in a box. Now here's why I care about this as a matter of business and not just as a movie-guy quote. Nolan calls this "a very healthy skepticism." And he says the skepticism has to extend not just to the technology but to the motives of the people handing it to you. His words: "The motives of the people giving it to us also have to be viewed with skepticism. That's when we'll get the best out of a new technology." If you're building a consumer product with AI baked in right now, that's your market condition. That's the weather report. You are not launching into a crowd of eager early adopters who think everything you touch is magic. You're launching into a crowd where the youngest, most online, most culturally influential segment has already coined a dismissive slang term and slapped it on the whole category. "Slop." That's a brand problem you inherit for free the day you ship. And Nolan, for what it's worth, is not a pure Luddite. He told the New York Times recently he thinks of himself as a "techno-skeptic," not a technophobe. His whole complaint is that new technology gets sold to people "at the expense of systems that might still be valid and viable." He said about film, "We almost lost film." That's a builder's critique, actually. Don't throw out the working thing just because the new thing is shiny. And notice the through-line between these first two stories, because it's real, I'm not forcing it. Bdeir's whole pitch is that you shouldn't trust a Silicon Valley company to decide for everyone. Nolan's whole pitch is that the public already doesn't. One's the supply side of distrust, one's the demand side. Same trust deficit, two ends of the pipe. Alright, let's get into the deep dive, because this is the one that matters most if you're building a company, and it's the one with the sharpest teeth for founders. Let's talk about Apple suing OpenAI, and what it actually does to OpenAI's plans to go public. Now, we touched the lawsuit itself back on July 11th. The bones of it: Apple filed a trade-secrets suit accusing OpenAI of a pattern of misconduct aimed at getting current and former Apple employees to hand over confidential information. So I'm not re-litigating the filing. What's fresh here is a really good discussion on TechCrunch's Equity podcast, with Kirsten Korosec, Sean O'Kane, and Anthony Ha, about the thing nobody was talking about on day one: what this does to the IPO. Let me lay out the stakes, because they interlock in a nasty way. OpenAI's first hardware product is reportedly a mobile smart speaker, done with Jony Ive and company. That's the thing Apple's lawsuit is circling, because the allegation is that OpenAI poached Apple people and used Apple trade secrets to build a competing hardware product. And they named names. The complaint names OpenAI's chief hardware officer, Tang Tan, directly. And it claims more than four hundred former Apple employees now work at OpenAI. Now here's Sean O'Kane's read, and it's the one I'd underline. He says, quote, "Even setting aside whether or not the court grants any kind of injunctive relief or any kind of restraining order over what OpenAI is doing, it just naturally can lead to that sort of situation where it's going to cause some delays in what OpenAI is working on. Which I'm sure was probably part of the reasoning behind Apple doing this. They don't do this stuff willy nilly." Chew on that, because it's the whole game. Apple may not even need to win. The lawsuit itself is the weapon. It's sand in the gears. Every hour a lawyer spends on discovery is an hour not spent shipping the speaker. And here's the part that makes it dangerous, the timing. Sean lays it out. OpenAI has filed confidentially for an IPO. The thinking is it could happen as early as the end of this year, or early next, if you take Sam Altman's cautious language at face value. And here's the collision. Sean's point is that OpenAI's business right now is overwhelmingly software. They're not really counting a hardware business in the numbers yet. But when you go public, you walk into a room full of bankers and investors and you pitch them on your total addressable market, your TAM, how big this thing can get.

card.tagline: Apple's OpenAI lawsuit may not need to win to destroy the IPO
card.description: This episode breaks down how Apple's trade-secrets suit could derail OpenAI's public offering before a single share is sold, plus who should own AI infrastructure.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks the legal threat quietly reshaping the AI industry

scored.description:
"The Neutral Corner" boxing podcast covers all the latest news in the fight game. Every Monday, host Michael Montero reviews/previews all the fights from around the world and takes listener calls. He is never afraid to ruffle feathers when he gives hot takes or goes on heated rants.

Montero has contributed to numerous boxing publications in multiple countries, performed ringside commentary around the USA and is an International Boxing Hall of Fame voter.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Episode 389: Jaron "Boots" Ennis stops Xander Zayas; Oleksandr Usyk vacates Titles

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a boxing analysis podcast covering fight results and fighter rankings — sports content with no substantive political or news policy dimension.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves from Usyk belt-vacating commentary to a guest interview with reasonable transitions, though the live-stream format produces some meandering and tangential asides.

clipAbility.rationale: The duck-vs-avoidance distinction and the Ward/Usyk comparison offer quotable moments, but most require boxing-fan context to land as standalone previews.

card.tagline: Downtown Louisville is getting pianos, murals, and string lights this summer
card.description: Rebecca Fleishaker walks through new public art, two-way street conversions, and Louisville's first open-street string lighting project coming to South Fourth Street.
card.reason: Because this episode covers what's changing in downtown Louisville right now

scored.description:
"Tavis Smiley" is the flagship program for KBLA, America's newest progressive radio network and LA's first Black-owned talk station. Broadcast live across southern California on 1580AM and around the world on KBLA1580.com and TuneIn, this podcast presents the best of Tavis Smiley's conversations with activists, influencers, thought leaders, and cultural vanguards.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/tavis-smiley--6286410/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Courtland Cox joins Tavis Smiley

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages current political developments — the SAVE Act, Supreme Court voting rights rulings, Trump 2.0 voter suppression efforts, and the stalled John Lewis Voting Rights Act — anchored to a specific anniversary date and a live presidential speech.

contentStructure.rationale: There is a discernible interview format with a clear throughline around voting rights, but pacing is uneven — the host's extended monologue before the guest speaks and the guest's mid-conversation resource plug interrupt the flow.

clipAbility.rationale: Courtland Cox's explanation of why voting rights legislation fails — framing it as economic self-interest rather than goodwill, with the Musk/SpaceX example — is a genuinely standalone, quotable moment that needs minimal setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Dr. Sarah L. Webb joins Tavis Smiley

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with live political fault lines — DSA electoral wins, Democratic Party ideological fractures, defund-the-police debates, and third-party viability — but the published date of 2026-06-30 makes some references (Bernie 2020, early DSA wins) feel temporally ambiguous rather than sharply current.

contentStructure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline moving from intra-Democratic ideological conflict to third-party prospects to DSA victories to defund-the-police, but transitions are conversational and loose, with a mid-segment Anita Baker digression that disrupts the political flow.

clipAbility.rationale: Several passages — West's 'neoliberal plantation' line, 'if all we have is the Democratic Party you're gonna lead folk into the crackhouse,' and the Bernie's babies framing — are punchy and self-contained enough to work as standalone previews without heavy setup.

latestEpisode.title: Courtland Cox joins Tavis Smiley

scored.description:
Live from West Virginia’s Capital City, with stories from every corner of the Mountain State. From Noon to 3 p.m., MetroNews Midday connects West Virginians with the news that matters most — hosted by 13 News and Tonight Live anchor Amanda Barren and Dave Allen of MetroNews. Amanda and Dave deliver up-to-the-minute headlines and breaking news while amplifying voices from around the state through insightful interviews and real-time conversation.

📺 Watch Hour 3 live on any device with MetroNews TV
📱 Join the conversation by texting 304-TALK-304 or call 800-765-TALK

 With featured segments like:
  • Mayor Monday – spotlighting towns and cities across the state
  • Wonderful West Virginian – honoring unsung heroes for their generosity and community spirit
MetroNews Midday is your live, local, and lively midday connection to West Virginia.

clipAbility.rationale: Individual sign pairings are briefly self-contained, but they are formulaic and interchangeable, offering little that would function as a compelling standalone hook.

clip.text: Il cancro è considerato uno dei segni zodiacali più romantici e sensibili. Chi ha cancro o ha una crash di questo segno tende a vivere le emozioni in modo profondo e cerca sempre amore e dolcezza nelle relazioni. Secondo le stelle, la compatibilità migliore in amore per il cancro si trova con lo scorpione, i pesci e il toro. Con lo scorpione nasce una connessione intensa e profonda. Vi sentite protetti e riuscite a tirar fuori il meglio l'uno dall'altro. nonostante qualche piccolo momento di gerosia. Con i pesci c'è una sintonia emotiva unica, fatta di coccole e sostegno reciproco, che vi permette di vivere una storia dolce e sognante.

Vi sentite protetti e riuscite a tirar fuori il meglio l'uno dall'altro. nonostante qualche piccolo momento di gerosia. Con i pesci c'è una sintonia emotiva unica, fatta di coccole e sostegno reciproco, che vi permette di vivere una storia dolce e sognante. Il toro, invece, garantisce stabilità e sicurezza. Insieme vi sentite davvero a casa e riuscite a costruire una relazione solida e duratura. Le combinazioni meno fortunate includono i gemelli, l'ariete e l'acquario. Con i gemelli è difficile trovarsi, perché voi cercate sicurezza mentre loro amano la libertà, portando spesso a incomprensioni. Con l'ariete c'è attrazione, ma le differenze nel modo di affrontare i problemi possono creare tensioni e richiedere molta pazienza. Con l'acquario vedete l'amore in modo troppo diverso. Voi volete vicinanza, loro indipendenza, e rischiate di sentirvi trascurati. Ricordate però che questi sono solo suggerimenti legati alle caratteristiche dei segni zodiacali. Ognuno è unico.

scored.description:
Beyond the Horizon is a project that aims to dig a bit deeper than just the surface level that we are so used to with the legacy media while at the same time attempting to side step the gaslighting and rhetoric in search of the truth. From the day to day news that dominates the headlines to more complex geopolitical issues that effect all of our lives, we will be exploring them all.

It's time to stop settling for what is force fed to us and it's time to look beyond the horizon.

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers two timely, specific local and regional news events — a deadly marina fire in Cabo San Lucas and earthquake relief efforts in Venezuela — giving it clear current-events grounding, though the scope is hyperlocal rather than broadly political.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves between distinct interview segments with identifiable guests and clear topic transitions, but pacing is uneven and commercial breaks interrupt the flow without strong signposting between segments.

clipAbility.rationale: The fire chief's account of the marina blaze and the Venezuela corruption commentary both contain self-contained, quotable moments, though most require at least minimal setup to land fully.

card.tagline: A yacht fire, a sunken fireboat, and a death at Cabo's marina
card.description: Cabo San Lucas's fire chief details a four-hour marina blaze that killed one person and nearly sank Mexico's first purpose-built marine firefighting vessel, while guests discuss Venezuela's earthquake response and government obstruction of aid.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down two urgent disasters unfolding right now

scored.description: Each week comedian Ross Patterson shares his wildly unique views on pop culture, entertainment, and sports as he's joined by the world's worst producer Jessie Wiseman. She's easy on the eyes, but terrible on Google.

card.tagline: Kanye's sample lawsuits, Madonna's comeback album, and Ross loses his mind over Ray of Light
card.description: Ross and crew break down how Kanye games sample clearance, debate whether Madonna's July 3rd album can actually be her best ever, and spiral into Sean Penn territory.
card.reason: Because this episode digs into the business and drama behind pop music's biggest names

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a radio call-in trivia game show covering pop culture, TV history, and music — no substantive connection to news or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a loose call-in format with recurring segment branding ('Reverse Trivia') but pacing is erratic, calls drop, and transitions are improvised rather than deliberate.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require context of the game format and prior caller setup; a few punchy one-liners exist but nothing that stands alone as a compelling standalone preview.

card.description: Chris Baker's reverse trivia game returns to KFAB with callers quizzing the host on Monkees history, Bonanza character names, and obscure pop culture facts.
card.reason: Because you enjoy classic talk radio banter and pop culture trivia

scored.feedTitle: Good News York by Growth Mode Content
scored.description:
Your go-to podcast for inspiring stories, uplifting news, and positive vibes from across New York State!

Whether it's groundbreaking innovations, community heroes, or businesses making a difference, we bring you the stories that remind us all of the good happening around us.

Join us as we highlight the changemakers, dreamers, and everyday folks bringing positivity to the Empire State.

From local businesses thriving in Syracuse to heartwarming tales from Buffalo, Albany, and beyond—this is the news you’ll actually want to hear. Because good news deserves to be heard.

🔊 Listen in, get inspired, and spread the good! Good News York is a project of Growth Mode Content in Syracuse, NY.

Visit: GoodNewsYork.com for full video episodes, clips, links and more!

Want to create podcasts and content for your brand? That's what Growth Mode Content is all about! Visit: GrowthModeContent.com to learn more!

Growth Mode Content is a division of Growth Mode Technologies LLC.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: GNY EP.201 | feat. Andrea Dotto with The Dusty & Dott Company

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is a local arts and entertainment interview about a puppetry company and their upcoming children's show — no connection to news or political content.

contentStructure.rationale: The interview follows a clear conversational arc from past work (Frozen at Syracuse Stage) to the upcoming Hurt Monster premiere, with identifiable topics and a natural host-guided throughline.

clipAbility.rationale: The Hurt Monster story synopsis and the 'stitching together a creative living' moment are quotable, but most exchanges require the surrounding interview context to land.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: GNY EP.198 | feat. Gracie Thorpe with The Crouse Health Foundation

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a local community fundraising/healthcare charity promotional interview with no connection to news or political content.

contentStructure.rationale: Follows a clear interview format with identifiable Q&A segments, a logical progression from NICU overview to donation mechanics to upcoming events, though pacing is conversational and loose.

clipAbility.rationale: The '900 little fighters a year' stat and the record-breaking $320,000 fundraiser mention are usable standalone moments, but most exchanges require the surrounding interview context to land.

scored.description:
The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is a deep-dive investigative podcast that pulls back the curtain on one of the most protected criminal networks in modern history. This series is built from the ground up on the actual paper trail—unsealed court records, depositions, exhibits, emails, and filings that were never meant to be read by the public. No pundit panels. No spin. Just the documents themselves, examined line by line, name by name, connection by connection—paired with precise, document-driven analysis that explains what the record truly shows.

Each episode opens the vault on newly unsealed or long-buried Epstein files and walks listeners through what they actually reveal about power, money, influence, and the systems that failed survivors at every turn. Alongside the filings themselves, informed commentary breaks down the legal strategy, the institutional behavior, the contradictions, and the implications hiding between the lines. From judges’ orders and sealed exhibits to sworn testimony and back-channel communications, the show connects the dots the media often won’t—or can’t. Patterns emerge. Timelines collapse. Excuses fall apart.

The Vault is a working archive in audio form, a living record of the Epstein case as told by the courts themselves—supplemented by rigorous analysis that provides context, challenges official narratives, and exposes where the record has been distorted, sanitized, or deliberately ignored. Every claim is grounded in filings. Every episode is anchored to the record. Listeners aren’t told what to think—they are shown what exists, what was said under oath, and what the commentary reveals about how those facts were buried, softened, or misrepresented.

If you want to understand how Jeffrey Epstein was protected, who circled him, how institutions closed ranks, and why accountability keeps slipping through the cracks, The Vault: The Epstein Files Unsealed is where the record finally speaks for itself—and where the commentary ensures the documents do what no press release ever will.

contentStructure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline following the DOJ memo's findings, but the host's editorial asides and personal backstory interrupt the flow, making pacing uneven across segments.

clipAbility.rationale: Several standalone, quotable moments — particularly the 'set up your fucking camp' passage and the victim compensation fund breakdown — work as self-contained previews without requiring surrounding context.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a loose but identifiable structure — a brief trial update, a media criticism segment, and a pivot to reading a Guardian article — but pacing is uneven and transitions are conversational rather than deliberate.

clipAbility.rationale: The passage about legacy media running Bob Dole coverage instead of Maxwell trial analysis, and the warning that a reporter would be 'assigned to the obituaries department,' are genuinely self-contained and quotable moments.

scored.description:
Stay informed, no matter how busy you are. Reporter Noa Levi of Israel Today: Ongoing War Report brings you the most crucial updates from Israel every hour. Sourced directly from leading news channels and top-rated RSS feeds, our team distills extensive news coverage from 'Telegram Channel Israel Today', 'Haaretz', 'JPost.com', 'The Times of Israel', 'Ynet' and more, into concise 2-3 minute summaries. From breaking headlines to political shifts and cultural happenings, get a comprehensive snapshot of the nation's heartbeat without the fluff. Subscribe now and never miss a beat from the heart of Israel.

Sources:
  • Telegram Channel Israel Today
  • Telegram Channel Stand With Us Breaking News
  • Telegram Channel News Before Everyone
  • Front - Globes
  • Haaretz latest headlines
  • JPost.com - Homepage & Breaking
  • Maariv.co.il - News
  • The Times of Israel
  • Israel Hayom
  • ynet

DISCLAIMER DISCLAIMER DISCLAIMER
This podcast's content is generated using advanced artificial intelligence techniques and aims to summarize information from various sources. It's important to note that while the AI strives for accuracy, it might not always provide an exact representation of the news. There's potential for unintended inaccuracies or omissions. The content provides an overview of discussions over the last hour and serves as a digest for those seeking a concise update. For comprehensive and direct information, please refer to primary news sources.

scored.feedTitle: True Crime Podcast 2026 - Police Interrogations, 911 Calls and True Police Stories Podcast
scored.description:
True Crime Podcasts 2026 — 911 Calls, Police Interrogations & Crime Stories

This is your nonstop true crime feed — built for fans who binge for hours.

Hear authentic 911 calls, full-length police interrogations, unsolved mysteries, and shocking cases from across the country.

Episodes drop in shifts — mornings, afternoons, and nights — so there’s always fresh crime waiting, whether you’re commuting, on the job, or streaming through the night.

Perfect for shift workers, commuters, night owls, and binge listeners who want hours of true crime content — not once a week, but every single day.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/true-crime-podcast-2026-police-interrogations-911-calls-and-true-police-stories-podcast--5693470/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The Psychology of a Control Freak

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a true crime podcast featuring a police interrogation transcript with no connection to current news or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The interrogation follows a natural investigative progression with the detective methodically working through the suspect's account, giving it a discernible throughline, though the raw transcript format creates uneven pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: Several exchanges are genuinely self-contained — particularly the detective's 'you're not in fear for your life anymore, you're in fear of getting in trouble' breakdown and the suspect's admission that 'it wasn't the right decision' — and would work as standalone previews.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: When The Victim Is Actually The Murderer

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a true crime episode featuring police interrogation audio with no connection to current news or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a loose narrative thread through the interrogation transcript but lacks clear signposting, with the excerpt jumping between suspect monologue and brief detective interjections without structured segmentation.

clipAbility.rationale: A few visceral moments — 'all I hear is pop' or the $20,000 ultimatum scene — could work as standalone hooks, but most of the excerpt requires significant prior context to follow the cast of characters and sequence of events.

latestEpisode.title: The Psychology of a Control Freak

card.tagline: He said she begged him — the detective wasn't buying it
card.description: A homicide detective methodically dismantles a suspect's self-defense claim, knife wound by knife wound, in a chilling recorded interrogation.
card.reason: Because this episode puts you inside a real murder interrogation room

scored.description:
Melissa Unfiltered - a podcast where nothing is off limits. As a Mom with three adopted kids, I dive into foster care system failures, realities of Mom life, homeschooling, sexual abuse cover ups, domestic violence and any issues that get me fired up each week.

My perspective is unfiltered, fearless, and usually different from the mainstream narrative. 

Join me as I question and expose our cultures “accepted” truths, shine a light on the uncomfortable, and explore topics through a lens that’s without boundaries and uniquely mine.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Charlie Kirk Preliminary Hearing: What Doesn't Add Up?

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers a specific, high-profile criminal case — the attempted assassination of Charlie Kirk and the preliminary hearing of accused shooter Tyler Robinson — which is a timely, politically charged news event with direct public interest.

contentStructure.rationale: The host works through hearing evidence point by point but relies heavily on verbal notes and stream-of-consciousness asides, with inconsistent pacing and frequent self-corrections that undermine a clear throughline.

clipAbility.rationale: A few punchy observations — like the missing shot video and the DNA analyst's inability to match bullet fragments — could stand alone, but most moments require surrounding context and the host's hedging language weakens standalone impact.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The Quiet War

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a lifestyle/self-help episode about social media habits, attention economy, and digital wellness — not a news or politics episode, despite brief incidental mentions of COVID opinions and political posting.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a loose but followable throughline — from social media oversharing to the attention economy to manipulation resistance — though it meanders conversationally without tight segmentation or clear signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: Several genuinely self-contained, quotable moments exist — particularly the 'the real war isn't left vs. right, it's a war for your attention' passage and the 'hardest person to manipulate' list — that could stand alone as preview clips.

latestEpisode.title: Charlie Kirk Preliminary Hearing: What Doesn't Add Up?

shown. 00:27:46.799 --> 00:27:51.599 And they could be waiting for the trial to show that because again, they don't want the jury to hear it. 00:27:51.839 --> 00:27:54.640 Um, so that could be something that's coming. 00:27:54.799 --> 00:27:55.359 I don't know. 00:27:55.519 --> 00:28:04.319 And then these weird Discord messages, and then there was a note, like a weird little handwritten note, but none of that proves. 00:28:04.480 --> 00:28:07.680 I mean, people admit to doing things that they didn't do. 00:28:08.480 --> 00:28:09.920 I've seen it before. 00:28:10.240 --> 00:28:14.160 So for some odd reason, if they're promised something, or I don't know. 00:28:14.240 --> 00:28:19.599 I'm not saying that happened, but um that's what what they had. 00:28:19.920 --> 00:28:22.640 All right, let me see what else do we have here. 00:28:23.119 --> 00:28:28.400 Um okay, then they had Lance Twiggs interview. 00:28:28.720 --> 00:28:39.839 Now, Lance Twiggs was given immunity almost immediately, which I find incredible because his DNA is on the same items that Tyler Robinson's DNA is on. 00:28:40.000 --> 00:28:48.000 So I don't know how they so quickly just decided from like day one that Lance Twiggs is innocent and he has immunity. 00:28:48.400 --> 00:28:55.440 I'm not the lead investigator, I'm not involved here, but that still doesn't sit right with me. 00:28:55.519 --> 00:29:04.400 And they've only had him in twice the first time he came in, right when this all happened, and then they had him come in and do an interview on camera. 00:29:04.559 --> 00:29:08.960 They're not gonna have him, they say, appear in court, which I also find really weird. 00:29:09.119 --> 00:29:11.359 Like he's claiming all these things. 00:29:11.519 --> 00:29:13.599 I think he should be interviewed in court. 00:29:13.839 --> 00:29:18.400 Seems pretty odd to me, and they were only playing some of the video for the court. 00:29:18.559 --> 00:29:28.799 Um, they were worried about different amendment rights and protecting Lance Twiggs and tainting the jury. 00:29:29.039 --> 00:29:37.519 But from what I heard, it was a lot of him just like agreeing to things, it wasn't a lot of him specifically talking. 00:29:37.759 -->

scored.feedTitle: All Caught Up: A Newsworthy podcast
scored.description:
Each week the All Caught Up team examine a newsworthy topic, talk to those in the know and consider what it means for young Australians and beyond. 


Hosts: Anoushka Israni @anoushkaisrani  and Ben Tresno-Turner @bentresnoturner 

Sound Editor and Engineer: Michelle Zeng  @michelle.zeng.creative

Executive producer: Alexandra Spring  @newsworthy_aus

episodeScores.episodeTitle: AI-assisted dating is here. Will it change Gen Z’s love lives?

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is a lifestyle/technology feature about AI in dating apps, with no substantive connection to news events, legislation, policy, or political developments.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear throughline from scam risks to app features to an expert interview to student vox pops, with identifiable segments and a loose but functional narrative arc.

clipAbility.rationale: The student vox pop from Eva and the clinical psychologist's explanation of AI-assisted matching both work as reasonably self-contained moments with minimal setup required.

latestEpisode.title: AI-assisted dating is here. Will it change Gen Z’s love lives?

--> 00:02:56.000 And if you're getting scammed, it makes it quite icky and quite difficult. 00:02:56.240 --> 00:03:00.000 How do you know that there is somebody real behind the screen, you know, that's really telling you the truth? 00:03:00.159 --> 00:03:02.560 Technology has made deception so much easier. 00:03:02.800 --> 00:03:03.120 That's true. 00:03:03.199 --> 00:03:13.280 And I think a really deceptive part about dating apps is how you're putting your whole, you know, personality, that's 21 years of life experience, on six photos and three like cringy little props. 00:03:13.360 --> 00:03:17.039 It's just like such a weird way to package yourself up and put yourself out there into the world. 00:03:17.280 --> 00:03:17.599 Exactly. 00:03:17.680 --> 00:03:25.599 And these six pictures you're choosing, they have to be the perfect angle, the best lighting, your favorite outfit, everything to perfectly curate who you are. 00:03:25.680 --> 00:03:27.280 It's almost like a personal brand. 00:03:27.360 --> 00:03:31.520 And then by that point, it's hard to know who you're actually swiping on and what they actually look like. 00:03:31.840 --> 00:03:32.000 Right. 00:03:32.080 --> 00:03:39.439 So it feels a bit like a scam in a way, like you're seeing this perfect portrayal of a person, but then in person themselves, it might be a little bit different. 00:03:39.599 --> 00:03:41.439 And the stats sort of back that up. 00:03:41.520 --> 00:03:48.000 So Cyber Daily revealed that 40% of Australians have reported being targeted by some form of dating scam. 00:03:48.319 --> 00:03:48.639 Exactly. 00:03:48.719 --> 00:03:51.199 And romance scams are becoming more and more of an issue. 00:03:51.360 --> 00:04:01.680 According to the Australian Competition and Consumer Commission, romance scams accounted for$28.6 million of financial losses between January and December of 2026. 00:04:01.840 --> 00:04:03.599 And that was reported to ScamWatch. 00:04:03.840 --> 00:04:07.840 AI has added a new level of speed and scale to these romance scams. 00:04:07.919 --> 00:04:14.719 So it allows the scammers to code automated messages and create scarily convincing online personas to trick people, you know? 00:04:14.800 --> 00:04:17.519 They can even do photos and videos and deepfakes now. 00:04:17.600 --> 00:04:18.720 It's all quite scary. 00:04:18.959 --> 00:04:21.279 Yeah, you might be talking to this one girl and you think she's really cool. 00:04:21.439 --> 00:04:23.120 Turns out she's an AI scam. 00:04:23.279 --> 00:04:27.199 But let's bring this back to why people are being icked out on dating apps. 00:04:27.360 --> 00:04:33.680 Well, I reckon it's because you're growing up, watching the notebook, you're hearing about how your parents met in such a romantic way. 00:04:33.839 --> 00:04:35.040 But where are you meeting people? 00:04:35.199 --> 00:04:41.360 Well, it's on this app that's riddled with scammers and catfishing and you know falsified, curated versions of people. 00:04:41.519 --> 00:04:45.360 So I guess to put it simply, it's just less romantic, right? 00:04:48.480 --> 00:04:52.720 AI is not only being utilized by these scam artists, but also by the other side of it all. 00:04:52.879 --> 00:04:57.920 So the dating apps themselves are integrating AI into the platforms to get better results. 00:04:58.160 --> 00:04:58.319 Right. 00:04:58.399 --> 00:05:05.360 Some of the most popular apps have it, and they're trying to help people meet in person through AI compatibility because that's where the sparks fly. 00:05:05.519 --> 00:05:13.519 So Hinge, for example, uses generative AI prompts, you know, to provide feedback on your profile and suggest some random sort of conversation starters. 00:05:13.839 --> 00:05:16.480 And on Tinder, they have an AI tool called Chemistry. 00:05:16.639 --> 00:05:18.480 So this is an AI-powered matching system. 00:05:18.560 --> 00:05:23.920 It's very similar to what Hinge does, and it provides a daily curated batch of highly compatible matches. 00:05:24.079 --> 00:05:26.879 It's kind of instead of your random swiping to give you a bit more direction. 00:05:27.199 --> 00:05:27.839 Yeah, that makes sense. 00:05:28.000 --> 00:05:34.959 Like on paper, they're showing the people that would work with you, who you should match with, so you're not just like randomly swiping at the most random people of all time, right? 00:05:35.199 --> 00:05:36.480 Yeah, and especially with Tinder. 00:05:36.560 --> 00:05:38.079 So what they do, it's really interesting. 00:05:38.160 --> 00:05:43.600 They learn your preferences either through an interactive QA, but also through an optional camera roll scan. 00:05:43.680 --> 00:05:50.000 So they'll scan your photos, decide what kind of person you are, and then match you with someone else who has a similar lifestyle. 00:05:50.240 --> 00:05:50.399 Okay. 00:05:50.480 --> 00:05:53.040 Do you think your camera roll is like indicative of your personality? 00:05:53.279 --> 00:05:53.759 You know what? 00:05:53.839 --> 00:05:59.519 I don't think it's it might be indicative of who I am, but I don't think it would define what I want in a future partner. 00:05:59.759 --> 00:06:00.160 I agree. 00:06:00.319 --> 00:06:09.839 And I guess the stats also support what you're saying because the match.com survey revealed that 47% of people view AI in romantic contexts negatively. 00:06:10.160 --> 00:06:15.120 And that makes sense to me because I know I view human emotion and dating in a very separate spot to AI. 00:06:15.360 --> 00:06:17.120 Yeah, other sides of the coin, in my opinion. 00:06:17.360 --> 00:06:18.639 But maybe that's not the case. 00:06:18.800 --> 00:06:23.040 I came across an app made by a clinical psychologist that utilizes AI. 00:06:23.439 --> 00:06:24.800 Hi, I'm Rachel Harker. 00:06:24.959 --> 00:06:28.319 I'm a clinical psychologist and founder of Tribal Group. 00:06:28.399 --> 00:06:30.160 So we have two apps. 00:06:30.319 --> 00:06:36.480 One is Tribal, our dating platform, and the other one is Connect by Tribal, which is our friendship and community platform. 00:06:36.800 --> 00:06:44.240 To give you a little bit more background context, Tribal, the app, it uses psychology journals to create an algorithm to then match people up. 00:06:44.639 --> 00:06:51.600 So I think AI is such a good tool to assist, but it definitely shouldn't be replacing humans. 00:06:52.000 --> 00:06:52.160 Okay. 00:06:52.399 --> 00:06:54.480 So almost like an assistant, if you will. 00:06:54.639 --> 00:07:01.279 So we have put in the psychology, we've put in the evidence, and we use AI to help us with that and refine it. 00:07:01.439 --> 00:07:04.160 So I mentioned before the compatibility quiz. 00:07:04.240 --> 00:07:07.279 So that has scientific journals backing it up. 00:07:07.360 --> 00:07:22.240 We have the scoring system that I developed, and we've input that into the algorithm, but then we use AI to refine that over time and almost provide kind of more informative recommendations based on the psychology that was input. 00:07:22.319 --> 00:07:23.759 So they work hand in hand. 00:07:24.319 --> 00:07:24.560 Right. 00:07:24.639 --> 00:07:31.040 And I downloaded the app just for scientific purposes here, and they gave me a compatibility score ranked on a bunch of different questions. 00:07:31.120 --> 00:07:37.759 So a 44 question quiz, some were on family value, some were how career-centric you are or how creative you are. 00:07:38.000 --> 00:07:42.399 But interestingly, in my opinion, you can't see who you match with for 72 hours. 00:07:42.480 --> 00:07:45.680 You can only see their sort of AI compatibility score. 00:07:45.920 --> 00:07:47.199 That's very interesting. 00:07:47.360 --> 00:07:55.519 I also think with the compatibility score, one thing that might be an issue, the algorithm doesn't really account for the whole opposite attract idea, does it? 00:07:55.600 --> 00:07:58.240 It just sort of pigeonholes you into one concept. 00:07:58.560 --> 00:08:00.800 That's something that we are refining over time. 00:08:01.279 --> 00:08:04.959 The more data that you put into an AI model, the more it will learn. 00:08:05.199 --> 00:08:08.480 So we have had almost 12,000 downloads since we launched. 00:08:08.560 --> 00:08:14.000 So we now have 12,000 data points of people's quizzes, and you can see how they then match. 00:08:14.079 --> 00:08:20.959 And that's where AI actually is an amazing tool because we might say, well, these are the answers to the quiz, and this is what we think is a good recommendation. 00:08:21.120 --> 00:08:33.279 But then as AI learns from those 12,000 people, they'll be like, actually, you know, it's okay to, it might be really good to have the same family values, but a compatibil compatible couple might have quite different career goals. 00:08:33.360 --> 00:08:40.159 One might be okay to maybe take, you know, a step back and support their partner going forward, depending on, you know, family dynamic. 00:08:40.399 --> 00:08:43.679 So you don't always have to have the exactly same values. 00:08:43.840 --> 00:08:45.279 They just need to be aligned. 00:08:45.600 --> 00:08:50.080 Okay, so they're trying to see AI as more of an assistant, not like the star of the show. 00:08:50.159 --> 00:08:51.759 It's not taking any of the credit. 00:08:51.919 --> 00:08:56.320 And we do also agree that there are some positive aspects of using AI in dating. 00:08:56.480 --> 00:08:58.480 For example, how about like religion matching? 00:08:58.639 --> 00:09:04.480 If you're maybe Christian and you only want to date other Christians, this is a good way to sort of filter out those people who just don't suit your type. 00:09:04.720 --> 00:09:08.399 Or even for age range, you've got AI age verification for safety. 00:09:08.559 --> 00:09:10.399 Date within your age range. 00:09:10.559 --> 00:09:11.840 No one too young, no one too old. 00:09:12.000 --> 00:09:12.240 I agree. 00:09:12.320 --> 00:09:16.799 And how about like another form of AI safety is like face scanning, so you can verify your profile. 00:09:16.879 --> 00:09:20.000 So maybe that's going to reduce the amount of catfishes available online. 00:09:20.320 --> 00:09:20.639 Definitely. 00:09:20.720 --> 00:09:23.200 And then you've also got stating your goals, you know? 00:09:23.360 --> 00:09:26.320 You know when somebody wants a long-term or just a short-term hookup. 00:09:26.720 --> 00:09:30.320 So maybe it's not that bad after all, integrating dating with AI. 00:09:33.759 --> 00:09:38.480 Look, I'm all for AI making things a bit more efficient and a little bit easier for you. 00:09:38.720 --> 00:09:42.879 But when AI and dating get mixed up, I'm really not sure how I feel about it. 00:09:43.120 --> 00:09:44.399 But that's just my opinion. 00:09:44.559 --> 00:09:51.679 So we asked around the UNSW campus to see how other students felt about AI's integration into dating apps and if it worked out for them. 00:09:52.080 --> 00:09:53.200 My name is Eva. 00:09:53.360 --> 00:09:55.360 I'm a student at UNSW. 00:09:55.759 --> 00:10:00.240 There are a couple people that I matched with based on the AI feature. 00:10:00.559 --> 00:10:07.279 And I'm gonna be so honest, I did not see any sort of compatibility between me and them. 00:10:07.440 --> 00:10:18.240 Like, for example, um on a very shallow level, I'm very into sports, but for some reason I was getting matched with people that are more musical and so on. 00:10:18.559 --> 00:10:25.840 And in fact, I thought I would give it a good crack and go on a date based on this AI compatibility level, and I did. 00:10:26.399 --> 00:10:37.200 And what I've learned is that there's just such a huge difference, and what I kind of gathered is that AI doesn't account for all the niche parts of your personality. 00:10:37.679 --> 00:11:07.919 For example, I I'm a bit more of a serious person, and I like someone that's a bit more like you know, bubbly and easygoing, but you can't really catch that on the apps, and AI definitely can't catch that because it does not understand the niche parts of personality, and yeah, so that actually ultimately made me stop going on hinge altogether because I did some research, and AI is really an underlying tone of hinge these days. 00:11:08.159 --> 00:11:08.879 Definitely, yeah. 00:11:09.120 --> 00:11:14.000 Um, and so yeah, now I don't

card.tagline: Swiping on AI: Is your dating app matchmaker actually a machine?
card.description: Two UNSW students investigate how AI is reshaping Gen Z dating — from romance scams to psychology-backed compatibility algorithms — with a clinical psychologist and real user experiences.
card.reason: Because this episode explores how AI is quietly changing how young people find love

scored.description: Plugged In Australia is your essential podcast for the latest electric vehicle news tailored to Aussie drivers. We break down fresh updates on sales trends, policy changes like road-user charges and tax exemptions, and infrastructure developments—from charging networks in Sydney to regional rollouts. Get quick insights on new models hitting the market, like affordable BYD imports and Tesla’s latest, plus analysis on how global shifts affect Oz. Whether you’re tracking EV adoption rates or debunking myths, tune in weekly for concise, no-fluff coverage to keep you informed on the road to a greener future. Subscribe now and plug into the conversation
