The broader implications of today's events 85 00:04:48.240 --> 00:04:51.920 suggest a complex landscape for Philippine leadership as the day closes. 86 00:04:52.920 --> 00:04:56.240 We are currently witnessing a unique convergence where domestic legal 87 00:04:56.279 --> 00:05:00.600 battles and external territorial disputes are occurring in almost perfect synchrony. 88 00:05:01.120 --> 00:05:04.560 The impeachment trial of Vice President Sarah du Turti acts 89 00:05:04.560 --> 00:05:08.000 as an internal pressure cooker, testing the stability and unity 90 00:05:08.000 --> 00:05:11.240 of the administration's corps members while forcing them to navigate 91 00:05:11.319 --> 00:05:16.959 intense public scrutiny simultaneously. The recent collision at Eingen Shoal 92 00:05:17.120 --> 00:05:20.920 highlights the persistent and escalating pressures from our maritime neighbours. 93 00:05:21.560 --> 00:05:25.040 This creates a significant challenge for the executive branch. They 94 00:05:25.120 --> 00:05:27.879 must find the focus to manage a high stakes domestic 95 00:05:27.920 --> 00:05:32.519 political crisis while maintaining an unwavering stance on national sovereignty 96 00:05:32.600 --> 00:05:37.480 amidst rising regional aggression. The proximity of the upcoming Ascian 97 00:05:37.519 --> 00:05:40.160 meetings in Manila adds a layer of urgency to our 98 00:05:40.199 --> 00:05:42.920 maritime defense as it forces the government to project a 99 00:05:43.000 --> 00:05:46.000 unified and firm front to the international community. As these 100 00:05:46.000 --> 00:05:48.439 two fronts unfold at once, one must ask does our 101 00:05:48.480 --> 00:05:51.920 leadership currently have sufficient bandwidth and political capital to effectively 102 00:05:51.959 --> 00:05:55.519 manage both a volatile domestic impeachment trial and an escalating 103 00:05:55.639 --> 00:06:00.600 territorial standoffs simultaneously. As we conclude today's probe, let us 104 00:06:00.600 --> 00:06:03.040 reflect on a word of wisdom from scripture. Let your 105 00:06:03.120 --> 00:06:04.879 light so shine before men, that they may see your 106 00:06:04.920 --> 00:06:07.160 good works and glorify your Father, which is in heaven, 107 00:06:07.199 --> 00:06:09.720 As found in Matthew five sixteen. In the face of 108 00:06:09.759 --> 00:06:12.079 complex national issues, let us find the strength to act 109 00:06:12.120 --> 00:06:14.680 with integrity and purpose in our daily roles. That is 110 00:06:14.720 --> 00:06:17.439 the evening briefing for today from Manila. I am Brian. 111 00:06:17.519 --> 00:06:19.439 Thank you for joining me tonight and have a peaceful 112 00:06:19.480 --> 00:06:23.120 and safe night ahead. Thank you for listening to Philippine 113 00:06:23.120 --> 00:06:27.120 Politics now. Please note that the commentary and opinions shared 114 00:06:27.120 --> 00:06:30.800 on this show belong exclusively to the individuals speaking and 115 00:06:30.879 --> 00:06:34.040 do not represent the standpoint of the entire network, its 116 00:06:34.040 --> 00:06:36.000 parent company, or its sponsors.

card.description: Brian's July 20 evening briefing covers Sarah Duterte's Senate trial, a Chinese Coast Guard confrontation at Ayungin Shoal, and two Filipino seafarers killed in Black Sea drone strikes.

scored.description:
Welcome to the Mix 92.3 R&B Playback Podcast! 🎙️

Detroit's favorite R&B station just got even better—on demand.

Listen in for the best moments from the Steve Harvey Morning Show and Keith Sweat’s Sweat Hotel, plus exclusive celebrity interviews hosted by Mix 92.3’s own Cheron and Bushman bringing you the inside on music, culture, and real talk.

From Keith’s wild confessions, to Shirley's juicy Strawberry Letter, and Nephew Tommy's legendary prank calls, we’ve got your mornings and nights covered. 🎧

Missed it live? Want to play it back? Hit follow and take the Mix 92.3 experience with you, wherever you go.

scored.description: Hear hourly updates on the biggest news stories impacting the Black community from the only 24/7 National News service dedicated to covering news from the Black Perspective. Hosted by the award winning anchor team at the Black Information Network and covering all aspects of the news stories that matter to the Black community, “BIN News This Hour” will provide credible news updates and information on an hourly basis.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: BIN News This Hour

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers multiple timely, specific political stories — Trump's election integrity address, Democratic reactions, reparations legislation in Alameda County, and the DEI policy fallout at UNC Wilmington — making it sharply relevant to current news/politics listeners.

contentStructure.rationale: Follows a clear broadcast news format with a headline tease, news brief segment, and individual story blocks, though the rapid-fire transitions and brief runtime limit deeper structural development.

clipAbility.rationale: The Georgia Democratic candidate's direct quote on Trump 'sowing seeds of doubt' and the Alameda reparations board quote both stand reasonably well on their own as 30-45 second excerpts without heavy surrounding context.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: BIN News This Hour

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers multiple timely, specific news stories — an ICE shooting of a wrongly identified man in Houston, a federal investigation into Gavin Newsom, a blocked Chicago tax referendum, and the suspicious death of a Black college athlete — all current political and civil rights developments.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear broadcast news format with a headlines segment, anchor handoffs, and distinct story blocks, though the rapid-fire pacing leaves little room for depth or deliberate signposting between topics.

clipAbility.rationale: The attorney's statement about Lorenzo Salgado Araujo's 35 years in the US and the father's account of the timeline inconsistencies in Nolan Wells's death are both emotionally charged, self-contained moments that would work as standalone previews.

latestEpisode.title: BIN News This Hour

scored.description:
Take some zany and serious journeys with The Pipeman aka Dean K. Piper, CST on The Adventures of Pipeman also known as Pipeman Radio syndicated globally “Where Who Knows And Anything Goes”.

Listen to & Watch a show dedicated to motivation, business, empowerment, inspiration, music, comedy, celebrities, shock jock radio, various topics, and entertainment. The Adventures of Pipeman is hosted by Dean K. Piper, CST aka “The Pipeman” who has been said to be hybrid of Tony Robbins, Batman, and Howard Stern. The Adventures of Pipeman has received many awards, media features, and has been ranked for multiple categories as one of the Top 6 Live Radio Shows & Podcasts in the world.

Pipeman Radio also consists of multiple podcasts showing the many sides of Pipeman. These include The Adventures of Pipeman, Pipeman in the Pit, and Positively Pipeman and more. You can find all of the Pipeman Podcasts anywhere you listen to podcasts. With thousands of episodes that focus on Intertainment which combines information and entertainment there is something for everyone including over 5000 interviews with celebrities, music artists/bands, authors, speakers, coaches, entrepreneurs, and all kinds of professionals.

Then there is The Pipeman Radio Tour where Pipeman travels the country and world doing press coverage for Major Business Events, Conferences, Conventions, Music Festivals, Concerts, Award Shows, and Red Carpets. One of the top publicists in music has named Pipeman the “King of All Festivals.” So join the Pipeman as he brings “The Pipeman Radio Tour” to life right before your ears and eyes.

The Adventures of Pipeman Podcasts are heard on The Adventures of Pipeman Site, Pipeman Radio, Talk 4 Media, Talk 4 Podcasting, iHeartRadio, Pandora, Amazon Music, Audible, Spotify, Apple Podcast, Google Podcasts and over 100 other podcast outlets where you listen to Podcasts. The following are the different podcasts to check out and subscribe to:

• The Adventures of Pipeman
• Pipeman Radio
• Pipeman in the Pit
• Positively Pipeman

Follow @pipemanradio on all social media outlets
Visit Pipeman Radio on the Web at linktr.ee/pipemanradio , theadventuresofpipeman.com, pipemanradio.com, talk4media.com, w4cy.com, talk4tv.com, talk4podcasting.com

Download The Pipeman Radio APP

Phone/Text Contact – 561-506-4031

Email Contact – dean@talk4media.com  

The Adventures of Pipeman is broadcast live Wednesdays at 1PM ET and Music & Positive Interviews daily at 8AM ET on W4CY Radio (www.w4cy.com) and replays on K4HD Radio (www.k4hd.com  – Hollywood Talk Radio part of Talk 4 Radio (www.talk4radio.com) on the Talk 4 Media Network (www.talk4media.com). The Adventures of Pipeman TV Show is viewed on Talk 4 TV (www.talk4tv.com).

The Adventures of Pipeman Podcast is also available on www.theadventuresofpipeman.com and www.pipemanradio.com,  Talk 4 Media (www.talk4media.com), Talk 4 Podcasting (www.talk4podcasting.com), iHeartRadio, Amazon Music, Pandora, Spotify, Audible, and over 100 other podcast outlets.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-adventures-of-pipeman--941822/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: US Bombs Iran After Troops Killed, Starmer Says Goodbye, More

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode leads with a major breaking geopolitical story — US military strikes on Iran following troop deaths — and covers UK leadership transition and a Maine Senate race, all timely and substantive political developments.

contentStructure.rationale: Classic news-brief format with clear segment handoffs between anchors and correspondents, though the rapid-fire transitions between Iran, UK politics, markets, and sports feel more like a rundown than a structured narrative.

clipAbility.rationale: Senator Warner's 'war of choice' quote and Starmer's farewell remarks are genuinely self-contained and quotable, though most segments are too brief and context-dependent to stand alone as previews.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: US and Iran Trade Fresh Strikes, Lindsey Graham’s Death, More

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode leads with active US-Iran military exchanges, Lindsey Graham's death and its geopolitical implications, McConnell's health, and Middle East-driven market moves — all sharply timely political and foreign policy developments.

contentStructure.rationale: Follows a clear news-brief format with distinct segments on Iran, Graham, McConnell, markets, and sports, though transitions are abrupt and pacing is broadcast-functional rather than deliberate.

clipAbility.rationale: The Bloomberg reporter's line about Graham leaving the interventionist faction 'without a very clear champion' and Trump's tribute audio are genuinely self-contained, quotable moments that work without much setup.

scored.description: Audio recordings of Mises Wire articles, offering contemporary news and opinion through the lens of Austrian economics and libertarian political economy.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The Evolution of Politics: From Civic Virtue to Realpolitik

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with current American political dynamics — referencing Citizens United, Thomas Massey's defeat, Trump, and deep state narratives — making it clearly on-topic for News/Politics, though its framing is more ideological essay than timely news analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a discernible throughline from historical political philosophy (Machiavelli, C. Wright Mills) to contemporary application, with a loose three-part taxonomy of the public, but transitions between sections feel abrupt rather than deliberately signposted.

clipAbility.rationale: The three-group taxonomy of the American public offers a potentially self-contained moment, but most passages assume the listener is tracking a longer argument and rely on setup from earlier in the episode to land with full force.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Praxeology within a Physics of the Social Sciences

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is a philosophical defense of Austrian economics methodology (praxeology vs. empiricism) with no connection to current news, legislation, or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The essay follows a clear argumentative throughline — analogy setup, critique of critics, physics comparison, resolution — with consistent pacing typical of a read academic article.

clipAbility.rationale: The diminishing marginal utility passage is the closest thing to a standalone moment, but most segments require the surrounding argument to land meaningfully.

Consequently, ordinary voters find themselves functionally alienated, 75 00:06:07.879 --> 00:06:12.360 possessing virtually no influence over final policy outcomes, and

transcript.scoringWindow: 00:02:02.239 and relentlessly expanding personal power, prestige, and financial influence. For some, 24 00:02:02.680 --> 00:02:05.879 it is easy to link the Prince with President Donald Trump. 25 00:02:06.760 --> 00:02:12.280 This cynical framework heavily mirrors the landscape of modern American politics, 26 00:02:12.840 --> 00:02:18.280 which is increasingly defined by public fragmentation, disillusionment with the 27 00:02:18.400 --> 00:02:22.719 nation and especially the mainstream media, and rule by the 28 00:02:22.759 --> 00:02:27.319 deep state and the entrenched global power elites. Today, the 29 00:02:27.400 --> 00:02:33.000 general populace is divided into distinct types. One those most 30 00:02:33.080 --> 00:02:37.080 confused are typically hypnotized by the claims of one cable 31 00:02:37.159 --> 00:02:41.039 news channel or the other, but increasingly confronted by a 32 00:02:41.080 --> 00:02:45.599 world not working or matching up with the broadcasts. By 33 00:02:45.639 --> 00:02:48.759 the way, the third group described below owns all the 34 00:02:48.840 --> 00:02:53.599 major news sources, has completely infested social and alternative media, 35 00:02:54.319 --> 00:02:57.400 and have rigged the whole information sector of the economy 36 00:02:57.879 --> 00:03:01.599 to promote their selfish interests and to purposefully divide the 37 00:03:01.639 --> 00:03:06.840 American people. Two. Those that are out of touch and 38 00:03:06.960 --> 00:03:12.000 disgusted by the government. These are the busy, productive, working 39 00:03:12.039 --> 00:03:16.000 people raising families, who can afford to waste too much 40 00:03:16.080 --> 00:03:20.599 time and energy watching news or participating in the political process. 41 00:03:21.400 --> 00:03:24.639 A good society surely would put this group in an 42 00:03:24.680 --> 00:03:28.719 elevated policy status, take from them as little as possible, 43 00:03:29.120 --> 00:03:31.479 and make it possible for them to do their work 44 00:03:31.599 --> 00:03:35.879 and enjoy their lives without the burden of state and 45 00:03:35.960 --> 00:03:40.919 the insult of modern politics. These are the people who 46 00:03:40.919 --> 00:03:44.479 are pulling the wagon of society and paying the most 47 00:03:44.800 --> 00:03:49.639 in taxes out of their true productivity. This group dominates 48 00:03:49.719 --> 00:03:53.680 the two thirds of those Americans polled that now oppose 49 00:03:53.759 --> 00:03:58.400 the President the Congress and believe government is working against 50 00:03:58.479 --> 00:04:02.520 their interests. More than half of the remaining one third 51 00:04:02.759 --> 00:04:07.560 also do not like the government. Three. The next group 52 00:04:07.599 --> 00:04:13.159 controls politicians and political parties and controls of vast propaganda network. 53 00:04:13.680 --> 00:04:16.600 The primary purpose of the AI data centers is to 54 00:04:16.639 --> 00:04:20.920 expand this groups, including the deep state in government, to 55 00:04:21.040 --> 00:04:26.160 spy on propagandas and control the population. At the highest level. 56 00:04:26.279 --> 00:04:30.759 It represents the global power elites The top levels are 57 00:04:30.800 --> 00:04:34.040 the people who show up at Davos and the World 58 00:04:34.120 --> 00:04:38.000 Economic Forum, but there are plenty of unknown and seldom 59 00:04:38.040 --> 00:04:42.319 seen members of this group. The controlling individuals and families 60 00:04:42.360 --> 00:04:48.680 come from the large multinational corporations, international banking and investment firms, 61 00:04:49.240 --> 00:04:54.480 political dynasties, a mass of propagandist opinion makers, as well 62 00:04:54.519 --> 00:04:59.279 as leaders in higher education and research, and of course, 63 00:04:59.600 --> 00:05:05.199 the milit taries of the advanced economic nations. The subversion 64 00:05:05.360 --> 00:05:10.560 of sovereignty, real politic, and the machinery of modern governance. 65 00:05:12.680 --> 00:05:17.160 The conceptual baseline of politics is fundamentally shifted from ancient 66 00:05:17.319 --> 00:05:21.959 ideals of communal justice that emerged with the successful genesis 67 00:05:21.959 --> 00:05:27.240 of society into a clinical, transactional exercise in power maximization. 68 00:05:28.360 --> 00:05:31.600 Governance is not a pursuit of justice, but a system 69 00:05:31.680 --> 00:05:37.160 of calculated ruthlessness, strategic trade offs, and the continuous expansion 70 00:05:37.360 --> 00:05:43.639 of institutional power and individual prestige. Sociologists see Wright Mills 71 00:05:43.800 --> 00:05:48.319 famously identified this phenomenon in his nineteen fifty six work 72 00:05:48.800 --> 00:05:53.360 The Power Elite, arguing that the critical decisions of modern 73 00:05:53.399 --> 00:05:59.720 states are concentrated within an interlocking directorate of political, military, 74 00:06:00.199 --> 00:06:07.279 and corporate leaders. Consequently, ordinary voters find themselves functionally alienated, 75 00:06:07.879 --> 00:06:12.360 possessing virtually no influence over final policy outcomes, and at 76 00:06:12.399 --> 00:06:16.639 odds with their fellow citizens. The political party duopoly has 77 00:06:16.759 --> 00:06:21.000 very few items in the budget in dispute. Both parties 78 00:06:21.079 --> 00:06:26.399 also want to outlaw minor party competitors, independent candidates, and 79 00:06:26.480 --> 00:06:30.839 any reforms that might make the system more effectively. Competitive 80 00:06:31.720 --> 00:06:36.079 political campaigns are dominated by funding from special interest groups 81 00:06:36.439 --> 00:06:40.160 and the political parties themselves. They have not made the 82 00:06:40.199 --> 00:06:43.639 traditional increases in the size of the House of Representatives 83 00:06:44.040 --> 00:06:48.720 since the progressive socialist takeover of government during the nineteen thirties. 84 00:06:49.439 --> 00:06:56.839 This negatively impacts political competition and congressional representation of voters' views. 85 00:06:57.600 --> 00:07:01.360 It also turns the old traditional care campaigns in tiny 86 00:07:01.439 --> 00:07:07.879 districts into vast financial orgies dominated by consultant driven tv ads. 87 00:07:08.480 --> 00:07:12.680 There are other aspects to this consolidation of power. Elites 88 00:07:13.000 --> 00:07:19.040 systematically bypassed the public will by buying elections and politicians 89 00:07:19.079 --> 00:07:25.199 through immense campaign contributions, dark money networks, and legalized bribery. 90 00:07:26.160 --> 00:07:30.120 This dynamic was significantly amplified by the US Supreme Court's 91 00:07:30.560 --> 00:07:36.800 twenty ten Citizens United Versus FEC decision which allowed unlimited 92 00:07:37.000 --> 00:07:43.240 independent political expenditures by corporations and unions. This devil's brew 93 00:07:43.319 --> 00:07:47.680 of politics recently raised its ugly head in the recent 94 00:07:47.720 --> 00:07:53.439 defeat of Congressman Thomas Massey. When direct financial capture is 95 00:07:53.600 --> 00:07:58.560 insufficient for buying elections or stealing elections at the ballot box, 96 00:07:59.120 --> 00:08:03.120 elements within the power structure pivot to more aggressive measures, 97 00:08:03.160 --> 00:08:09.680 such as asserting their dominance through systemic intimidation, threats, and blackmail. 98 00:08:10.480 --> 00:08:13.879 I've even heard people suggest that the President is being 99 00:08:13.959 --> 00:08:18.240 threatened or being blackmailed, in addition to the several attempts 100 00:08:18.279 --> 00:08:23.439 made on his life historically and structurally. When politicians resist 101 00:08:23.560 --> 00:08:29.120 elite agendas, the mechanisms of control escalate to devastating extremes, 102 00:08:29.720 --> 00:08:35.240 including character assassination, threats to family safety, and physical elimination. 103 00:08:36.159 --> 00:08:40.559 Physical elimination serves the dual purpose of removing an obstacle 104 00:08:41.000 --> 00:08:46.399 for the deep state and intimidating others into compliance. Not surprisingly, 105 00:08:47.159 --> 00:08:51.919 this new system has triggered a violent feedback loop. As 106 00:08:51.960 --> 00:08:56.399 citizens recognize that elected officials are acting on behalf of 107 00:08:56.480 --> 00:09:01.039 global elites and deep state networks rather than the public interest, 108 00:09:01.720 --> 00:09:05.200 they have begun fighting back. This has resulted in a 109 00:09:05.399 --> 00:09:10.240 historic surge of threats and incidents directed from below against 110 00:09:10.279 --> 00:09:14.320 public officials above at all levels of government. The power 111 00:09:14.360 --> 00:09:18.360 elites are the cause and are responsible for these desperate 112 00:09:18.639 --> 00:09:24.039 citizen responses. The modern political arena has thus deteriorated into 113 00:09:24.080 --> 00:09:28.759 a volatile battleground where elites utilize top down coercion to 114 00:09:28.879 --> 00:09:34.559 maintain control, while a disillusion desperate populace responds with bottom 115 00:09:34.639 --> 00:09:39.039 up hostility, threatening the fabric of society to maintain and 116 00:09:39.120 --> 00:09:43.720 expand their ill gotten gains. For more content like this, 117 00:09:43.960 --> 00:09:45.639 visit meases dot org.

card.tagline: Machiavelli meets modern America: who really runs the country?
card.description: A Mises Institute essay argues that U.S. politics has fully shifted from civic virtue to elite power maximization, tracing the arc from The Prince to Citizens United and Thomas Massey's primary loss.
card.reason: Because this episode argues the American political system is structurally captured by global elites

scored.description: Kenny is a professional comedian, talk radio host, and proud member of the New Right. He's also a millennial (but don't hold it against him).

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Jimothy the Racoon saves America

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode touches on real political topics (voter ID, ICE enforcement, AOC) but frames them through extended comedy bits about Olive Garden pasta passes and pop culture riffs, making the political content incidental to the entertainment format rather than substantively news-driven.

contentStructure.rationale: The host jumps between voter ID, Olive Garden, Jersey Shore, Roman Empire on Netflix, looksmaxing, AOC, Cori Bush, and ICE with minimal transitions or throughline, creating a loose, stream-of-consciousness format that's hard to navigate.

clipAbility.rationale: The Olive Garden pasta pass voter ID riff is genuinely self-contained and punchy enough to stand alone as a preview clip, and the 'President AOC, First Gentleman Clovicular' bit lands without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: How do we stop a cult in West Texas?

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with criminal justice disparities, prosecutorial discretion, and libertarian-vs-government philosophy through a sitting DA, giving it a clear political/policy throughline despite the conversational format.

contentStructure.rationale: The excerpt meanders across prison sentencing, sexual assault prosecution, education reform, and AI without clear segment breaks, though a loose interview throughline keeps it from being entirely formless.

clipAbility.rationale: Several genuinely standalone moments — 'I wake up a libertarian, I go to bed an authoritarian' and 'now that I'm part of the government, it's even more terrifying' — land as self-contained, quotable hooks without requiring setup.

latestEpisode.title: Jimothy the Racoon saves America

card.tagline: Olive Garden's pasta pass has stricter ID rules than California elections
card.description: Kenny Webster uses Olive Garden's Never Ending Pasta Pass to skewer voter ID debates, ICE body cameras, and a possible AOC presidential future.
card.reason: Because this episode riffs on voter fraud and ICE enforcement debates

scored.feedTitle: Good News for Lefties | Daily News for Democracy
scored.description:
Positive, progressive news for democracy daily! Democrats, Leftists, liberals, and democratic socialists welcome. A respite from troubling headlines in difficult times. Sure, read the bad news, but then listen to us! Send good news tips to beowulf@twosquaredmediaproductions.com or 202-656-6271

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/good-news-for-lefties-daily-news-for-democracy--6256627/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: California Invests in Local News and Minneapolis City Council Rejects Drone Deal

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers specific, timely political developments — California's local news funding, Minneapolis drone surveillance rejection, DC anti-scalping legislation, Virginia reproductive rights, and Idaho LGBTQ legislation — but the framing as progressive 'good news' briefs keeps it from the sharpest tier of news analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear headline-by-headline format with a consistent intro and outro, though transitions between stories are minimal and a mid-roll ad interrupts the flow without strong segmentation.

clipAbility.rationale: The Minneapolis drone story and the Massey quote on the Epstein Files Act offer reasonably self-contained moments, but most stories are delivered as brief summaries that lack the punchy standalone framing needed for strong preview clips.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Trump’s Crypto Troubles, and Geothermal Growth

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers specific current political and policy developments — Trump crypto controversies, nurses strikes, LGBTQ civil verdicts, geothermal energy policy, and reproductive healthcare legislation — giving it clear news/politics relevance with timely hooks.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear news-brief format with distinct story segments and a listener-suggested story section, though transitions are abrupt and pacing is uneven across topics.

clipAbility.rationale: Several segments — particularly the Massachusetts nurses strike and the Virginia defamation verdict — contain self-contained, quotable summaries that could stand alone as previews without much setup.

scored.description:
Jeffrey Epstein was a multi millionaire who had political and business ties to some of the most rich and powerful people in the world. From businessmen to politicians at the highest levels, Epstein broke bread with them all.

Yet for years the Legacy media and the rest of high society looked the other way and ignored his behavior as multiple women came forward with allegations of abuse.

Even after he was convicted and subsequently received a sweetheart deal those same so called elites welcomed him back with open arms.

Now after his death and the arrest of Maxwell, the real story is starting to come together and the curtain has begun to be drawn back and what it has revealed is truly disturbing.

From Princes to Ex Presidents, the cast of scoundrels in this play spans continents and political affiliations leaving us with a transcontinental criminal conspiracy possibly unlike any we have ever seen before.

In this podcast we will explore all of the levels of Jeffrey Epstein and his criminal enterprise.

From his most trusted assistants to obscure associates, we will leave no stone unturned as we swim through the muck searching for clarity and answers to some of the most pressing questions of the case.

From interviews with people directly involved in the case to daily updates, the Epstein Chronicles will have it all.

Just like our other project, The Jeffrey Epstein Show, you can expect no punches pulled and consistent content. We have covered the Epstein case daily(everyday since October 1st 2019) and will continue to do so until there are convictions. With a library of well over 1k shows, you can expect a ton of content coming your way including on scene reporting from the Maxwell trial and from places like Zorro Ranch.

Thank you for tuning in and I look forward to having you all along for the ride.




(Created and Hosted by Bobby Capucci)

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-epstein-chronicles--5003294/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Anya’s Account of Life Inside Jeffrey Epstein’s Cult (Part 1) (7/19/26)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers the Epstein sex trafficking case and references a Congressional hearing, but the content is primarily true-crime narrative and personal testimony rather than timely news or political analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode alternates between reading BBC source material and extended host editorializing without clear segment breaks, creating an uneven, loosely organized flow.

clipAbility.rationale: The host's blunt takes on Sarah Kellen Vickers and the 'blueprint' accountability argument are punchy and quotable, but most moments require surrounding context to land fully.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Mega Edition:   The CVRA Was Meant To Protect Survivors.   It Did The Opposite.  (7/13/26)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is substantively focused on the CVRA, prosecutorial misconduct, and the Epstein plea deal — a specific legal and political accountability story — though it leans heavily on a 2016 article rather than breaking 2026 developments.

contentStructure.rationale: The host reads from a sourced article and interjects commentary throughout, but the structure is loose and reactive rather than deliberate, with no clear segmentation or signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: Several punchy, quotable moments exist — particularly around the CVRA violation argument and the 'sweetheart deal' framing — but most require surrounding context to land with full force.

with their hosting plan. 00:00:41.359 --> 00:00:45.439 So implementing a website may be much easier than you initially thought. 00:00:45.600 --> 00:00:53.119 I'd like to share three reasons how a podcasting website levels up your show, and then we can determine if it's truly a need. 00:00:53.280 --> 00:00:56.719 I want you to think of your website as your podcast home on the internet. 00:00:57.039 --> 00:01:16.079 Instead of sending listeners to multiple locations where they can hear your episodes like Spotify and Apple Podcasts or maybe even social media platforms, you can send them one link to your website, and that actually has everything available in one convenient spot and then let your listeners choose what listening platform works best for them. 00:01:16.319 --> 00:01:21.760 Since your website is the home base for your podcast, it's important to know what to include in that home base. 00:01:22.079 --> 00:01:26.640 So I want to break down some of the components of a really great podcast website. 00:01:26.799 --> 00:01:42.640 Typically, a podcasting website will include all of your episodes, your episode details like show notes, chapters, artwork, and more, your episode transcripts, if supported, and links to follow or subscribe through your favorite listening directories. 00:01:42.799 --> 00:01:46.879 Your website becomes the central hub where your audience can explore your content. 00:01:47.040 --> 00:01:56.079 And if you choose to build your own website or work with a host that gives you customization controls, then the best part is you get control over that experience. 00:01:56.239 --> 00:02:03.280 When you have control over those customization options, you want to focus on making your podcasting website really specific to you. 00:02:03.439 --> 00:02:07.760 Show off your personality and make sure that it fits your overall vibe of your podcast. 00:02:07.920 --> 00:02:13.680 Of course, you'll want to include the ability to not only display your episodes, but also play them on your website. 00:02:13.840 --> 00:02:20.639 Buzzprout websites include our built-in player, which can actually be customized to match the look and feel of your podcast. 00:02:20.800 --> 00:02:24.159 You'll want to provide a way of connecting with you as the podcaster. 00:02:24.319 --> 00:02:34.400 A website can give listeners an easy way to engage with you either by using email, comments, or message board, or even a direct message communication option like fan mail. 00:02:34.560 --> 00:02:40.240 Your podcasting website can highlight your hosts, your guest, and all of the contributors assigned to your podcast. 00:02:40.319 --> 00:02:45.039 But you'll even have the option of assigning each guest to the episodes that they're on. 00:02:45.199 --> 00:02:49.360 And then you'll see that list of episodes when you visit their page on your website. 00:02:49.520 --> 00:02:56.960 Your website should also include a follow page, of course, where listeners can subscribe easily to your podcast using their favorite listening directory. 00:02:57.120 --> 00:03:05.360 And last but certainly not least, we recommend including your social media links so that your listeners can easily join your community and the discussion around your podcast. 00:03:05.520 --> 00:03:10.800 A fun feature that we offer here at Buzzsproute for your podcasting website is actually PodRole. 00:03:10.960 --> 00:03:16.560 So PodRoll allows you to link to some of your favorite podcasts right on your podcasting website. 00:03:16.719 --> 00:03:24.719 And since podcasting is so easily spread through word of mouth, this is kind of like a virtual word of mouth recommendation for your favorite podcast. 00:03:24.960 --> 00:03:39.120 Since the goal here is to make this website personal to your podcast and avoid anything generic, you'll also want to make sure you are customizing things like the color scheme, the background image, the fonts you are using, and even the URL you are using for your website. 00:03:39.280 --> 00:03:46.080 If you have purchased your own domain name, we recommend go ahead and change the URL for your site to match that domain. 00:03:46.240 --> 00:03:50.080 This is building identity and branding around your podcast. 00:03:50.319 --> 00:03:58.400 I feel like this question about having a podcasting website naturally leads to the follow-up question: is the podcast host provided website enough? 00:03:58.560 --> 00:04:00.400 Or should I be building my own? 00:04:00.639 --> 00:04:08.879 Shoot us some fan mail if you'd like for me to dive a little deeper into this question and explore some of the pros and cons for building your own custom website. 00:04:09.039 --> 00:04:16.399 Whether you're using a website provided by your podcast host or creating something custom, a website helps to build your brand. 00:04:16.560 --> 00:04:21.199 A website isn't just about listening, it's about growing something bigger than your podcast. 00:04:21.360 --> 00:04:28.079 When you have a dedicated space on the internet, it gives you the opportunity to build brand identity, it becomes searchable. 00:04:28.240 --> 00:04:34.000 And as you grow, this will help with discoverability and even lead to some sponsorship opportunities. 00:04:34.240 --> 00:04:41.920 You can promote products or services that you offer through your website, and you can even turn listeners into subscribers and super fans. 00:04:42.079 --> 00:04:47.199 Your podcast website is essentially working as a marketing engine for everything else you're building. 00:04:47.439 --> 00:04:56.480 Even if you're not monetizing yet, having the foundation of a website early on makes a huge difference later down the road, depending on your goals and how they evolve. 00:04:56.639 --> 00:05:04.720 If you have questions about your podcasting website, you can reach out to us on the podcaster success team by emailing support at buzzsprout.com. 00:05:04.879 --> 00:05:12.319 If there's a question you'd like to answer on a future podcasting QA episode, send it to me by clicking the send us a text link in the show notes. 00:05:12.480 --> 00:05:16.959 Remember, your question could help hundreds of other podcasters just like you. 00:05:17.040 --> 00:05:18.319 So don't keep it to yourself. 00:05:18.480 --> 00:05:19.279 Just send it in. 00:05:19.439 --> 00:05:22.079 Be sure to join us every Monday to kickstart your week. 00:05:22.240 --> 00:05:23.519 Thank you so much for listening. 00:05:23.600 --> 00:05:26.000 And as always, keep podcasting.

card.tagline: Do you actually need a podcast website? Here's the honest answer.
card.description: Buzzsprout's Cara Passetti breaks down three reasons a podcast website builds brand identity, discoverability, and long-term monetization potential.
card.reason: Because this episode offers practical setup advice for independent podcast creators

doctor's note for, say, lower back pain that can't be proven or disproven, or if you can have something like this happen where it's, you know, these are extenuating circumstances, our fighter wasn't able to secure a visa. 00:11:10.960 --> 00:11:15.360 Oops, because we forgot to submit the paperwork properly or on time. 00:11:15.600 --> 00:11:20.960 Well, then you got a free ticket out of that legally binding contract that you signed. 00:11:21.279 --> 00:11:27.120 So that's what I think they just

00:15:58.480 --> 00:16:01.759 Is there if any, would you change any of the rules? 00:16:02.480 --> 00:16:09.360 Well, the only thing that I would do, given that I do three different rules codes, I would put the rules codes all the same. 00:16:09.679 --> 00:16:14.720 So we have different pitching rules for USA versus high school versus college. 00:16:15.440 --> 00:16:20.320 So as a kid grows up, we're teaching them one way, and then all of a sudden they need to change. 00:16:20.480 --> 00:16:24.480 And that's why we see the illegal pitch pitching situations that we have. 00:16:24.639 --> 00:16:26.559 Because you know in high school you're allowed to step back. 00:16:26.720 --> 00:16:28.799 We sure can't do that in college, right? 00:16:29.120 --> 00:16:34.240 So I I just and there's other rules too that I wish they would all just be this be the same. 00:16:34.480 --> 00:16:45.039 Yeah, because I've seen that that's actually a really good point because I've seen that, especially with the pitching, like with different positions and different, and someone will call something, and I'm like, where I I've never heard that before. 00:16:45.200 --> 00:16:46.639 Like, what is that rule? 00:16:46.879 --> 00:16:59.360 And the problem is, you know, with something like that, and if a pitcher's not trained, you can see they're they're like kind of rattled, especially at the young ages, like and you could see them getting all off, like with their mechanics, uh, because then they're trying not to get in trouble. 00:16:59.600 --> 00:17:01.360 And you can't change something like that in a game. 00:17:01.600 --> 00:17:05.200 So you basically you want to like basically a universal rule book across the board. 00:17:05.440 --> 00:17:05.599 Yes. 00:17:05.759 --> 00:17:12.960 I I would like it if all of our rules, because it's difficult to train umpires because we all do multiple levels because there just aren't enough of us. 00:17:13.119 --> 00:17:13.440 Right. 00:17:14.000 --> 00:17:19.039 And so one one day I could do a USA softball game, the next day do a high school game, and the next day do a college game. 00:17:19.119 --> 00:17:20.880 I got three different sets of rules codes. 00:17:21.039 --> 00:17:21.440 Yeah. 00:17:21.680 --> 00:17:23.519 So I mean, I'm not that bright. 00:17:23.839 --> 00:17:24.400 You're gonna confuse them. 00:17:25.039 --> 00:17:25.599 I'm not that bright. 00:17:26.079 --> 00:17:31.759 So uh I that's what I would I mean, there are some rules that I usually say. 00:17:31.839 --> 00:17:36.960 You know what, I don't make the rules, I just enforce them, but none are coming to me right now. 00:17:37.359 --> 00:17:41.359 So that uh that leads me to a specific rule. 00:17:42.240 --> 00:17:46.319 What is the book definition of an infield fly? 00:17:46.559 --> 00:17:47.920 Ah, yes, that's a good one. 00:17:48.160 --> 00:17:48.880 An infield fly. 00:17:49.440 --> 00:17:54.799 You probably don't remember, but when I was back in Rec, I had an issue with you on an infield fly call. 00:17:55.119 --> 00:17:55.920 Well, you've been holding on the call. 00:17:56.079 --> 00:17:56.799 I do not remember. 00:17:59.599 --> 00:18:00.880 You've never had an issue with this. 00:18:01.519 --> 00:18:02.799 It was not like an argument or nothing. 00:18:02.880 --> 00:18:07.839 I just didn't agree with you, did you call Did you set this recording up tonight just to bring that to bring this question up? 00:18:08.160 --> 00:18:10.240 I think it just popped in my head when we were talking about rules. 00:18:10.400 --> 00:18:16.400 Well, but see, the thing about Chris is I know he looked at the rules book, so I can't just BS him because he looked. 00:18:16.559 --> 00:18:16.640 Yeah. 00:18:16.799 --> 00:18:18.799 And he'll say, I can't find a rule for that. 00:18:19.039 --> 00:18:20.880 Why are you looking at the rule book? 00:18:21.119 --> 00:18:23.039 Um, the infield fly rule. 00:18:23.200 --> 00:18:25.920 Okay, that this that's a great question. 00:18:26.559 --> 00:18:31.359 It's in play when there's either runners on first and second or basis loaded less than two outs, right? 00:18:31.519 --> 00:18:31.839 Yep. 00:18:32.079 --> 00:18:43.759 We call infield fly when there is a pop in the infield, pop fly in the infield within the confines of the infield that can be caught with relative ease. 00:18:44.079 --> 00:18:45.839 And what's it normal? 00:18:46.000 --> 00:18:49.119 Well, there we go with that subjectivity again. 00:18:49.519 --> 00:18:58.160 Um I base that on the level that I'm at, right? 00:18:58.240 --> 00:18:58.400 Yeah. 00:18:58.559 --> 00:19:02.240 So a college kid's gonna catch a lot more than a 10-year-old is. 00:19:02.640 --> 00:19:07.039 So if I feel like that 10-year-old is not gonna get to that ball, I'm not calling it, right? 00:19:07.200 --> 00:19:10.240 The other thing that comes into play is the weather, the wind, whatever. 00:19:10.480 --> 00:19:18.240 Just I always always have to keep in mind that the infield fly is a rule that protects the offense. 00:19:18.480 --> 00:19:18.720 Right. 00:19:18.880 --> 00:19:19.119 Right? 00:19:19.279 --> 00:19:21.119 It is there to protect the offense. 00:19:21.440 --> 00:19:28.559 So I'm not gonna call it if it's a ball that I think might get down, because I'm not because then I'm giving the defense an out. 00:19:28.799 --> 00:19:29.039 Yeah. 00:19:29.200 --> 00:19:33.599 So, but it's the whole thing is with normal effort. 00:19:33.759 --> 00:19:34.000 Right. 00:19:34.160 --> 00:19:36.799 The play needs to be made with normal effort. 00:19:37.119 --> 00:19:39.599 Not like a diving awesome catch. 00:19:39.839 --> 00:19:40.079 Correct. 00:19:40.160 --> 00:19:42.240 So and it has to be in the field of play. 00:19:42.480 --> 00:19:47.039 And it's because I I I've seen it where as soon as that ball goes up in the air, into a fly. 00:19:47.279 --> 00:19:47.440 Absolutely. 00:19:47.680 --> 00:19:51.839 It's like without even without even glancing down to see what actions the infield's taking. 00:19:52.160 --> 00:19:52.480 Correct. 00:19:52.640 --> 00:19:56.799 We are taught to not make that call till the apex of the pop-up. 00:19:57.119 --> 00:19:57.359 Okay. 00:19:57.519 --> 00:19:58.319 So you shouldn't be. 00:19:58.799 --> 00:20:00.079 And you're not following the ball the whole way. 00:20:00.319 --> 00:20:00.799 You gotta look at it. 00:20:00.960 --> 00:20:02.319 You should not be following the ball. 00:20:02.480 --> 00:20:06.079 But we should umpires, and this is what we teach, you shouldn't ever follow the ball. 00:20:06.240 --> 00:20:09.279 The kid will take you to where the ball's going, right? 00:20:09.519 --> 00:20:15.920 Just like when I'm behind a plate and there's a pop-up that goes and I have no freaking idea where it is, I just watch the catcher's shoulder. 00:20:16.000 --> 00:20:17.440 She'll take me to it, right? 00:20:17.599 --> 00:20:24.480 So any pop-up, I watch the kid, I'm not watching the ball because there's a whole lot that can happen down here when I'm watching that ball. 00:20:25.119 --> 00:20:30.559 And that the the obstruction, the interference, those are the things we need to be aware of, not watch the ball. 00:20:30.720 --> 00:20:36.240 So when the ball goes up, I know it's in the infield, I just watch players and then I make my determination. 00:20:36.400 --> 00:20:38.880 But I don't say it as soon as the ball's hit, you know. 00:20:39.200 --> 00:20:42.240 Yeah, my my mine with you, mine with you was what was it? 00:20:42.400 --> 00:20:58.799 It was we were on our JV field, and it was a pop-up to the third towards the third baseman, and the it was the wind was blowing decent, and it was carrying her further and further out towards the outfield, and you called infield fly, and I'm like, that was not normal effort. 00:20:58.960 --> 00:21:02.640 Like she had to so that was my only disagreement with you. 00:21:02.880 --> 00:21:03.440 You were right then. 00:21:03.599 --> 00:21:04.000 You were right. 00:21:04.160 --> 00:21:06.559 That was my only in all these years, my only disagreement. 00:21:06.799 --> 00:21:09.119 If it was not normal effort, you're you were right. 00:21:09.279 --> 00:21:10.319 I I blew it. 00:21:10.559 --> 00:21:15.359 But yeah, that's my least favorite rule is the drop third strike. 00:21:15.599 --> 00:21:20.640 The the drop third strike just gets very frustrating to me, probably as a pitcher's dad. 00:21:20.799 --> 00:21:40.799 Um, probably it's just it's but like it's just a frustrating rule because and

scored.description: Welcome to 'ASX BRIEFS,' the definitive podcast for enthusiasts, investors, and professionals keen on staying ahead of the curve in the Australian Stock Exchange (ASX). Hosted by Andrew Musgrave, 'ASX BRIEFS' delves deep into the heart of Australia's financial markets, bringing you insightful conversations with the minds shaping the future of investing down under. Each episode, join Andrew as he interviews a diverse lineup of fund managers, executives, and industry insiders, offering you a unique blend of expert analysis, strategic insights, and the latest trends affecting the ASX. Whether you're a seasoned investor or just starting out, 'ASX BRIEFS' is your go-to source for comprehensive updates and thought-provoking discussions designed to inform, inspire, and empower your investment journey. Tune in to 'ASX BRIEFS' and take the pulse of Australia's financial markets right at your fingertips.

transcript.scoringWindow: Andrew Musgrave Welcome again to ASX Brief. And today we're joined by Phil Hodgson, the Managing Director and CEO of Calix Limited, an Australian industrial technology company developing platform solutions for industrial decarbonisation across cement, steel, alumina, critical minerals, as well as solutions in water treatment and carbon dioxide removal. Phil, great to have you with me today and welcome to the ASX Briefs Podcast. Phil Hodgson Thanks very much, Andrew. Andrew Musgrave Phil, for listeners who are new to Calix, can you explain the core platform technology, what it does, and why it matters for heavy industry? Phil Hodgson Certainly, Andrew. So, the core platform technology is a technology developed here in Australia. It's essentially around a new type of kiln or furnace, a new way to heat stuff up, as we say. A lot of today's minerals, cement, steel, etc., are made in furnaces and Calix has a new way to make these products. Essentially, what we do is we separate how you heat from what you heat. We do that with a rather large steel tube. The biggest tubes we've made are now as big as we'll ever make them, 1.8 meters in diameter and over 30 meters high. So, we're already a full scale on the core technology. We heat that tube to over a thousand degrees centigrade. So, this tube's glowing red hot. And whatever we heat goes down the middle of the tube. It's going to be a small particle size, much like sort of fine powder. So, iron ore fines, cement, sort of meal, all of absolutely the right size for our technology. That basically gets dropped down the tube. And as the dust or small particles floats down the tube, the red-hot walls of the tube radiate into the particles and heats them up and we can heat the tube any way you like. We can heat it with renewable energy, we can heat it with traditional fossil fuels on the outside of the tube and so the technology represents a way to heat stuff up that can use all sorts of different energy sources. But the other interesting thing is inside the tube, a lot of people may not realize that, say limestone, which is used to make cement, is nearly half by weight CO2. And so, when the cement and lime industries heat up that limestone, the CO2 comes out of the rock, and that's over half the emissions of the cement and lime industry. And so, when you're heating limestone in our tube, that CO2 doesn't escape. It doesn't head off into the atmosphere or get mixed with furnace gases. It comes out the top of the tube as a pretty pure stream. And so, the first applications of the technology we developed were to separate out CO2 when making lime and cement. But of course, since then we've gone on to look at all sorts of different applications, including our latest applications in making green iron, in which case we've got hydrogen inside the tube, which converts the iron ore to iron. So, you can see it's quite a versatile core technology platform that basically, as I say, is replacing the traditional kiln or furnace in all sorts of different industries. Andrew Musgrave And industrial decarbonisation is one of the hardest problems in climate. Sectors like cement and steel account for a significant share of global emissions and have few easy levers to pull. So why is Calix's approach particularly well suited to solving what others can't? Phil Hodgson Yeah, if you sort of think back to the tube, I described heated externally, it's actually a fairly simple, or we like to term it elegant solution. There are no moving parts, there's no complex pieces of equipment. It's basically a tube that's being heated externally. Of course, there's a lot of know-how that goes into developing the full-scale versions of this. But that's our first key advantage. The core concept is simple and elegant. The other advantage is, of course, decarbonisation, depending upon where you are in the world, is developing at different pieces. In some parts of the world, it's moving ahead, in other parts it's not. So, we need a technology that's going to be cost effective today, regardless of if there's a carbon price. And so, if you recall, I mentioned the energy flexibility of the tube, the ability to heat with electrons or heat with traditional fossil fuels or burning waste is actually one of the things that could make the technology cost effective today. So, you sort of get the decarbonization as a free ride or a cherry on top to economic solutions today with the tube. So, I think that's what separates us from other technologies attempting to achieve this. Andrew Musgrave Now, in April this year, the company announced the restructure of the lithium midstream project joint venture with PLS, another ASX listed company. Can you walk us through the logic of the deal? What does Calix give up? What does it retain? And why is this outcome strategically the right one for the company at this point in time? Phil Hodgson Yeah, this is an interesting one. We started our journey with PLS over three years ago, and that was to take a core, our core technology and use it in the lithium industry. PLS dig up spodumene, and we're using our core technology to take advantage of cheap energy, as in renewable energy at a mine site, to extract and concentrate lithium at PLS's site in the Pilbara. and so originally, we went into this deal as a joint venture partner with equity, us 45%, PLS 55%. but as the industry structurally changed and margins tightened on lithium, and that's the difference between the spodumene price and the lithium price, it became harder for us to participate in this simply because of the capital required. And so, we changed, we agreed to PLS to change the structure of the deal where we back out effectively our equity. We recovered all of the equity that we're put in, and PLS moved forward with carriage of the project, licensed from us. And so, the solution, which is the ability to produce lithium at a mine site with renewable energy, is jointly owned by us and PLS. And moving forward, we'll be sharing the margins on that when applied to third parties. But as part of the deal, PLS get a free license. So, so we got a capital back, PLS get a free license. So, the logic is that there's no downside for us. We're not exposed from a capital perspective. There's only upside with respect to license income from third parties, and we get paid engineering fees. It's really part of a capital light strategy that we're very focused on and doubling down on to some extent now as well. So that's the logic behind the deal. Andrew Musgrave Now turning to Leilac Technology. Leilac Limited is Calix's cement and lime application subsidiary. So, for our listeners who may not be familiar, can you explain what Leilac does and specifically why the ability to separate processed CO2 in cement is such a difficult and valuable problem to solve? Phil Hodgson Yeah. So, I described previously, I guess, our tube, the core part of our technology that we that we heat from the outside. And when we're processing cement, which is mainly limestone, that gets dropped down the middle of the tube and as that heats up, the CO2 comes out of the rock. And so that CO2 in a normal cement kiln gets mixed with furnace gases and ejected into the atmosphere. So separating CO2 from a whole lot of other gas is really difficult. You need big chemical plants to solve that problem and with our technology though, because the CO2 is trapped in the tube, it exits the top of the tube as a pretty pure stream. And so, the core technology has the ability to directly separate that CO2 that's coming out of the rock. And so that's what makes it a little different. We're not trying to separate a gas from a gas, we just don't allow the gases to mix in the first place. And of course, the ability to heat the tube, say with renewable electrons, you can see how we can start to get to very low to zero emissions cement using our technology. Andrew Musgrave And Leilac's hybrid heating system has the potential to lower fuel costs for cement producers independent of any carbon price. How important is that dual value proposition reducing costs now while enabling carbon capture later in convincing cement majors to commit to the technology? Phil Hodgson Well, I'd say it's critical. If you've in terms of the early part of the podcast, we talked a little bit about decarbonisation and the different pace at which different parts of the world are chasing decarbonisation. Some areas certainly stalled. So, if you have a technology that has a value proposition today without a price on carbon, then that can allow some of these cement majors to move forward on looking at the technology and committing to the technology. And they get the carbon and decarbonisation down the track as the cherry on top. So, the hybrid heating system, this is the ability of our tube to be heated in any way you like. We can have renewable electrons on the outside of the tube. We can have gas burning normal gases like fossil fuels, or we can burn waste. And so, the ability to switch between electrons, which may be expensive in some parts of the day, but cheap in other parts of the day, switching between those electrons and a standard fuel or even a waste fuel, which in some cases is very cheap to negative cost, that is what enables the technology to be economic today. And so, the hybrid heating system is a very important part of the technology that allows it to be economic today. Andrew Musgrave And you've landed a joint development agreement with the Adani Group's Ambuja Cements, one of the world's top cement producers, and it's a significant commercial milestone. So, how did this partnership come about and why has Leilac been selected as a strategic partner? Phil Hodgson Yeah, so we've been in discussion with many cement producers over several years, Adani among them. Adani, of course, became a major cement producer overnight with the purchase of Holcim's assets in India. They instantly became number two cement group in India and so in discussion with Adani and numerous other cement players, they've become

card.tagline: The Australian company turning a glowing steel tube into a climate solution
card.description: Calix CEO Phil Hodgson explains how a simple externally-heated tube could decarbonise cement, steel, and lithium production without waiting for a carbon price.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a hard-to-decarbonise sector with unusual clarity

scored.description: Podcast by Alaskans for Sustainable Budgets. Our top 3 issues each week in the world of Alaska oil, gas & fiscal policy.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The Weekly Top 3 (7.13.2026)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses Alaska's $1.9 billion budget deficit, Permanent Fund Corporation performance, and state fiscal policy affecting middle-income residents — specific, timely state-level political and budgetary issues.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a loose but identifiable segment structure (Permanent Fund analysis transitioning into a 'Weekly Top 3' segment on middle-income families), though pacing is uneven and chart references create dead air for audio-only listeners.

clipAbility.rationale: The line 'Why are we spending a billion dollars a year to make less money?' is a strong standalone moment, but most of the discussion relies on visual charts and back-and-forth crosstalk that requires surrounding context to land.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The Weekly Top 3 (7.6.2026)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers specific Alaska state budget mechanics, oil revenue thresholds, K-12 education funding disputes, and LNG legislation — clearly tied to current state political developments but limited to a regional audience and niche policy detail.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a numbered segment format ('Weekly Top 3') with a clear transition between topics, though the discussion within segments is conversational and occasionally rambling.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require significant context about Alaska's oil revenue waterfall structure or specific legislative history to land, with only a few punchy lines — like the 'I want my two dollars' riff — that could stand alone.

Each month we explore key topics important to the City of Redding and connect with the leaders and experts of these initiatives to learn more about how and why things are happening. Connect with us at podcast@cityofredding.org or on Facebook, Twitter or Instagram @cityofredding.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Inside IRONMAN 70.3 Northern California: Bringing a World-Class Race to Redding.

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a local government promotional podcast episode about a triathlon event logistics — sports and tourism content with no meaningful political or news dimension.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear interview format with identifiable topic segments (course route, athlete preparation, event logistics), though pacing is conversational and somewhat meandering.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context about the race or Redding specifically; the DNS/DNF explanation is the closest thing to a self-contained, quotable segment but still leans local.

scored.description: Hosted by Andrew Parker and sponsored by Parker | Daniels | Kibort law firm in Minneapolis, MN. The show focuses on Politics, Israel and the Law and features many prominent guests and compelling topics from the news in each episode. Visit https://theandrewparkershow.com/ for more info.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Episode 492,The Andrew Parker Show - Warmongers or Realists? When War Becomes the Last Resort

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with current political developments — the congressional vote on Israel aid, Democratic Party positioning, Iran negotiations, and the legacy of Lindsey Graham — making it clearly on-topic for News/Politics, though it stops short of the sharpest tier due to its broad, opinion-driven framing rather than breaking-news specificity.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through related themes (Israel defense, warmonger accusations, Democratic Party criticism, colonizer debate) but lacks clear segmentation, with the host frequently circling back and trailing off, and the transcript ending mid-thought on an unrelated prediction segment.

clipAbility.rationale: There are a handful of punchy standalone assertions — particularly the colonizer rebuttal passage and the Democratic primary electability argument — but most moments require surrounding context to land and the rhetorical style is discursive rather than quotable.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Episode 491, The Andrew Parker Show - The Dangerous Shift in American Politics that Few are Talking About

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with specific current political dynamics — primary determinism, DSA electoral gains, gerrymandering, and Hamas-adjacent political figures — though the analysis leans more toward ideological commentary than breaking news coverage.

contentStructure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline from cultural alarm to electoral mechanics, but the opening meanders through socialism critiques and October 7 commentary before landing on the more structured primary-determinism argument.

clipAbility.rationale: The explanation of why 90% of House seats are decided in primaries — not general elections — is a genuinely self-contained, quotable passage that could stand alone as a preview clip without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: M and M EXTRA  07.17.26 | Trump’s Election Speech; Walz’s Rapist Pardon; “Odyssey” Film

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with a recent Trump election speech, the Walz pardon controversy, and media framing of political claims — all timely political topics, though the analysis leans heavily toward partisan commentary rather than hard news reporting.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has identifiable topic segments (Trump speech, election fraud debate, media language) but the conversation meanders through tangents like time-travel hypotheticals and personal anecdotes, disrupting consistent pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: The extended riff defining 'lie' as requiring intent to deceive — and applying it to media coverage of Trump — is a self-contained, quotable argument that could stand alone as a preview clip without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: M and M EXTRA  7.10.26 | Might Platner stay in?  And bleeps or no bleeps?

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode centers on a specific, timely political scandal — rape allegations against Maine gubernatorial candidate Graham Platner and whether he will formally withdraw before a ballot deadline — making it substantively on-topic for News/Politics listeners.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves from a lengthy ad read into a loosely organized discussion that incorporates an audio clip from another show, but transitions are abrupt and the conversation meanders without clear signposting or a defined close.

clipAbility.rationale: The segment speculating about a potential deal between Platner and the Maine AG — 'you step down, we won't charge you with rape' — is a genuinely standalone, provocative moment, but most of the surrounding discussion requires context about who Platner is and what preceded it.

card.tagline: Todd Blanche, ICE killings, and a president who faces zero consequences
card.description: Mark Thompson and a co-host dissect Blanche's dismissive Epstein meeting, Susan Collins' confirmation calculus, and whether ICE violence has lost its political shock value.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down the week's most consequential confirmation fight

scored.description: Weekdays 7:00am – 10:00am on 660 AM The Answer in Dallas/Fort Worth. A local North Texas favorite! Mark Davis continues to trade opinions with listeners on 660 AM The Answer. Mark Davis has become one of the most successful radio talk show hosts in America. More than forty years hosting shows, with most of those years in North Texas, have established him as a foremost conservative voice both locally and nationally.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: M and M EXTRA  07.17.26 | Trump’s Election Speech; Walz’s Rapist Pardon; “Odyssey” Film

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with Trump's recent election speech, the Walz pardon controversy, and media framing of political claims — all timely, specific political news hooks.

contentStructure.rationale: The transcript shows a clear conversational throughline around Trump's speech and election integrity, but the dialogue meanders through tangents (time-travel hypotheticals, personal compliments) without tight signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: The 'definition of a lie' segment is a genuinely self-contained, quotable moment that could stand alone as a preview, though most other exchanges require surrounding context to land.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: M and M EXTRA  7.10.26 | Might Platner stay in?  And bleeps or no bleeps?

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode centers on a specific, timely political scandal — rape allegations against Maine Democratic candidate Graham Platner and whether he'll formally withdraw before a ballot deadline — making it substantively on-topic for News/Politics listeners.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves from a lengthy ad read into a clip from another show (Scott Jennings) before hosts resume commentary, creating a loose, patchwork structure without clear signposting or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: The speculation about a potential deal between Platner and the Maine Attorney General — drop out in exchange for no rape charges — is a genuinely self-contained, provocative moment that would work as a standalone preview.

card.tagline: Is calling Trump a liar actually the lie? Mark Davis makes the case.
card.description: Davis and a co-host dissect Trump's election speech, the media's use of 'debunked,' and why Walz's rapist pardon is the week's most damning political story.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down the week's sharpest conservative political flashpoints

scored.feedTitle: The Dana Show with Dana Loesch – Politics & Culture Commentary
scored.description:
The Dana Show with Dana Loesch is a daily conservative news and political commentary podcast delivering clear, fearless analysis of today’s biggest headlines. Each weekday, Dana Loesch breaks down American politics, culture, media bias, and government overreach with sharp insight and unapologetic conservative principles.From Second Amendment news and free speech debates to media hypocrisy and Washington dysfunction, The Dana Show cuts through the noise to explain what’s really happening—and why it matters. If you’re looking for common-sense political analysis, independent conservative commentary, and a daily news podcast that challenges the mainstream narrative, The Dana Show with Dana Loesch is your go-to source.

https://www.radioamerica.com/the-dana-show/
https://heliosmedia.us/dana-show/

episodeScores.episodeTitle: BONUS: Trump Reveals China Declared War On Our Elections

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with current political developments — Rubio's counter-terrorism speech, Trump's China election interference claims, VP communications strategy, and U.S. geopolitical moves in Latin America — but the excerpt also wanders into Michelle Obama commentary and a Schumer bathroom joke, diluting the focus.

contentStructure.rationale: There is a loose throughline connecting Rubio's speech to VP messaging failures to China policy wins, but the episode meanders through unrelated tangents (Michelle Obama, Schumer gas joke) before returning to its core argument, making the structure feel inconsistent.

clipAbility.rationale: The passage 'politics should not be entertainment — when you make policy entertainment, that becomes an issue' and the VP comms critique ('Who's running comms for the VP?') are punchy, self-contained moments that would work as standalone previews without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: BONUS: Inside Iran's Assassination Plot Against President Trump

topicRelevance.rationale: Despite the episode title referencing Iran's assassination plot against Trump, the sampled transcript is dominated by prohibition bar complaints, World Cup antisemitism commentary, a heated hair brush anecdote, and a SuperBeets ad — not substantive news or political analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The transcript lurches between unrelated tangents — bars, World Cup riots, personal grooming, sponsor reads, and a Florida Man segment — with no discernible throughline or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context or are mid-ramble asides; the antisemitism riff has some standalone energy but relies on setup and devolves into a hair brush joke before landing.

card.tagline: Dana Loesch blasts VP's podcast conspiracy tour while Rubio's speech went ignored
card.description: Loesch argues the administration squandered Rubio's landmark left-wing terrorism speech by letting the VP dominate headlines with conspiracy theories and attacks on a U.S. ally.
card.reason: Because this episode dissects a major rift in Trump administration messaging strategy

scored.description:
The Mike Gallagher Show is a daily visit with America's happy conservative warrior.  Whether it's politics, culture, entertainment or the media, Mike dives in to the issues of the day with a vengeance.
You can also watch Mike Gallagher on Salem News Channel

episodeScores.episodeTitle: M and M EXTRA  07.17.26 | Trump’s Election Speech; Walz’s Rapist Pardon; “Odyssey” Film

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with a recent Trump election speech, the Walz pardon controversy, and media framing of political claims — all timely political topics, though the discussion leans heavily into opinion and relitigating 2020 rather than breaking news analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The transcript shows a conversational back-and-forth with identifiable topic threads (election fraud framing, media language, Pence counterfactual), but pacing is loose and the segment meanders through tangents without tight signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: The 'perfect definition of a lie' riff and the time-machine hypothetical about 2020 are genuinely self-contained and quotable moments that could stand alone as preview clips without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: M and M EXTRA  7.10.26 | Might Platner stay in?  And bleeps or no bleeps?

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers a specific, timely political scandal — rape allegations against Maine Democratic candidate Graham Platner and whether he'll formally withdraw before a ballot deadline — with substantive discussion of prosecutorial decisions and potential deal-making with the state AG.

contentStructure.rationale: The excerpt moves from a weight-loss ad read into a clip from the Scott Jennings Show into co-host commentary, with loose threading between segments and no clear signposting or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: The speculation about a deal between Platner and the Maine AG — 'you step down, we won't charge you with rape' — is a genuinely standalone, provocative moment that would work as a preview clip without much setup.
