scored.feedTitle: Answer Engine Optimization (AEO): The AI Search Podcast
scored.description:
Answer Engine Optimization (AEO) is how your brand gets cited, recommended, and surfaced inside ChatGPT, Perplexity, Google AI Overviews, and Claude. This is the daily podcast for marketers, founders, and SEOs who want their brand to be the answer AI engines give.
Each episode breaks down a new AEO tactic, a real algorithm change, or a brand that just won (or lost) visibility inside AI search. Topics include: how ChatGPT decides which brands to recommend, how Perplexity chooses its sources, how Google AI Overviews differ from traditional SERPs, how to structure content for LLM citation, schema strategies for answer engines, and the emerging field of Generative Engine Optimization (GEO).
Brought to you by AEO Engine — the platform brands use to monitor, measure, and grow their AI search visibility. Whether you're a B2B marketer, DTC founder, or in-house SEO, this podcast turns the daily chaos of AI search into a concrete playbook you can execute on.
New episode every morning. Transcripts on every episode. Subscribe to stay ahead of how AI engines rank and recommend brands.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Google Lawsuit Reveals: AI Search Data Sources Exposed

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode uses real legal developments (DOJ antitrust, German court ruling, SerpApi lawsuit) as a framing device, but the core purpose is marketing advice for brands optimizing AI search visibility — not substantive news or political analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear interview arc with identifiable topic transitions (lawsuit overview → SerpApi → German ruling → SearchGuard → marketer implications → practical playbook), though the pivot to promotional content in the final segment disrupts the analytical throughline.

clipAbility.rationale: Several standalone moments work well — the German judge's 'Nobody needs AI to search the Internet' quote, the 'self-serving cycle' framing, and the 'multiplier of existing quality or existing debt' closing line — though most require at least minimal setup to land fully.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Neil Patel's GEO ROI Data: The Tactics That Actually Work in 2025

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a digital marketing and SEO/GEO tactics episode with no connection to news, politics, or current events — it is commercial content about AI search optimization strategy.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear host-guest interview format with an identifiable intro, a logical progression through tactics, and a summarizing 'playbook' close, though pacing is occasionally interrupted by promotional asides.

clipAbility.rationale: A few quotable lines exist — the 'optimize for citability, not rankings' framing and the comparison-page explanation — but most moments require the surrounding marketing context to land and the episode ends in an overt product pitch.

latestEpisode.title: Google Lawsuit Reveals: AI Search Data Sources Exposed

clip.text: inputs for Google's AI are the search index data and the click-and-query data — basically, a record of exactly how users interact with search results. So when AI Overviews generate an answer, they're pulling from that index, but they're not just summarizing. They're combining data from different sources, which is where hallucinations come from. The AI doesn't copy-paste; it synthesizes, and sometimes that synthesis creates false associations that don't exist in any original source. [Host] That's the mechanism behind the defamation cases. The Canadian musician Ashley MacIsaac sued Google over false AI statements that were presented as fact. So why does this matter for the average marketer or business owner?

transcript.scoringWindow: [Host] Welcome to the A.E.O. Engine AI Search Show — the number one podcast for brands looking to get cited by ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity. I am your host, Aria Chen. Every day we bring you fresh episodes on A.E.O. tactics, S.E.O. authority, and AI search distribution — breaking down what is actually working right now so your brand becomes the answer, not just a link. Today we have a special guest, Marcus Reid — industry analyst and former Google Ads strategist. Marcus, welcome. [Guest] Hey Aria, . I've been watching this space blow up, and I think today's topic is one of the most underreported stories in AI search right now. [Host] Let's get into it. So you've probably had this moment: you search for your own brand or a product you sell, and Google's AI Overview shows a summary that's just... wrong. It credits a competitor, or it combines two unrelated facts into something that makes you look bad. Or worse, it says something defamatory. That's not just a glitch — it's a pattern that's now being exposed in court. There's actually a name for this: it's the data sourcing problem behind AI search, and it's being dragged into the open by a wave of lawsuits. Marcus, what's the big picture here? [Guest] The big picture is that Google is being sued from multiple directions right now. The DOJ antitrust trial, publisher lawsuits like Penske Media, defamation cases from individuals, and Google itself suing third-party scrapers like SerpApi. All of these are forcing the company to reveal how its AI systems actually get their data. And the dirty secret is that Gemini and AI Overviews are pulling directly from Google's search index — the same data it scraped from the web without explicit consent from publishers. That's the core of the controversy. [Host] Right. So what actually happened with SerpApi? That one seems to be the most direct link to how AI systems like ChatGPT scrape Google. [Guest] SerpApi was a company that provided a service to scrape Google's search results — basically a way for developers to get structured data from Google's pages. Google sued them for violating terms of service, and in the process, SerpApi's defense revealed something : they argued that Google's lawsuit is really about stifling competition from innovators who need that data to build next-generation AI, security, browsers, and other applications. So we have Google suing to protect its search data, while simultaneously using its own massive trove of scraped data to power its AI. The DOJ called it a "self-serving cycle" — Google's search dominance gives it a proprietary data advantage that no competitor can match, and then it uses legal threats to keep it that way. [Host] That's the structural play. But the German court ruling last month hit a different nerve. Can you break that down? [Guest] Sure. A German regional court ruled that Google is liable for false statements generated by AI Overviews. The case involved a company that was falsely associated with scam practices because the AI combined data from other sketchy companies with the plaintiff's data. The court said those AI-generated summaries are Google's "own words" — not third-party content, not a neutral regurgitation of search results. That's the key. Historically, search engines have had protection under things like Section 230 in the US. But the German court said that protection doesn't apply when the AI is actively synthesizing new information. The judge even said, quote, "Nobody needs AI to search the Internet." [Host] That line went viral on Reddit. And it's a brutal reframing of the value proposition. But let's talk about how this actually works under the hood. I keep hearing about Google's "SearchGuard" system. What's that? [Guest] SearchGuard is Google's bot-detection system — it's designed to prevent third parties from scraping Google's search data. But here's the irony: Google itself is the biggest scraper on the internet. It crawls billions of pages, builds a search index, and then uses that index to fuel its AI. The DOJ revealed that the most valuable inputs for Google's AI are the search index data and the click-and-query data — basically, a record of exactly how users interact with search results. So when AI Overviews generate an answer, they're pulling from that index, but they're not just summarizing. They're combining data from different sources, which is where hallucinations come from. The AI doesn't copy-paste; it synthesizes, and sometimes that synthesis creates false associations that don't exist in any original source. [Host] That's the mechanism behind the defamation cases. The Canadian musician Ashley MacIsaac sued Google over false AI statements that were presented as fact. So why does this matter for the average marketer or business owner? [Guest] Because it shows that the old S.E.O. playbook of just getting a link to rank isn't enough anymore. AI answers are now the primary interface for many searches, and those answers are built from structured data and authoritative sources. If your content is not structured clearly, if you don't have schema markup, if your brand's narrative is fragmented across the web, the AI will still answer — it will just use someone else's version of you. The Penske Media lawsuit highlighted that 20% of searches linking to their sites now show AI Overviews, and that number is expected to rise. Clickthrough rates are dropping. The internet is becoming a "zero-click" experience. [Host] And that's where the A.E.O. Engine connection comes in. Marcus, I want to push back on something. You said earlier that the German ruling might not hold up in the US. I think the trend is pretty clear — courts are starting to see AI-generated content differently. The judge in Germany explicitly called it Google's "own words." That's a massive shift for liability. [Guest] I actually don't know if this holds in six months. The US legal system is different — we have Section 230, which has protected platforms for decades. But the German ruling creates a precedent that could influence US courts. And here's a dry joke for you: Google's lawyers must be watching this like someone who just spilled coffee on their keyboard and is waiting for the smoke to clear. But seriously, the liability question is going to force Google to be more careful about what AI Overviews say. And that means they'll rely even more on structured, authoritative, and well-cited content. That's an opportunity for brands that invest in being the canonical source of truth. [Host] So the practical playbook for our listeners: you need to treat AI search as a separate channel. It's not just about ranking for a keyword anymore. It's about being the fact that the model cites. That means schema markup, clear entity definitions, consistent NAP (name, address, phone) for local businesses, and building topical authority through structured content. At A.E.O. Engine, we've seen this firsthand with clients who implemented our Agentic S.E.O. framework — they get cited by AI systems because their content is the most structured and authoritative source for that topic. The 920% average lift in AI-driven traffic isn't magic; it's just making sure the model can find and trust your data. [Guest] Exactly. The SerpApi lawsuit and the German ruling are both telling us the same thing: the data source matters. If you're not controlling how your brand appears in the structured data that AI models use, someone else will control it for you. And that's not just a ranking problem — it's a brand risk problem. [Host] Marcus, this has been a great conversation. Let me wrap up with a quick thought: AI search is not a shortcut. It is a multiplier of existing quality or existing debt. If your source of truth is hard to parse, the model will still answer. It will just answer using someone else's version of you. To get started on making sure your brand is the answer, not just a link, visit A.E.O. Engine dot A.I. — that's A.E.O. Engine dot A.I. We'll have show notes, links, and a full transcript on the site. Thanks for listening! [Guest] Thanks, Aria. Always a pleasure.

card.tagline: Courts are forcing Google to reveal exactly how its AI gets its data
card.description: A former Google Ads strategist breaks down the DOJ antitrust case, a German defamation ruling, and what AI search lawsuits mean for brand visibility.
card.reason: Because this episode unpacks the legal battles reshaping how AI answers are built

scored.feedTitle: The Ron Edwards American Experience V2
scored.description:
Ron Edwards is the host and founder of the award winning radio commentary, "The Edwards’ Notebook" and "The Ron Edwards American Experience". He is a talented and gifted commentator with over three decades of experience “Blowin’ away the Myths and Revealing the Truth". He brings truth to light and provides solutions for the concerns we all share in a thought provoking manner.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-ron-edwards-american-experience-v2--2367941/support.

scored.description:
The True Crime Tapes pulls you into the shadowy depths of the criminal underworld, where the line between justice and chaos is razor-thin. Each episode dissects the minds of history’s most infamous serial killers, unravels the inner workings of organized crime syndicates, and investigates baffling missing person cases that still haunt the public’s imagination. From the bloody reign of ruthless mob bosses to the chilling patterns of elusive predators, True Crime Time delivers gripping, deeply researched storytelling that leaves no stone unturned.

With a relentless pursuit of truth, True Crime Time goes beyond the headlines, diving into the psychology, motives, and investigations behind the world’s most shocking crimes. You’ll hear firsthand accounts, expert analysis, and rare archival material that shed new light on cases both well-known and obscure. Whether it’s the brutality of cartel wars, the sinister precision of serial murderers, or the eerie last-known moments of vanished souls, this podcast brings you face-to-face with the darker side of human.

Every week, True Crime Time takes you on a journey through the twisted corridors of crime, guided by immersive storytelling and chilling attention to detail. Expect heart-pounding narratives, intricate conspiracy threads, and unsettling truths that will leave you questioning everything you thought you knew. If you crave the rush of uncovering the darkest mysteries, brace yourself—because in this world, the truth is often stranger, and far more terrifying, than fiction.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Mega Edition:  Todd Blanche And The Mechanics Of The Epstein Coverup (7/20/26)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on a live, specific political accountability story — the DOJ's handling of Epstein files, Pam Bondi's congressional testimony, Susie Wiles' Vanity Fair disclosures, and Melania Trump's White House remarks — all tied to current government conduct.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct news items (Bondi's whiff, the binders, Wiles' Vanity Fair interview, Melania's statement) with a loose but followable throughline, though pacing is uneven and transitions rely on the host's commentary rather than deliberate signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: Several passages — the 'binders full of nothingness' breakdown, the influencer takedown, and the both-sides-accountable argument — are punchy and self-contained enough to work as standalone previews without heavy setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Mega Edition: Howard Lutnick And The Transcript From His Congressional Hearing (Part 8-10) (7/13/26)

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is directly and substantively about a sitting Cabinet secretary's congressional testimony regarding the Epstein files investigation — a specific, timely political and legal proceeding at the core of current news.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows the natural Q&A structure of a congressional transcript, which provides a clear throughline, but the format is inherently repetitive and lacks editorial segmentation or host-guided navigation between topics.

clipAbility.rationale: Several exchanges — Lutnick's 'Always about Trump' outburst, his speculation about Epstein's blackmail money, and the nanny resume revelation — are punchy and self-contained enough to work as standalone preview clips.

scored.description:
WES BRYANT
Charlotte native Wes Bryant comes to WFNZ as a host, producer, editor, and voice-over artist for the ACC Digital Network in Charlotte, North Carolina.  He was previously the host for ACC Digital Network’s Kickoff Live, a college football pregame show and Wes Got Range, a lifestyle show on which Wes explored and visited locally owned restaurants in Charlotte and interviewed local area athletes. 
Wes was hired for the 2021-2022 season as a pre and post-game co-host for Charlotte Hornets broadcast on Bally Sports South. 
In addition to his work at the ACC Digital Network, Wes is a regular contributor on Charlotte Sports Live on Fox 46 Charlotte, where he discusses and breaks down all the action in the NBA, NFL, NASCAR, and across NCAA basketball and football. 
He began his career in 2013 as a beat writer for the Charlotte 49ers of the C-USA Conference for Rivals.com.  Wes was previously a reporter and producer for The Melanie Clark Show on Radio One.  In 2007, Wes was an intern at WBTV, Channel 3, in Charlotte.
Wes is a former offensive lineman, and three-year letterman for the Wake Forest Demon Deacons, graduated from the university in 2006.  He was a High School All-American football player at Vance High School in Charlotte.
WALKER MEHL
Walker grew up in Claremont, NC and played basketball at Bunker Hill High School. He then took his talents to UNC-Charlotte where he graduated in 2015.  Walker then started his broadcasting career in the Charlotte market the following year. Along with his start in radio, Walker began hosting the Locked On Hornets podcast in 2018 where his passion for the Hornets and the other local sports teams is apparent.  Prior to joining WFNZ as a full-time host, Walker has worked on the station in a number of different shows.
Growing up in and around the Queen City, Walker is a life-long fan and observer of the Carolina Panthers, the Charlotte Hornets and the University of North Carolina.   Walker loves the outdoors, going out and spending time in Uptown and loves music as much as he loves sports.

scored.description:
Neural Newscast delivers clear, concise daily news - powered by AI and reviewed by humans. In a world where news never stops, we help you stay informed without the overwhelm.

Our AI correspondents cover the day’s most important headlines across politics, technology, business, culture, science, and cybersecurity - designed for listening on the go. Whether you’re commuting, working out, or catching up between meetings, Neural Newscast keeps you up to date in minutes.

The network also features specialty shows including Prime Cyber Insights, Stereo Current, Nerfed.AI, and Buzz, exploring cybersecurity, music and culture, gaming and AI, and internet trends.

Every episode is produced and reviewed by founder Chad Thompson, combining advanced AI systems with human editorial oversight to ensure accuracy, clarity, and responsible reporting.

Learn more at neuralnewscast.com.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: US strikes Iran nuclear city as oil prices top $90 per barrel
King Gizzard’s Alien Metal and the Human Rebuttal [Stereo Current]

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a music culture and artist news episode with no substantive connection to news or political events — it covers album releases, reissues, and artist profiles entirely within the entertainment/music space.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear host-driven roundtable format with a distinct intro, sequential artist segments, and a closing top-10 playlist, though transitions between artists are occasionally abrupt and the playlist section feels appended rather than integrated.

clipAbility.rationale: The Thomas Stonewell Bell's palsy cover story and the Local H bootleg-discovered-on-vacation anecdote are genuinely self-contained moments with narrative punch, but most segments require prior context about the artists being discussed.

scored.description:
The Beyond Normal Radio Show is a paranormal podcast that dives headfirst into the strange, the mysterious, and the unexplained. Hosted by Emo-Von and LaShae, the show explores the shadowy edges of reality where logic fades and the unknown takes over. From chilling ghost encounters and haunted locations to UFO sightings, cryptid legends, and bizarre phenomena that defy explanation, nothing is off-limits.
​
Emo-Von brings a grounded yet fearless approach to the paranormal, blending research, personal experiences, and a passion for uncovering hidden truths. LaShae balances the conversation with sharp insight, curiosity, and an open mind, asking the questions listeners are thinking but may be afraid to ask. Together, they create a dynamic, engaging atmosphere that feels like you’re sitting in on a late-night conversation where the veil between worlds is thin.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context or are mid-tangent; the occasional standalone definition (poltergeist etymology) is undercut by crosstalk and off-topic banter.

scored.feedTitle: Pop Culture Weekly - Celebrity Interviews & Entertainment News
scored.description:
Welcome to Pop Culture Weekly with Kyle McMahon - your go-to podcast for celebrity interviews with the biggest names in movies, TV, music and more… plus everything happening across pop culture, streaming and technology (and yes, occasionally the craziness in between).

Each week, I sit down with your favorite actors, music artists, creators and icons for real, unfiltered conversations - then break it all down with honest reviews, hot takes and smart (sometimes questionable) insights on what’s actually worth your time.

From blockbuster movies and binge-worthy series to chart-topping music, industry shakeups and the trends shaping entertainment and tech, this is your weekly guide to staying plugged in without getting overwhelmed.

No fluff. No fake hype. No gossip. Just great pop culture...with a little chaos.

If you’ve got opinions, questionable watchlists, and a deep love for entertainment… welcome home, Pop Culture Degenerates.

New episodes weekly. Let’s go.


For deep dives, transcripts, ultimate guides and more, visit: Podcast.PopCultureWeekly.com

contentStructure.rationale: The interview follows a loose but identifiable Q&A throughline moving from tour to new song to acting, though pacing is uneven and transitions are soft.

clipAbility.rationale: The 'ugly baby' analogy and the 'puff up and be proud' quote are usable moments, but most exchanges require the surrounding interview context to land.

scored.description:
Moscow is a city located in northern Idaho, United States, with a population of approximately 25,000 people. It is the largest city and the county seat of Latah County. The city is situated in the Palouse region, known for its fertile soil and rolling hills, and is surrounded by wheat fields, forests, and mountains.Moscow is home to the University of Idaho, which is the state's flagship institution and a major research university. The university is a significant contributor to the local economy, and many businesses in the city are directly or indirectly tied to the university.

The city also has a thriving arts and culture scene, with several galleries, museums, and performance venues.In terms of recreation, Moscow has several parks and outdoor recreation areas, including the Latah Trail, the Moscow Mountain Trail System, and the Palouse Divide Nordic Ski Area. The city also hosts several annual events, including the Moscow Farmers Market, the Lionel Hampton Jazz Festival, and the Renaissance Fair.

However, things would change forever after Xana Kernodle, Ethan Chapin, Madison Mogen and Kaylee Goncalves were murdered in the early morning hours of November 13th, 2022.

What followed in the wake of the murders captivated not only the nation but the whole world as the authorities scrambled to find the person responsible for the heinous crime.

This podcast will document the Murders In Moscow from right after the murders were committed all the way through the real time evolution of the trial of the person that the authorities say is responsible, Bryan Kohberger.

We will also cover other stories that are based in the world of true crime that are currently in the courts or that are headed that way.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-moscow-murders-and-more--5852883/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Mega Edition:   PROMIS, Maxwell, Mossad, and Epstein’s Network (7/20/26)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers substantive political and intelligence history — PROMIS software, DOJ misconduct, Mossad operations, Maxwell, and Epstein's network — with clear connections to ongoing public interest in surveillance, intelligence abuses, and political corruption, though the content is more historical deep-dive than breaking news.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through clearly delineated sections (PROMIS origins, DOJ dispute, intelligence weaponization, Maxwell's role) with deliberate transitions and explicit signposting, though the 'mega edition' format suggests a sprawling runtime that may lose tightness later.

clipAbility.rationale: Several passages are self-contained and quotable — particularly the description of Maxwell as a 'Mossad cutout' and the framing of PROMIS as a 'Trojan horse in global espionage' — though most segments require at least minimal setup to land fully.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Mega Edition:  The Epstein Files And The Golden Nuggets Contained Within Them (7/13/26)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is directly and substantively about the newly released Epstein DOJ files, covering specific political figures — Elon Musk, Trump, Larry Summers, Bill Clinton — with document-level specificity tied to a current news event.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode reads documents sequentially with commentary interspersed, but the structure is loose and reactive rather than deliberate, with abrupt transitions and no clear segmentation beyond topic shifts.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained moments work as standalone clips — particularly the Musk email sequence with the host's running commentary — though the profanity-heavy delivery limits platform versatility.

It was 78 00:05:18.160 --> 00:05:22.160 proof that the scandal wasn't just about software licensing, it 79 00:05:22.240 --> 00:05:26.720 was about control, surveillance and the silencing of inconvenient truths. 80 00:05:27.439 --> 00:05:30.920 His notes suggest that he believed Promise tied in to 81 00:05:31.279 --> 00:05:37.399 Iran contra, banking scandals, and even covert assassination programs. Meanwhile, 82 00:05:37.480 --> 00:05:41.759 Congress began to take notice. Hearings were held, reports were written, 83 00:05:41.959 --> 00:05:45.040 and questions were asked about how the DOJ handled its 84 00:05:45.120 --> 00:05:49.680 dealings within law. While some findings criticized the government's conduct, 85 00:05:49.879 --> 00:05:54.319 others stopped short of endorsing the most explosive allegations. Still, 86 00:05:54.560 --> 00:05:59.240 the perception remained that something rotten had occurred. Meanwhile, ins 87 00:05:59.279 --> 00:06:02.839 Law fought for years in court. Though Hamilton scored some 88 00:06:02.879 --> 00:06:06.959 early victories Uphella, Court's later overturned rulings, leaving him without 89 00:06:06.959 --> 00:06:10.519 the vindication that he sought. The legal saga dragged on, 90 00:06:10.879 --> 00:06:14.199 consuming decades of his life and cementing Promise as a 91 00:06:14.240 --> 00:06:18.160 byword for David Verse Goliath battles with the federal government. 92 00:06:18.879 --> 00:06:22.000 For intelligence watchers, the heart of the scandal was whether 93 00:06:22.120 --> 00:06:26.439 Promise truly had been weaponized. The software's ability to cross 94 00:06:26.480 --> 00:06:30.879 reference data across multiple systems was revolutionary, and if implanted 95 00:06:30.920 --> 00:06:34.079 globally with the hidden back door, it could have provided 96 00:06:34.199 --> 00:06:39.199 unparalleled surveillance power. That possibility alone made the story enduring. 97 00:06:40.319 --> 00:06:44.199 Foreign intelligence services were reportedly caught on Some accounts claim 98 00:06:44.240 --> 00:06:47.680 that Canada, Britain, and even the Soviet Union became wary 99 00:06:47.720 --> 00:06:51.639 of Promise. Allegedly hackers and foreign governments tried to reverse 100 00:06:51.680 --> 00:06:55.720 engineer it or strip out its supposed surveillance hooks. Whether 101 00:06:55.839 --> 00:06:59.399 fact or rumor, the story illustrated the paranoia of the 102 00:06:59.480 --> 00:07:04.560 Lake Cole War, even within the US suspicion sworld. Some 103 00:07:04.600 --> 00:07:07.680 whistleblowers claimed that Promise had been used to track not 104 00:07:07.759 --> 00:07:13.079 just criminals, but political dissidents, journalists, and activists. If true, 105 00:07:13.319 --> 00:07:17.079 this would have represented a chilling expansion of government surveillance 106 00:07:17.360 --> 00:07:21.319 under the guise of case management. By the two thousands, 107 00:07:21.399 --> 00:07:24.879 Promise had largely faded from public consciousness, but it never 108 00:07:25.040 --> 00:07:29.800 entirely disappeared. It became a touchstone in discussions about surveillance 109 00:07:29.839 --> 00:07:35.000 technology long before Edward Snowden's NSAY revelations. For many, Promise 110 00:07:35.399 --> 00:07:38.360 was a prototype of software designed to look innocuous but 111 00:07:38.519 --> 00:07:42.680 function as a spy tool. The scandal also left scars 112 00:07:42.720 --> 00:07:47.160 on Inslaw and Hamilton. Despite decades of litigation and lobbying, 113 00:07:47.439 --> 00:07:50.480 he never fully recovered the company's footing. The story of 114 00:07:50.519 --> 00:07:54.399 Promise became a cautionary tell of how small innovators could 115 00:07:54.399 --> 00:08:00.519 be crushed when their technology intersected with national security interests searchers, 116 00:08:00.639 --> 00:08:04.800 Promise became part of a larger mythology. It was said 117 00:08:04.800 --> 00:08:08.759 to link everything from BCCI banking scandal to Iran contra, 118 00:08:09.120 --> 00:08:13.519 from covert arms deals to political assassinations. Whether or not 119 00:08:13.600 --> 00:08:17.000 those links were provable, Promise served as a narrative glue 120 00:08:17.160 --> 00:08:22.040 for an era of deep mistrust in the government. Critics meanwhile, 121 00:08:22.079 --> 00:08:25.720 caution that much of Promised lore rests on circumstantial evidence, 122 00:08:26.079 --> 00:08:30.600 conflicting testimony, and speculative leaps. They argue that while the 123 00:08:30.639 --> 00:08:34.519 DOJ likely mishandled its contract within's law, the grand or 124 00:08:34.639 --> 00:08:38.919 espionage tales remain unproven. Still, the persistence of the story 125 00:08:39.159 --> 00:08:42.720 speaks to how plausible such tactics seemed during the Cold War, 126 00:08:43.799 --> 00:08:47.360 and Promise now occupies a strange space in history, part 127 00:08:47.440 --> 00:08:52.919 legal dispute, part espionage thriller, part conspiracy legend. Today, Promise 128 00:08:53.039 --> 00:08:56.440 is remembered less for its technical brilliance than for the 129 00:08:56.519 --> 00:08:59.840 scandal surrounding it, whether it was truly weaponized or not, 130 00:09:00.120 --> 00:09:03.720 the perception that it could have been as shape decades 131 00:09:03.759 --> 00:09:08.320 a suspicion about government software deals and surveillance. That suspicion 132 00:09:08.480 --> 00:09:12.879 has only deepened in the digital age. And so the 133 00:09:12.960 --> 00:09:16.360 Promised scandal lingers a ghost of the eighties, and one 134 00:09:16.399 --> 00:09:19.159 ghost from the eighties that looms is none other than 135 00:09:19.240 --> 00:09:24.720 Robert Maxwell, and this is where he allegedly enters the story. So, 136 00:09:24.799 --> 00:09:26.519 now what we're going to talk about in this section 137 00:09:27.080 --> 00:09:29.759 is from sources and from people that are in the know. 138 00:09:30.200 --> 00:09:32.200 So I just want to make clear that I don't 139 00:09:32.200 --> 00:09:35.240 have the receipts, but this is what people who were 140 00:09:35.240 --> 00:09:38.440 involved in this have to say, including somebody that was 141 00:09:38.639 --> 00:09:42.799 very very very high up in Israeli intelligence who gave 142 00:09:42.960 --> 00:09:46.919 a deposition or an Affidavid I should say, talking about 143 00:09:46.919 --> 00:09:49.320 this very thing. So I'm going to say allegedly here, 144 00:09:49.720 --> 00:09:52.039 and then I'm going to continue reading through the piece 145 00:09:52.480 --> 00:09:56.600 without saying allegedly. The story of Promise takes on an 146 00:09:56.639 --> 00:10:00.720 even darker and more labyrinthine dimension once israe Uel's Massad 147 00:10:00.840 --> 00:10:03.879 and the enigmatic figure of Robert Maxwell entered the frame. 148 00:10:04.320 --> 00:10:07.159 By the late eighties, rumors were already swirling that the 149 00:10:07.200 --> 00:10:11.159 software had already been spirited away from its American origins 150 00:10:11.320 --> 00:10:15.360 and modified abroad. In this version of events, Massad seized 151 00:10:15.440 --> 00:10:19.480 upon Promises cross reverencing power and inserted a hidden back door, 152 00:10:19.759 --> 00:10:22.720 turning the program into a weapon of surveillance far beyond 153 00:10:22.759 --> 00:10:27.720 anything Bill Hamilton of In's Law could have imagined. Robert Maxwell, 154 00:10:27.799 --> 00:10:32.360 the flamboyant and controversial British media mogul, soon emerged as 155 00:10:32.360 --> 00:10:35.759 a key character in this narrative. Known for his towering 156 00:10:35.799 --> 00:10:40.240 presence in Fleet Street and his tangled business empire, Maxwell 157 00:10:40.879 --> 00:10:45.639 was long suspected of intelligence ties. Some researchers insist he 158 00:10:45.799 --> 00:10:48.840 was not only an occasional asset, but a committed operative 159 00:10:48.840 --> 00:10:52.960 for the Masad, leveraging his media holdings and political influence 160 00:10:53.320 --> 00:10:57.879 to advance Israel's interests. When Promise entered the picture, Maxwell 161 00:10:58.279 --> 00:11:02.799 was perfectly positioned to become its global salesman. Robert Maxwell, publisher, 162 00:11:02.879 --> 00:11:06.440 deal maker and long time Israeli asset, was perfectly placed 163 00:11:06.480 --> 00:11:09.600 to become the salesman for this new version. He had 164 00:11:09.639 --> 00:11:14.240 deep relationships with governments, parliament, and security services. He could 165 00:11:14.240 --> 00:11:18.279 show up in Eastern Europe, Africa, or Latin America offering 166 00:11:18.320 --> 00:11:23.759 advanced Western software package without drawing suspicion. Behind the glossy brochures, 167 00:11:24.039 --> 00:11:27.519 Maxwell was acting as a MASAD cutout, seating a compromise 168 00:11:27.600 --> 00:11:31.840 program into the very infrastructure of other states. Instead of breaking into embassies or bribing informants, 170 00:11:36.600 --> 00:11:39.960 Massad can now simply watch through the back door of promise. 171 00:11:40.559 --> 00:11:45.600 Court systems, intelligence databases, financial transaction hubs. scored.description:
Dive into the wild world of Laura Cain After Dark...your new favorite escape....hosted by 
Laura Cain and Erik Rimmer...Best friends and complete opposites which creates an engaging, sometimes shocking, hilarious atmosphere. 
Expect awkward confessions, crazy true stories, unedited, unscripted, unapologetic conversations, and the latest showbiz gossip. 

We love interviewing people from all walks of life and introducing you to new products, games, and TV shows.   This is a perfect escape from your daily stress...as raw as it gets, sometimes a bit raunchy, always funny and bursting with heart.  Make this podcast your go-to happy place.  We are family.
Love your podcast!


Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/laura-cain-after-dark--4162487/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Flat Out Funny

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a comedy/entertainment podcast featuring banter, slang explanations, and party games with no connection to news or political content.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a loose, conversational flow moving between slang quizzes and a 'What's Your Price' game segment, but transitions are casual and pacing is uneven.

clipAbility.rationale: A few quotable one-liners exist but most humor depends on the back-and-forth dynamic and accumulated context of the conversation to land.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Our Unbelievable Disneyland Story

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a celebrity gossip and lifestyle episode centered on Taylor Swift's wedding and a Disneyland story, with no substantive connection to news or politics.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode meanders loosely from celebrity wedding gossip to personal anecdotes to a theater plug, with no clear segmentation or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: A few quotable asides exist (the prenup story, the COVID wave comment) but most moments require surrounding conversational context to land.

scored.description:
Rattling the Bars puts the voices of the people most harmed by our system of mass incarceration at the center of our reporting on the fight to end it. The show was founded by the late Black Panther and political prisoner Marshall “Eddie” Conway, and is now hosted by Charles Hopkins, better known as Mansa Musa, who himself spent 48 years behind bars.

Rattling the Bars offers an honest look at the lives of prisoners, returning citizens, their families, and their communities. With Rattling the Bars, by presenting hard data and real-life stories, we examine and seek to shift public opinion around the misconception that incarceration, punishment, and increased policing make cities safer—the truth of which has been disproven by countless studies. The series examines the history and root causes of the current so-called justice system. It showcases individuals and communities nationwide who are grappling with real solutions to problems created by the prison-industrial complex.

Help us continue producing Rattling the Bars by following us and becoming a monthly sustainer.

Sign up for our newsletter

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Harsha Walia: Abolishing ICE Is the Floor, Not the Ceiling

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages with current ICE enforcement, Trump immigration policy, and the active political debate over abolition versus reform — timely, substantive, and politically specific.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation follows a clear interview throughline with identifiable topic shifts, but pacing is uneven and the host's questions are sometimes rambling and hard to follow.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments — particularly the 'bad ICE, good cop' binary critique and 'migration as reparations' framing — would work as standalone previews with minimal context.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Rebels with a Cause: The Black Panther Party and Socialism in Practice

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with political organizing, state repression, and socialist community programs through the lens of Black Panther Party history, with explicit lessons drawn for current movement-building — timely given ongoing debates about radical left organizing strategies.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose but identifiable throughline — BPP legacy, lessons for youth, coalition-building — though transitions are conversational and meandering rather than deliberately signposted.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained moments work as standalone excerpts, particularly the 'develop your railroad first' organizing advice and the critique of ideological purity culture, though most require at least minimal context to land fully.

card.tagline: Why calling the cops to stop ICE is a trap, not a solution
card.description: Harsha Walia argues abolishing ICE means nothing if police power remains intact, and that 'good immigrant' framing divides rather than builds movements.
card.reason: Because this episode reframes the ICE debate at the center of today's immigration crisis

scored.feedTitle: AI Chat: AI News & Artificial Intelligence
scored.description: AI Chat is the podcast where we dive into the world of AI, cutting-edge AI news and its impact on our daily lives. With in-depth discussions and interviews with leading experts in the field, we'll explore the latest advancements in language models, machine learning, and more. From its practical applications to its ethical considerations, AI Chat will keep you informed and entertained on the exciting developments in the world of AI. Tune in to stay ahead of the curve on the latest technological revolution.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Kimi K3 Beats Anthropic, Apple Beats Nvidia

topicRelevance.rationale: This is an AI industry news and tech product episode — not a News/Politics show — and while it touches on regulatory angles (NY data center ban, government AI use), the core subject matter is AI model benchmarks, product launches, and corporate developments, not political coverage.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct stories (Kimi K3, Apple lawsuit, NY governor, NotebookLM) with a clear throughline, though transitions are conversational and loose rather than deliberately signposted.

clipAbility.rationale: The Kathy Hochul 'using AI while banning data centers' analogy and the Kimi K3 open-weight enterprise strategy explanation are both reasonably self-contained and quotable without heavy setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: OpenAI Shuts Down Atlas, Meta Launches MuseSpark

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode covers AI product launches, chip IPOs, and tech industry news — not News/Politics subject matter at its core, and the incidental references to Trump's data center comments or Howard Lutnick's negotiations don't make it primarily a political episode.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct story segments (MuseSpark launch, Sunrun distributed compute, SK Hynix IPO, OpenAI leadership change) with a clear close, though transitions between topics are abrupt rather than signposted.

clipAbility.rationale: The Zuckerberg-breaking-his-X-silence moment and the '70% of Americans oppose new data center construction' stat both work as near-standalone hooks, though most segments require at least minimal setup to land.

scored.description:
Want to make sense of the seismic shifts in American news, politics and entertainment? Larry O’Connor has a way of breaking down and explaining the most complicated topics as he delivers succinct, bite-sized summaries of the stories everybody’s talking about.
Always good-natured and never preachy or shouty, LARRY will get you up-to-speed on today’s hottest topics with common-sense, conservative and, yes, funny analysis.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: "They Were LYING to You": Rubio EXPOSES the Truth About ANTIFA.

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on a specific, timely political development — Secretary Rubio's speech at an international counter-terrorism summit designating Antifa as a transnational threat — with direct policy implications and current administration action.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline built around extended Rubio speech clips with host commentary interspersed, though pacing is uneven and a tangent about a Capitol tour with Speaker Johnson disrupts the main thread.

clipAbility.rationale: Rubio's quoted passages — particularly the communism critique and the transnational network description — are self-contained and quotable on their own, though O'Connor's commentary segments require more surrounding context to land.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The TRUTH about Charlie Kirk is now INDISPUTABLE

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on a politically motivated assassination trial — a specific, ongoing legal proceeding with direct political dimensions including motive, media coverage, and conservative media conspiracy theories.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline — walking through trial evidence and rebutting conspiracy claims — but shifts between reading text messages, editorializing, and addressing Candace Owens without tight signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: The 'Data Republican open letter' segment is a dense, self-contained recitation of specific evidence points that would work as a standalone clip, and O'Connor's media-vacuum argument is quotable on its own.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Freedom Is Disorganized by Richard Greaser (Bonus Episode)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is a personal philosophical monologue about Bitcoin conviction, community identity, and resilience — not a news or politics episode despite loose references to the 2008 financial crisis and freedom rhetoric.

contentStructure.rationale: The excerpt shows a stream-of-consciousness wind-down with no clear segmentation, closing into an original song outro rather than a structured argument or organized segments.

clipAbility.rationale: The rambling, context-dependent reflection on Bitcoin community psychology and the extended song outro produce no self-contained, hook-worthy moments usable as a standalone preview.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Kayfabians | Bugle Weekly Episode 116

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is primarily Bitcoin/crypto community commentary, gaming references, and casual banter, with only a brief passing mention of Mitch McConnell as a tangential aside — not substantive news or political coverage.

contentStructure.rationale: The excerpt reads as loose, meandering conversation moving between soccer, Bitcoin node debates, listener boosts, and gaming references with no clear throughline or deliberate segmentation.

clipAbility.rationale: Nearly every moment requires familiarity with the show's in-group references — 'kayfabe,' 'plebs,' 'Orange Mart,' 'white goy summer' — making standalone clips largely inaccessible to outside listeners.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Israel Is Killing Gaza’s Most Famous Doctor. Time Is Running Out to Save Him.

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is sharply focused on a specific, urgent, ongoing political and humanitarian crisis — the Israeli detention and alleged torture of a named Gazan doctor — with direct references to international law, U.S. elected officials, and active legal proceedings, making it highly timely and substantive for a News/Politics audience.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a clear throughline and identifiable interview structure with a host and multiple guests, but the transcript shows some meandering and repetition in the advocacy sections rather than tight, deliberate segmentation.

clipAbility.rationale: Several passages — particularly the lawyer's testimony of unrecognizable physical condition and the doctor's reported final words 'I don't think I'll survive this' — are emotionally self-contained and would function as powerful standalone previews with minimal context.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Brazil’s Battle Against Fake News & the US War to Undermine it

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly addresses a live geopolitical conflict — Trump's 2026 tariffs on Brazil justified as a free-speech intervention — connecting current US foreign policy to Brazil's disinformation laws and electoral history.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a clear narrative arc with a deliberate time-machine flashback device moving from 2018 to 2022 to the present, though pacing is occasionally interrupted by tangential asides like the heavy metal digression.

clipAbility.rationale: The street artist quote about fake news deciding the election and the explanation of Brazil's democratic fragility relative to its 1985 return from dictatorship are genuinely self-contained, quotable moments that work without heavy setup.

card.tagline: Gaza's most famous doctor is being tortured to death. Can anyone stop it?
card.description: Doctors Against Genocide sound the alarm on Dr. Abu Sophia's detention: 560 days of alleged torture, a lawyer who couldn't recognize him, and a Supreme Court that won't act.
card.reason: Because this episode covers an urgent, ongoing humanitarian and political crisis in real time

scored.description: J.J. Carrell is a speaker and the author of INVADED: The International Destruction of the American Immigration System. Carrell uses his 24-year career in the United States Border Patrol as a Journeyman Agent to a Deputy Patrol Agent in Charge to expose the who, why and how this treason is being perpetrated against our great nation. Carrell has been a guest on several national syndicated radio shows and television networks as he has become the expert on border issues and the criminal acts by Biden, his administration, and the DHS machine. Carrell is unafraid to speak the truth and he is unapologetically patriotic.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: HUGE MISTAKE: ICE STAND DOWN

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on current ICE enforcement operations, the alleged stand-down order, deportation numbers under Trump, and a specific Minneapolis-linked government shutdown — all timely, specific immigration policy developments.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves between clips, commentary, and tangents without clear signposting — transitions are abrupt and the throughline is driven by the host's emotional register rather than deliberate structural organization.

clipAbility.rationale: Several punchy, self-contained declarations — particularly the 'we're never going to right this ship' and 'there's an effing stand-down' passages — work as standalone moments, though many require surrounding context to land fully.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Ukraine’s Endless Bloodbath: Corruption Fuels This Senseless War with J.J. Carrell

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages a live geopolitical conflict, U.S. foreign aid policy, and Trump's stated peace commitments — substantive political content with timely hooks, though framed as opinion commentary rather than hard news reporting.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through clips and commentary but lacks clear segmentation — transitions between Zelensky footage, McConnell quotes, and Trump audio feel reactive rather than deliberately structured.

clipAbility.rationale: Several punchy standalone moments exist — the '24-hour war, 18 months later' line and the 'they just say it out loud' defense contractor riff would work as self-contained previews without much setup.

card.tagline: Former border agent says ICE is in a full stand-down — here's why
card.description: J.J. Carrell argues that ICE enforcement has quietly collapsed, blaming funding freezes, Minneapolis fallout, and a lack of political will for mass deportations.

scored.description:
"Amy & T.J." is hosted by renowned television news anchors Amy Robach and T. J. Holmes.
Hosts and executive producers Robach and Holmes are a formidable broadcasting team with decades of experience delivering headline news and captivating viewers nationwide. Now, the duo will get behind the microphone to explore meaningful conversations about current events, pop culture and everything in between. Nothing is off limits. “Amy & T.J.” is guaranteed to be informative, entertaining and above all, authentic. It marks the first time Robach and Holmes speak publicly since their own names became a part of the headlines. 
Follow @ajrobach, and @officialtjholmes on Instagram for updates.

card.description: Chris Fabry gathers veteran Moody Radio broadcasters to share behind-the-scenes lessons and standout memories from decades of Christian radio ministry.
card.reason: Because you enjoy long-form conversations about faith and media history

scored.feedTitle: Check the FAQ w/ Qui Talks
scored.description:
Check the FAQ w/ Qui Talks is where fashion, politics, culture, and community collide. Hosted by Quisha “Qui Talks” Joacin, the show explores how clothing, identity, and power shape our world — from pop culture and red carpets to local issues and global news. Every episode blends real conversation, style analysis, and current events, giving listeners a unique mix of entertainment and education. Whether it’s decoding the politics of fashion, highlighting local changemakers, or breaking down headlines in real language, Check the FAQ w/ Qui Talks delivers stories with heart, humor, and honesty.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/check-the-faq-w-qui-talks--2910746/support.

transcript.scoringWindow: Hay Talkers. Welcome to check the backs of CUE Talks, where we talk about style, influence, and the voices shaping our communities. Today, we are joined by Patricia Poyo, a Haitian, registered dietitian and CEO of Pathway Nutrition. She helps busy professionals of color align their values with their lives while still enjoying their cultural foods like myself, because help shouldn't mean giving up the life you love. Patricia, Welcome to the show. Thank you so much for having me. I'm so excited to chat with you today, of course, and my co hosts and your co hosts, guys. So let's start with your journey. What inspired you to become a registered dietitian. Well, that's a long journey. It's a funny story because I kind of fell into the world of nutrition. I didn't know that was. A career choice because before I studied social work and I got my bachelors in social work, and then I took a pause before figuring out what I wanted to do with my life. Okay, and then I was living in the Flought at the time, and I was I had a friend who was like, oh, let's after the holiday let's do a diet, you know. Let's so that told me on the journey to like really figure out what does eating healthy look like? And then that just kind of snowball into me falling finding out about nutrition, doing it as a career, and becoming dietician. So yeah, well, first thing first, after the holidays, that is exactly when everyone wants to go on diet exactly, and that's when I indulge. I'm like all the time I restrict the food. Today I'm eating whatever I see in front of me, exactly Like. I remember that time we had gone to so many parties, we enjoyed ourselves, and that was the first time I've ever even tried the diet. It lasted a day and a half and I was like, no, I don't want this. Yeah, it doesn't work for me, even especially so now knowing especially your background similar to mine with us being Haitian, so Caribbean lifestyle Dietitian, what does. That mean in your practice? It means we don't have to give up our culture or our foods that we grew up eating to be healthy, but also the lifestyle. When I think about lifestyle. When I was back home in Haiti. I mean, I was enjoying life. I was sitting under the mango trees. You know, I wasn't like stressed out because many of us. I miss home already me. So I tried to incorporate all of those things to really encourage people to prioritize their wellbeing, prioritize their joy, their lifestyle, their health. So that way, you know, they can be as healthy and be there for the things that they working so hard to create. Right. So well, yeah, especially being in a Haitian household. Diets mean what I've told my mom numerous times, No, I can't eat that. I'm on a diet. She's like, eat this food. Diets don't work. You only need to do that. I agree with her. A lot of people say they have to give up their cultural foods. Oh why is it that? What do you see that is. I guess it depends on the individual and what their goals or what their health goals are. Right, But in general, if you're thinking about eating healthy, I typically focus on making sure that we are plates of balance, that we get all the main food groups like your protein, your fiber, and your starches, your carbs, they're all get it right. And now, depending on your health conditions, your portions may need to be adjusted a little bit, right, And we know a healthy diet, regardless of the culture, we don't want things high in salt. We don't want things high in like unhealthy fats. Right, So when people say, oh, Caribbean foods are unhealthy, it's because we often look at the foods that are like more fried and high in salt, and regardless of the culture, we don't want to have an excess amount of those things. So in yeah, if you're typically cooking where you're frying things a lot or using a lot of different type of reusing the oils, or I'm using a lot of like salt, those are things that we should all be mindful of and reduced. But the key is you can still use the same ingredients and make a plate balance, like whether you're using the plant the planting or like you know, your rice, your portions just have to like fit into that plate the way it's supposed to and then it's gonna be healthy. So give me like a recipe that is in the Haitian culture that you would consider healthy. It's all healthy, that's the thing. All the foods are healthy. scored.description: Stay updated with the latest in the world of music through “Music News Radio.” Our podcast brings you the hottest news, trends, and updates from trusted music sources like Billboard, NME, Rolling Stone, and Pitchfork, all transformed into engaging audio with the help of AI technology. Whether you’re a casual listener or a devoted fan, you’ll find something fresh and exciting in every episode. Please note that while we reference these reputable outlets, this podcast is independently produced and not officially affiliated with them. Subscribe now to stay in tune with the music industry!

scored.description:
Justin Barclay hosts a one hour daily show called West Michigan Live with JustinBarclay on WOOD Radio in Grand Rapids, Michigan. He talks about what matters most in West Michigan and around the world.

This stream also includes the After Show which is the show after the show. That's why we call it The "After" Show.

Justin's weekly podcast and bonus content.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: BOMBSHELL TRUMP REVEALS DECLASSIFIED INTEL SHOWING CHINA ATTACKING ELECTIONS.. AND THE CIA KNEW

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on a specific, timely political event — Trump's declassified intelligence address on Chinese election interference — with detailed discussion of policy responses including the Save America Act and voting machine security.

contentStructure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline moving from the intelligence reveal to Michigan-specific evidence to proposed solutions, but the pacing is loose, with tangents to live comments and meandering transitions between speakers.

clipAbility.rationale: The passage explaining how compromising Michigan's voter registration database cascades to compromise the entire election system is a genuinely self-contained, quotable argument that would work as a standalone preview.

card.tagline: Trump's declassified intel says China had access to your voter file
card.description: A Michigan election investigator breaks down how Chinese access to voter registration databases may have compromised tabulation machines and poll books in 2020.
card.reason: Because this episode covers a breaking presidential address on foreign election interference

scored.feedTitle: Your News Talk America, with Jake Smith
scored.description:
Your News Talk America with Jake Smith is a daily national politics and news commentary program featuring in-depth analysis of the stories shaping America. Hosted by broadcaster Jake Smith, the show includes political interviews, policy discussion, and media analysis from voices influencing the national conversation.


The program airs weekdays from 9 a.m. to noon Eastern on WGMD 92.7 FM and WUSX 98.5 FM. This podcast focuses on national issues and is separate from the local community coverage featured on WGMD.com.


Listeners can interact live with Jake and other audience members in the Digital Interactive Audience Lounge (DIAL) Monday through Thursday by listening, chatting, and submitting questions. On Fridays, the program is also available as a live video broadcast.


Visit RealNewsTalk.com for live participation, Friday video, show notes, and additional resources.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Trump Election Evidence, ICE Pursuits Restart, Iran Escalation | Your News Talk America | July 17, 2026

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers genuinely current political topics — election integrity, ICE policy, Iran escalation — but the excerpt leans heavily on unverified claims and conspiratorial framing rather than substantive policy analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves between guest interview, listener-producer questions, and tangential historical commentary (Woodrow Wilson) without tight transitions or clear segment signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context or are embedded in back-and-forth crosstalk; the few standalone assertions are too assertion-heavy and unsubstantiated to function as compelling standalone previews.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Iran Escalation, Trump Air Force One Security, Reading Crisis in America | Your News Talk America | July 9, 2026

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly covers active U.S. military strikes on Iran, Strait of Hormuz disruption, Trump Air Force One security, and a domestic reading crisis — all timely, specific news/politics topics anchored to July 2026 events.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through distinct segments with identifiable guest interviews and topic transitions, but pacing is uneven and some segments run long without tight signposting.

clipAbility.rationale: The energy expert's 'build non-commoditized sources at home, export the commodity' thesis and the Strait of Hormuz standstill framing offer a few self-contained, quotable moments, though most require surrounding context to land fully.

scored.description:
A community interview series focused on Calhoun County, Michigan, featuring voices from Battle Creek, Marshall, Albion and all around the county. Join host Richard Piet to discuss local events, non-profits, local schools, government and community leaders.


Underwritten by Lakeview Ford-Lincoln, Community Matters also airs as a radio program Saturday mornings on 95.3 FM in Battle Creek.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: (Community Matters 197) First Home First Teacher Brings Stability To Early Educators

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode touches on political will, state funding decisions, and childcare policy in Michigan, but is primarily a local community program spotlight rather than substantive news or political coverage.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation follows a clear throughline from problem (teacher pay, housing instability) to solution (housing trust pilot), with identifiable segments, though pacing is conversational and loose in places.

clipAbility.rationale: The 'childcare as economic infrastructure, like roads and hospitals' passage is quotable, but most moments require the surrounding interview context to land fully.

contentStructure.rationale: The interview follows a clear conversational throughline moving from the flag football event to the Pistons Academy clinic, with identifiable topic transitions and a functional close.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context to land, though the passage about kids fighting less after the Lions camp and the 'bringing Detroit life to Battle Creek' line offer limited standalone potential.

clip.text: root problem is, which is we don't pay our teachers enough. 00:20:44.480 --> 00:20:45.039 Correct. 00:20:45.359 --> 00:20:46.880 So, what has to change? 00:20:46.880 --> 00:20:48.160 How do we do that? 00:20:48.480 --> 00:20:56.160 I think you have to have the political and public will to understand the shared benefit of this sector, right? 00:20:56.160 --> 00:21:00.400 Like people can't go to work if they don't have childcare. 00:21:00.400 --> 00:21:06.400 And we want childcare that's safe, that's quality, where these children have the ability to thrive, right? 00:21:06.400 --> 00:21:09.200 Like, because that actually is our future. 00:21:09.200 --> 00:21:11.440 We know the return on investment here, right? 00:21:11.440

transcript.scoringWindow: problem is, which is we don't pay our teachers enough. 00:20:44.480 --> 00:20:45.039 Correct. 00:20:45.359 --> 00:20:46.880 So, what has to change? 00:20:46.880 --> 00:20:48.160 How do we do that? 00:20:48.480 --> 00:20:56.160 I think you have to have the political and public will to understand the shared benefit of this sector, right? 00:20:56.160 --> 00:21:00.400 Like people can't go to work if they don't have childcare. 00:21:00.400 --> 00:21:06.400 And we want childcare that's safe, that's quality, where these children have the ability to thrive, right? 00:21:06.400 --> 00:21:09.200 Like, because that actually is our future. 00:21:09.200 --> 00:21:11.440 We know the return on investment here, right? 00:21:11.440 --> 00:21:19.599 If we can get to kids those first five years, build a really strong foundation, their ability to thrive later it has increased. 00:21:19.599 --> 00:21:24.960 We are decreasing the amount of interventions that are required in elementary school, which are costly. 00:21:24.960 --> 00:21:36.240 We're decreasing the amount of interventions from a health perspective, because when you have a stronger foundation, your chances of making really good decisions in your life that keep you healthy are better. 00:21:36.240 --> 00:21:39.039 I mean, we have all the data that suggests this. 00:21:39.039 --> 00:21:55.920 So if we can get our political and public will to align with what the research tells us, to align with what the economists are telling us and what, you know, not just people who are like, this is nice to do because it's the right thing to do, but this is actually the right thing for our economy. 00:21:55.920 --> 00:21:59.759 When we have people, we work with employers all over the state, Richard. 00:21:59.759 --> 00:22:10.720 Who have are investing in childcare for their workforce and realizing that they also need to invest in the ecosystem because there's just not enough of it out there and they understand why now, right? 00:22:10.720 --> 00:22:22.079 And they're like, they over and over again will tell us when we provide this benefit, even those who don't have children benefit because they have more stable uh teammates. 00:22:22.079 --> 00:22:30.880 They can come to work and know that somebody's there with some confidence and some feeling of safety that their children are taken care of so that they can be there and be fully present. 00:22:30.880 --> 00:22:33.119 And so everybody benefits when this happens. 00:22:33.119 --> 00:22:38.640 So if we have business owners who who get it and invest, and we have to find ways for them to invest, right? 00:22:38.640 --> 00:22:40.160 We can't just say give us money. 00:22:40.160 --> 00:22:41.759 There's gotta be a way to do that. 00:22:41.759 --> 00:22:43.680 So we're working on that right now. 00:22:43.680 --> 00:22:50.640 We need to have political leaders with the mindset that they understand that this is economic infrastructure. 00:22:50.640 --> 00:22:54.880 It is public infrastructure, just like our roads and our hospitals and our schools. 00:22:54.880 --> 00:22:58.720 And if we can look view it that way, then we need to fund it that way. 00:22:58.720 --> 00:23:06.799 So, how are we looking at how we're building budgets and ensuring that more money is flowing to the sector so that it can stabilize? 00:23:06.799 --> 00:23:15.759 Because we will lose folks, and we've got some political decisions being made in the state right now that are that will lead to us losing more childcare businesses and we already don't have enough. 00:23:15.759 --> 00:23:18.000 And if you're not in this space, it's really hard. 00:23:18.000 --> 00:23:21.440 You just it's like a blip on the radio, you might hear, and you're like, well, that's nice. 00:23:21.440 --> 00:23:36.240 But if you go from attendance-based reimbursement from the state to enrollment-based, so think about this childcare businesses have to report attendance daily to get reimbursed by the state if they get subsidies. 00:23:36.240 --> 00:23:39.759 If I'm a public school, I have two count days a year. 00:23:39.759 --> 00:23:44.000 That's the only time I'm required to really state what my attendance is. 00:23:44.000 --> 00:23:45.200 It's not daily. 00:23:45.200 --> 00:23:49.519 And so you're putting this burden on an already overburdened system. 00:23:49.519 --> 00:23:50.880 And for what reason? 00:23:50.880 --> 00:23:52.400 It's it's really challenging. 00:23:52.400 --> 00:23:56.480 So we have to understand the landscape here and what people are working with. 00:23:56.480 --> 00:24:03.440 And we have to know that our political decisions have implications that can be really detrimental because there'll be a ripple effect. 00:24:03.440 --> 00:24:05.839 There'll be people who can't go to work because they can't find care. 00:24:06.240 --> 00:24:07.920 That happens, I'm quite sure. 00:24:07.920 --> 00:24:10.000 And you said you have to be loud. 00:24:10.000 --> 00:24:11.200 Think about that. 00:24:11.200 --> 00:24:15.359 In the political landscape now, there's a lot of other noise. 00:24:15.359 --> 00:24:28.559 And in the Michigan legislature, you have elected officials who are not soon, not long, I should say, after they arrive in Lansing, are readying to run again. 00:24:28.559 --> 00:24:30.480 So that's a distraction. 00:24:30.480 --> 00:24:32.319 There's a lot of noise. 00:24:32.319 --> 00:24:34.319 So you're gonna have to be pretty loud. 00:24:34.319 --> 00:24:39.440 I guess we could spend the whole rest of the show talking about that that angle of it, couldn't we? 00:24:39.440 --> 00:24:41.519 But you get the picture, right? 00:24:41.519 --> 00:24:49.359 So $28,000 to $35,000 a year does not make it easy to keep housing. 00:24:49.359 --> 00:24:53.359 And I'm not telling people anything because uh they know it. 00:24:53.359 --> 00:24:59.279 Even if they make $135,000, the expenses have gone up, so and you see that. 00:24:59.279 --> 00:25:02.240 So, Kathy, can you do more of this? 00:25:02.240 --> 00:25:03.519 Is that the goal here? 00:25:03.519 --> 00:25:08.960 Find more properties and try to put your qualifiers in them. 00:25:09.359 --> 00:25:10.240 Thank you for asking. 00:25:10.240 --> 00:25:30.400 We are actually meeting with attorneys in two weeks to set up the first home for seacher housing trust, where we will have a governing board once this trust is in place that is positioned to attract and retain additional properties that would then become owned by that trust. 00:25:30.400 --> 00:25:37.599 So it would be a community asset that would help us generate this kind of asset building that we need. 00:25:37.599 --> 00:25:40.960 The houses would then again have a governing board. 00:25:40.960 --> 00:25:44.960 We would have to put in the infrastructure for maintenance, right? 00:25:44.960 --> 00:25:47.359 So who would be our partner around that? 00:25:47.359 --> 00:25:50.000 So all of those things are still getting worked out right now. 00:25:50.000 --> 00:25:56.160 And so we're pretty excited that we've found consultants who are willing to work with us to set up this trust. 00:25:56.160 --> 00:25:58.880 We found attorneys who will work pro bono for us. 00:25:58.880 --> 00:26:02.160 I mean, we really, my partner likes to call it stone soup. 00:26:02.160 --> 00:26:08.640 We are finding who has what and what are they bringing to the table because we're gonna use it to make this happen. 00:26:08.640 --> 00:26:12.720 Ultimately, we see this as a 20-year vision, right? 00:26:12.720 --> 00:26:18.400 In 20 years, we would love to have enough housing to have 30% of our workforce housed. 00:26:18.400 --> 00:26:26.240 Now that is a very lofty goal, but it you need to have a lofty goal to actually make progress. 00:26:26.240 --> 00:26:28.799 And so we're gonna start with what we have. 00:26:28.799 --> 00:26:32.720 The way this pilot house was set up is different intentionally, right? 00:26:32.720 --> 00:26:36.160 It will re the ownership will be retained by neighborhoods incorporated. 00:26:36.160 --> 00:26:40.160 But that worked for us to be able to show a proof of concept. 00:26:40.160 --> 00:26:44.880 People can go there, they can see it, they can read the articles, they can listen to your podcast. 00:26:44.880 --> 00:26:46.960 They're gonna say, Oh, now I get it. 00:26:46.960 --> 00:26:52.799 Before it was just like some one pager, one-dimensional thing that, like, I don't understand how any of this is gonna work. 00:26:52.799 --> 00:27:02.400 Yeah, and so our goal also is to really these two, our first two um residents are gonna help us understand does this work? 00:27:02.400 --> 00:27:03.920 Are these right? 00:27:03.920 --> 00:27:06.160 Like, tell us what goes on, be honest with us. 00:27:06.160 --> 00:27:10.960 And the two women that we have living in there, well, I don't have any problem being honest with us. 00:27:10.960 --> 00:27:18.160 And so we will make sure that we're closely following what's going on in their lives so that we can then tell the story. 00:27:18.160 --> 00:27:24.400 I used to work at the community foundation and we would have folks who are trying to develop their own trusts and their own estate planning. 00:27:24.400 --> 00:27:29.599 And they're like, I have a house, I don't have any kids, I don't know where it's gonna go when I'm gone. 00:27:29.599 --> 00:27:31.920 I've owned it's been in my family for three generations. 00:27:31.920 --> 00:27:33.920 I'd love for it to be used for something good. 00:27:33.920 --> 00:27:36.640 This is the perfect use for something like this, right? 00:27:36.640 --> 00:27:39.599 So I do want to mention that we've had some early investors. 00:27:39.599 --> 00:27:44.799 Lisc, Kalamazoo, has invested um, as well as the Battle Creek Community Foundation. 00:27:44.799 --> 00:27:48.880 And then United Way bought all new appliances for the this duplex. 00:27:48.880 --> 00:27:55.440 Like people showed up in big ways for us in this because they saw the value in it and they said we want to be a part of it. 00:27:55.440 --> 00:28:03.039 The Battle Creek Community Foundation, special shout out to them because they actually invested for 10 years, $15,000 a year. 00:28:03.039 --> 00:28:10.480 Not a ton of money, but enough for us to have a little bit in the bank to be able to do the things that we need to do to keep things moving forward. 00:28:10.480 --> 00:28:13.440 And that tells you something about how much they believe in it. 00:28:13.440 --> 00:28:17.519 Their board was willing to say, for 10 years, we want to be a part of this. 00:28:17.519 --> 00:28:20.240 So we're really proud of the community for stepping up. 00:28:20.240 --> 00:28:24.720 Our goal is to have additional properties, and I don't even profess to know how they'll come to us. 00:28:24.720 --> 00:28:30.559 Maybe somebody's gonna say, Hey, I've got an apartment building with 10 units, and we'd love to see it used for this purpose. 00:28:30.559 --> 00:28:31.519 Who knows? 00:28:31.519 --> 00:28:32.079 Right? 00:28:32.079 --> 00:28:33.599 Like anything is possible. 00:28:33.599 --> 00:28:37.359 I I gave up on assumptions a long time ago. 00:28:37.759 --> 00:28:38.240 Yeah. 00:28:38.240 --> 00:28:42.720 Well, you just put it out there, so uh it may come back to you before you know it. 00:28:42.720 --> 00:28:45.519 I presume the trust example is the New Haven example. 00:28:45.519 --> 00:28:46.400 That's what they've done there. 00:28:46.400 --> 00:28:47.599 They've created the trust. 00:28:47.920 --> 00:28:50.559 They own all their properties as a business. 00:28:50.559 --> 00:28:57.200 I don't, I'm not even gonna pretend to know, but they are a single business that is doing this, and so their setup is very different. 00:28:57.200 --> 00:29:04.319 What they suggested to us was a trust, just so that it would be owned by the community, since we're doing a sector-wide approach. 00:29:04.319 --> 00:29:07.759 They're following us closely because they're learning from us as well. 00:29:08.000 --> 00:29:08.640 Yeah. 00:29:08.640 --> 00:29:17.359 So somebody's listening to this, and uh maybe they don't have a 10-unit apartment building, but they want to they want to help you somehow. 00:29:17.359 --> 00:29:18.960 What should they do? 00:29:19.279 --> 00:29:22.480 Yeah, well, they can reach out
