card.tagline: Are socialists winning the voters Republicans abandoned? Jeff Dornik weighs in.
card.description: Jeff Dornik joins Kim Kennedy to discuss transgender policy at women's colleges, Trump assassination theories, and the political realignment leaving GOP voters behind.
card.reason: Because this episode covers conservative takes on gender policy and political realignment

scored.description:
Your go-to source for UFO and UAP news. No fluff. Just the latest information.

Five episodes a week covering Pentagon reports, congressional testimonies, whistleblower revelations, leaked footage, and the latest developments in government disclosure with host Cristina Gomez. 

Visit UFONews.co for articles, archives, and extra content.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/strange-and-unexplained--5235662/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: THE UFO ROLLOUT IS NOT WHAT IT LOOKS LIKE

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is substantively about a specific, timely government UAP disclosure process — including named officials, White House coordination, and a specific media figure (Dr. Phil) being used as an administration messenger — which gives it a genuine news/politics hook, though the UFO subject matter keeps it from the top tier.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear investigative throughline — establishing Dr. Phil's background, his White House ties, the disclosure mechanics, and ending with a pointed rhetorical question — with identifiable segments and a clean close.

clipAbility.rationale: The closing line — 'Did the management of the subject end or did it just get better production value?' — is a strong standalone hook, and the framing of Trump routing UAP files to a talk show host with a handwritten note is immediately graspable without setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: UFO DISCLOSURE IS NOT ABOUT THE TRUTH

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is substantively tied to current political developments — Trump's anticipated UFO speech, congressional hearings with David Grusch, and France's parliamentary UAP forum — making it genuinely timely news/politics-adjacent content, though the core subject matter is paranormal rather than strictly political.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves with a clear throughline from disinformation claims to comparative US/France disclosure approaches to polling data, with identifiable segments and guest voices, though transitions occasionally meander into tangents.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained, quotable moments land well — particularly the 'staged disclosure' framing from the political scientist and the closing argument about state control versus public right to know — usable without heavy setup.

latestEpisode.title: THE UFO ROLLOUT IS NOT WHAT IT LOOKS LIKE

scored.description:
NaturalQueen77 TV is a true crime podcast dedicated to the cases the mainstream forgot. While the biggest names in crime get documentaries and bestsellers, countless victims and unsolved mysteries slip through the cracks. This show pulls them back into the light.

Each episode dives deep into lesser-known murders, disappearances, and cold cases — examining the evidence, the suspects, and the systemic failures that left these cases gathering dust. And when a well-known case has more to the story than people think, we cover that too.

From small-town tragedies to overlooked urban cases to infamous crimes that still have unanswered questions, NaturalQueen77 TV tells the stories that deserve airtime.
New episodes drop weekly. Subscribe and follow on social media to join the conversation.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained moments work as standalone hooks, particularly the flat description opening and the 'every single person made a small, understandable assumption' passage.

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a true crime/missing persons episode — well-produced and socially conscious, but not a News/Politics episode; the racial disparity angle is noted but does not transform the content into political or policy coverage.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a tight, deliberate arc — cold open, content warning, sourcing disclosure, chronological case narrative, resolution, and closing call to action — with clear signposting throughout.

clipAbility.rationale: The opening hook ('Tomorrow never came. Nine years later, her mother is still waiting') and the closing indictment of systemic neglect of missing Black women both function as strong standalone moments requiring minimal context.

For over two years, 10 00:00:59.320 --> 00:01:03.840 nobody knows. This is the story of Joy's Carol Vincent, 11 00:01:04.159 --> 00:01:07.120 a woman who was surrounded by people for most of 12 00:01:07.120 --> 00:01:14.560 her life, and who somehow impossibly died completely alone and 13 00:01:14.920 --> 00:01:19.879 stayed that way for longer than most of us can imagine. 14 00:01:20.359 --> 00:01:25.040 Joyce Carol Vincent was born in nineteen sixty five to 15 00:01:25.159 --> 00:01:30.480 Grenadian parents and grew up in London. She wasn't a recluse. 16 00:01:31.120 --> 00:01:33.959 Friends who knew her in the nineteen eighties and ninety 17 00:01:34.000 --> 00:01:39.200 as described a vibrant, glamorous woman, someone with a wide 18 00:01:39.280 --> 00:01:44.120 social circle, connections in the music industry, and what looked 19 00:01:44.799 --> 00:01:49.680 from the outside like a full life, but somewhere along 20 00:01:49.719 --> 00:01:54.400 the way that life started to come apart. Quietly, she 21 00:01:54.599 --> 00:02:00.319 moved through a series of jobs. Around two thousand and one, 22 00:02:00.959 --> 00:02:05.959 she spent time in a domestic violent shelter, and slowly 23 00:02:06.519 --> 00:02:09.639 the people who once knew her lost track of where 24 00:02:09.680 --> 00:02:14.439 she was and stopped asking. She reportedly struggled with asthma 25 00:02:14.919 --> 00:02:19.560 and a peptic ulcer. Nothing about her health explains what 26 00:02:19.719 --> 00:02:24.400 happened next. What explains it is something much harder to 27 00:02:24.520 --> 00:02:29.840 sit with, isolation so complete that her death produced no 28 00:02:30.039 --> 00:02:33.599 ripple at all. On the twenty fifth of January two 29 00:02:33.680 --> 00:02:38.439 thousand and six, housing officers forced open the door of 30 00:02:38.479 --> 00:02:43.479 a bed sit above wood Green shopping City in the 31 00:02:43.520 --> 00:02:48.319 Herringay borough of North London. They weren't looking for a body, 32 00:02:48.840 --> 00:02:53.960 they were dealing with unpaid rent. What they found was 33 00:02:54.280 --> 00:02:58.520 a skeleton, the remains of a thirty eight year old woman, 34 00:02:59.000 --> 00:03:03.919 later identified through dental records. Because so little else remained, 35 00:03:04.479 --> 00:03:10.360 the television was still switched on. Wrapped Christmas presents sat nearby, 36 00:03:10.960 --> 00:03:14.520 never opened. Based on the state of the post pile 37 00:03:14.639 --> 00:03:19.800 behind the door, investigators believe Joyce had been dead since 38 00:03:19.879 --> 00:03:23.879 December of two thousand and three, meaning her body lay 39 00:03:23.960 --> 00:03:29.759 in that flat, undiscovered for over two years. An inquest 40 00:03:30.319 --> 00:03:35.719 later returned an open verdict. There was no forced entry, 41 00:03:36.439 --> 00:03:40.479 no evidence of foul play, just an ordinary door in 42 00:03:40.560 --> 00:03:45.599 an ordinary building that nobody thought to check. Here's the 43 00:03:45.639 --> 00:03:49.840 part that makes this case different from almost every other 44 00:03:49.879 --> 00:03:53.400 one we cover on this show. There is no killer, 45 00:03:53.879 --> 00:03:59.159 no suspect, no trial. The real question isn't who did this, 46 00:03:59.599 --> 00:04:03.080 It's how does a person disappear from a crowded city 47 00:04:03.400 --> 00:04:07.240 and have literally no one notice. Neighbors in the building 48 00:04:07.319 --> 00:04:11.039 assumed the flat was simply empty, family didn't check in, 49 00:04:11.680 --> 00:04:16.120 friends assumed she'd moved on with her life. Every single 50 00:04:16.199 --> 00:04:22.360 person in Joyce's orbit made a small, understandable assumption, and 51 00:04:22.600 --> 00:04:26.839 every one of those assumptions was wrong. Joyce's story could 52 00:04:26.839 --> 00:04:30.399 have ended in that flat filed away as a housing 53 00:04:30.439 --> 00:04:36.360 department footnote. Instead, filmmaker Carol Morley learned of the case 54 00:04:36.480 --> 00:04:40.360 and spent years tracking down the people who once knew Joyce, 55 00:04:40.759 --> 00:04:45.959 old friends, former partners, co workers to try to understand 56 00:04:46.040 --> 00:04:49.759 who she really was. The result was the two thousand 57 00:04:49.839 --> 00:04:54.920 and eleven docu drama Dreams of a Life starring zaw Ashton, 58 00:04:55.399 --> 00:05:01.319 which blended real interviews with dramatized recreation. The film doesn't 59 00:05:01.319 --> 00:05:05.160 solve anything, because there's nothing to solve. What it does 60 00:05:05.480 --> 00:05:10.199 is force viewers to sit with an uncomfortable question, how 61 00:05:10.240 --> 00:05:13.319 well do we actually know the people around us? And 62 00:05:13.399 --> 00:05:16.959 how long would it take before someone noticed we were gone. 63 00:05:17.519 --> 00:05:22.399 Joyce's case has since echoed well beyond the documentary, inspiring 64 00:05:22.519 --> 00:05:27.279 music from artists including Stephen Wilson and Paloma Faith and 65 00:05:27.680 --> 00:05:34.240 prompting renewed conversation in the UK about isolation, welfare checks, 66 00:05:34.279 --> 00:05:37.759 and how easily people can fall through the cracks of 67 00:05:37.920 --> 00:05:43.120 systems built to catch them. There's no twist ending here, 68 00:05:43.600 --> 00:05:47.920 no arrest, no justice to deliver, just a woman named 69 00:05:48.000 --> 00:05:52.240 Joyce Carol Vincent, who lived a life full of people 70 00:05:52.759 --> 00:05:56.600 and died in a room that stayed silent for two 71 00:05:56.800 --> 00:06:01.040 and a half years. If this episode's sits heavy with you, 72 00:06:01.879 --> 00:06:05.160 let it and maybe let it do one more thing. 73 00:06:05.279 --> 00:06:09.120 Check on somebody today, a friend you haven't heard from 74 00:06:09.519 --> 00:06:14.399 in a while, someone who's gone quiet. It costs nothing, 75 00:06:14.879 --> 00:06:18.879 and for Joyce, it came far too late. Thanks for 76 00:06:18.959 --> 00:06:23.199 listening to Natural Queen seven seven TV. We'll see you 79 00:06:31.000 --> 00:06:31.560 next time.

scored.description: Blue Lightning AI Daily is your go-to AI podcast for creators, delivering fast, focused updates on the world of generative AI. We cover the latest breakthroughs in large language models (LLMs), AI video editing, AI photography, AI audio tools, and creative automation. Each episode gives digital creators, content marketers, and creative professionals clear insights into how AI is transforming storytelling, production, and the creator economy. Stay informed, stay creative, and stay ahead with daily AI news made simple.

scored.description:
The Karel Show delivers bold commentary from Karel, an openly gay entertainer, author, and nationally syndicated talk show host known for saying what others won’t. With humor, heart, and zero patience for nonsense, Karel takes listeners on a fast-paced, no-filter journey through politics, culture, and modern American life—hilarious, incisive, and always honest.

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-karel-show--1368295/support.

card.tagline: LGBTQ rights are under siege globally — and closer to home than you think
card.description: Karel and co survey anti-gay laws in Senegal and Trinidad, Trump's domestic rollbacks, and Hollywood's complicated relationship with queer visibility.
card.reason: Because this episode tracks the week's biggest LGBTQ rights flashpoints worldwide

scored.description: NBA Hall of Famer & 4x Champion, Shaquille O'Neal aka SHAQ, goes on the record from Shahookah Studios with co-host Adam Lefkoe to bring you nonstop laughter and unfiltered thoughts on sports, life, entertainment, and more. Along the way, they will be joined by a host of VIP guests for a high energy show you won't soon forget.New episodes every Thursday on YouTube at THE BIG PODCAST WITH SHAQ. This is a Playmaker & Big Podcast Network Exclusive.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Aries Spears Response to Why He Doesn't Want to Perform in the South and Has Shaq in TEARS

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a celebrity entertainment/comedy podcast episode featuring Shaq and comedian Aries Spears discussing stand-up performance preferences and a comedy film concept — no substantive news or political content.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation flows loosely as a casual celebrity chat with no clear segments or signposting, interrupted by mid-roll ad breaks that further fragment the episode.

clipAbility.rationale: The buffalo-sauce security guard bit and the 'comedy about slavery' reveal are genuinely self-contained, punchy moments that could work as standalone previews without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: BIll Burr & Shaq Talk CRAZIEST NBA Conspiracy Theories: Is the Lottery Rigged?!

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a sports entertainment podcast — NBA conspiracy theories, basketball rule debates, and celebrity banter — with no substantive connection to news or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation drifts loosely from Utah anecdotes to rule changes to lottery conspiracies to Bill Burr's writing career, with no clear segments or deliberate throughline.

clipAbility.rationale: Bill Burr's riff on the NBA being 'massaged' rather than fixed and Shaq's David Stern anecdote are quotable moments, but most require the surrounding conversation to land properly.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Alex Recites the Declaration of Independence , 250 Years After Washington Read In NYC - July 9, 2026

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is a verbatim recitation of a historical document framed around a 250th anniversary commemoration, giving it a loose civic/political hook but no substantive news analysis or current political coverage.

scored.feedTitle: Front Porch Radio - Southern Middle TN Today News with Tom Price
scored.description: Daily News from MuleTown to Music City and beyond. Listen to Tom Price read the news of the day from Kennedy Broadcasting, WKOM & WKRM Radio.

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers local government decisions (fire department staffing, county commission actions, state business filings) with some civic relevance, but is primarily a community bulletin board — local memorials, a restaurant opening, and human-interest profiles — rather than substantive news or political coverage.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear segmented format moving from county fire department coverage to civic appointments, human interest, local business, obituaries, and state news, with identifiable transitions between each section.

[Mom] just gave birth to life, you know?” she said. Vaccher’s EMT firefighting job is anything but easy, though. transcript.scoringWindow: bulk of the work. County Commissioner Gabe Howard recently noted that the department is also a standalone nonprofit, not an official county department, and maintains its own facilities. Staffing fluctuates throughout the year, and because volunteers tend to sign up at the station closest to them, the most rural areas struggle most to find firefighters. “[The shortage] is in areas that have a smaller population that don’t have as many people to pull from,” Schatz said. Meanwhile, volunteer numbers have remained flat. Schatz reported that rising living costs have forced volunteer firefighters and their spouses to take on multiple jobs, work outside the county, and give up discretionary driving — devouring the free time they would otherwise spend protecting their neighbors. “[In fact,] we’ve probably seen a slight decline over the years in volunteer activity levels,” he said. County Fire is working to address the problem through incentives and systemic changes. Schatz thanked the County Commission for providing county firefighters a uniform budget for the first time last year, in addition to protective equipment the commission has already purchased for them. Looking ahead, the Safety Committee heard Schatz’s July 7 request that the county purchase land for an additional fire station on Santa Fe Pike. Unlike the current facility, the proposed station would include dormitories, better truck access and indoor bathrooms. County Fire is also considering hiring more part-time and full-time staff, a significant shift for the historically volunteer department. “This Commission has been very supportive of the Maury County Fire Department, and we’ve made major strides in the last four years … [like] the hiring of our first six full-time firefighters [in October 2024],” Schatz said. “We’ve definitely improved coverage with some full-time staffing.” To volunteer with the Maury County Fire Department (MCFD), submit an interest form on the Maury County Fire Department website. The all-volunteer department offers an in-house Recruit Academy for beginners with no experience, and a Lateral Academy for certified firefighters. Both programs feature free training and certification. Patrick Carter elected chair of State Bar Foundation (RELEASE) Patrick Carter, an attorney with Wolaver, Carter & Hogan and the city attorney for both Fairview and Spring Hill, has been confirmed as chair-elect of the Board of Trustees of the Tennessee Bar Foundation, following the foundation’s annual meeting in Knoxville in June. Carter is active in numerous legal and civic organizations throughout Tennessee. His election to the foundation’s leadership reflects his longstanding commitment to the legal profession and to public service. He will serve a one-year term as chair-elect before assuming the role of chair of the Foundation’s Board of Trustees in June 2027. “It is an honor to serve the Tennessee Bar Foundation and its mission of promoting equal access to justice,” Carter said. “The Foundation’s work has a meaningful impact on communities across Tennessee by supporting organizations that provide vital legal services to those who otherwise could not afford them. I look forward to helping further that mission.” Established in 1982, the Tennessee Bar Foundation is one of the state’s leading legal organizations dedicated to advancing equal access to justice. The Foundation administers Tennessee’s Interest on Lawyers’ Trust Accounts (IOLTA) program, recognizes distinguished attorneys as Fellows — an honor bestowed upon fewer than 5 percent of Tennessee lawyers — and provides financial support for law-related public interest initiatives across the state. Through the IOLTA program, the Foundation expands access to legal services for Tennesseans in need. In 2025, the Foundation announced grants totaling $3 million to support Tennessee’s legal aid organizations and nonprofit groups that provide legal assistance to low-income residents and work to improve the administration of justice throughout the state. Carley Vaccher, driven by Spring Hill camaraderie and helping others (Williamson Herald) She knew she wanted to help people from a young age, and she knew her avenue to do so would be the medical field. What she didn’t expect was finding herself in the Spring Hill Fire Department as an EMT firefighter. Carley Vaccher has been at Spring Hill for a little more than a year, but previously found herself working as an EMT in emergency rooms, on an ambulance in California, and now, responding to emergencies in Williamson County. “This is the best job ever,” Vaccher said. “I love my crew. We’re a team. It’s like a family; we all take care of each other and look out for each other.” But it’s helping people that truly drives Vaccher. Problem solving. Trying to make someone’s worst day a little better—and she does so through prayer and kindness. “I’ve been on a handful of calls where they’re like, ‘Can you pray?’ So I’ll sit there and I’ll pray with them during the ambulance ride,” she said. “I’ll sit there and just cover them in prayer and try to talk to them, try to tell them what’s going on, let their family members know what’s going on so no one is kept in the dark, so that it can create a little bit more peace within this situation.” Knowing she wanted to work in the fire service came much later in life. She didn’t follow in anyone’s footsteps, become inspired by a certain individual, and didn’t even know anyone in the fire service until she was grown and decided she wanted to be in the medical field. “I really wanted to travel and be able to help people in different countries,” she said. “Then I was graduating (college) and wanted to join the Coast Guard.” Upon encouragement to take a “safer” route, her mother suggested the fire service. “I never really thought about that,” Vaccher said. “So I did a ride along with one of her friends and I instantly fell in love. I was like, ‘Yeah, this is the job for me.’” Sitting behind a desk, working on a computer, and being in an office from nine to five is simply not the life that Vaccher has ever chased. Since “we’re all blessed with different things,” she strives for a path directed toward helping, healing, and serving those in need. Vaccher remembers a specific dispatch, when she was on the ambulance and helped deliver a baby that was born with the umbilical cord around its neck. When they unwrapped the cord and the baby began wailing, the moment was so impactful to Vaccher that she ended up crying after the fact. “It was just the craziest thing. The baby’s alive! [Mom] just gave birth to life, you know?” she said. Vaccher’s EMT firefighting job is anything but easy, though. She must also face life’s hardest moments, respond to calls from grieving wives, and get hearts beating again. But it’s the comradery of her team that is unlike any support she faced in her other jobs. “There’s a group of us who genuinely care, and they care about you as their teammate but also you as an individual,” she said, noting that they often check in on each other after a difficult call. Her team often asks: “How’s your family? How’s your mental state? Was that rough on you?” “I feel like you don’t get that at a lot of jobs,” she said. At the end of the day, Carley Vaccher has found the job perfect for her. And it encompasses a second family, miraculous moments during ambulance rides, and comforting those who need it most. Kissing House welcoming new chapter with Maury County staples (Williamson County Source) A new chapter is coming to one of Spring Hill’s most recognizable historic homes. The Kissing House, which has been part of Spring Hill’s story for nearly 150 years, will soon become home to three local brands under one roof, as they shared on their social media. The concept will bring together The Kissing House, Loosewheels Squashed Burgers, and Babylon Beer Garden. The new gathering spot is expected to open this summer and will combine history, hospitality, and community in one location. Loosewheels Squashed Burgers will serve handcrafted burgers made fresh to order, while Babylon Beer Garden will offer a place to relax with a cold beer and good company on Fridays and Saturdays. Together, the businesses are aiming to create more than a restaurant. The vision is to build a place where people can gather with friends, celebrate, catch up, and slow down for a while. The Kissing House is located at 412 McLemore Ave, Spring Hill, TN 37174. More details, including menu items and the people behind the project, are expected to be shared in the coming weeks. In the past, The Kissing House was home to I Love Juice Bar and Brunch Collective. Now for a look at your hometown memorials… Norma Johnson Rosson, 96, a beloved mother, grandmother, great-grandmother, and cherished friend, passed away peacefully on Sunday, July 12, 2026, at The Bridge, where she had made her home for the past several years. Funeral services will be conducted Friday, July 17, 2026, at 11:00 a. m. at Oakes & Nichols Funeral Home with Andrew Phillips officiating. The family will receive friends Friday from 10:00 a. m. until the time of the service at the funeral home. Burial will follow at Polk Memorial Gardens. Charles Sloan Delk, 87, a Maury County native, longtime resident of Blue Ridge Road, and retired engineer with L&N Railroad/CSX Transportation, passed away peacefully at his residence on Tuesday, July 14, 2026. Graveside services will be conducted at 11:00 a.m. Saturday, July 18, 2026, at Polk Memorial Mausoleum with Rusty Colvett officiating. Bethany Lynne Secrest Hord, 58, a lifelong resident of Columbia, Tennessee, passed away peacefully on Sunday, July 13, 2026, at Maury Regional Medical Center. Funeral services will be conducted Friday at 2:30 p.m. at Oakes & Nichols Funeral Home with Rev. Tony Brown officiating. Burial will follow at Polk Memorial Gardens. The family will receive friends Friday from 12:30 p.m. until the time of the service at Oakes & Nichols Funeral Home. Online condolences may be extended at www.oakesandnichols.com. And now news from around the state… State reports new records in business filings, population growth stays strong (RELEASE) New business filings in the first quarter of 2026 were the highest for a first quarter in the 33-year history of the data, according to the new Quarterly Business and Economic Indicators report issued by Secretary of State Tre Hargett’s office. Tennessee also set an all-time record for annual reports filed in the quarter, showing that business resilience and creation remain exceptionally strong. “Our state leaders work diligently to offer a welcoming business environment that supports jobs and opportunities,” said Secretary Hargett. “Tennessee’s recipe of lower taxes, light regulations, and responsible fiscal management is why people from across the country continue to move here.” During the first quarter, 24,852 new entities filed in Tennessee, a 20.6% year-over-year increase. A high level of business filings typically leads to jobs, personal income, and state revenue growth. 241,706 entities filed annual reports in the quarter, a huge 30.4% year-over-year increase. Over the last 12 months, there were 84,893 new entity filings and 373,992 annual reports. Davidson and Knox counties saw the largest number of filings in the third quarter, followed by Shelby and Hamilton counties. These four most-populous counties accounted for 42.6% of new filings statewide and grew collectively by 20.1%. Tennessee’s other 91 counties grew even faster with year-over-year growth of 21%. The report also includes a feature on Tennessee’s population growth that increased nearly 64,000 in 2025 to reach 7.32 million people. Net domestic migration (people moving to Tennessee from other U.S. states minus people moving out of Tennessee) was the primary driver, but net international migration and natural growth (births minus deaths) also contributed. Although Tennessee’s growth has returned to pre-pandemic norms, it remains strong. Tennessee had the 4th largest net domestic migration in the nation in 2025, and the 5th largest as a share of its population. Davidson County saw the largest increase, and 81 of the state’s 95 counties gained population. “The entrepreneurial spirit in Tennessee really shines through in this record-breaking level of both new

card.description: Southern Middle TN's July 17 roundup covers a rural fire staffing crisis, record Tennessee business filings, and a Spring Hill EMT's story of delivering a baby on a call.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: What's in a name?

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a personal/lifestyle episode about the stories and meanings behind people's names, with only an incidental political reference (a listener named after Reagan); it has no substantive news or political content at its core.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear listener-callout format with an identifiable throughline — the host's own name story anchors the close — though pacing is loose and segment transitions are informal.

clipAbility.rationale: The Reagan/Ray name-change anecdote and the host's parents recounting the origin of 'Johnqulin' are emotionally resonant standalone moments, but most clips require at least minimal setup to land fully.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Tanning is back

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is a health and lifestyle explainer about tanning trends and sunscreen science, with only a passing reference to FDA approval — not substantively a news/politics episode.

contentStructure.rationale: Clear two-part structure moving from cultural tanning trend to expert dermatologist interview, with identifiable segments and a logical throughline, though ad breaks interrupt flow.

clipAbility.rationale: The dermatologist's flat rebuttal — 'That is not real. Sunscreen does not cause skin cancer' and 'There is no healthy tan' — are punchy, self-contained moments that would work as standalone previews.

scored.description:
Welcome to the New Era Woman Podcast—
where purpose meets leadership and faith in real life.

This is a space for women who are healing, building, and becoming aligned. 

Each episode offers real conversations, biblical wisdom, and practical strategies to help you strengthen your inner world, hear God with confidence, and walk in peace, love, and emotional steadiness.

Whether you’re new to faith, returning to God, or seeking deeper discipleship, you belong here. 

Visit www.NewEraWoman.org and follow @newerawomanpodcast on IG to stay connected.

Follow us on Instagram at @newerawomanpodcast

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-new-era-woman-network--4771294/support.

scored.description: A fresh and intelligent start to your day - catch the very latest international and domestic news developments, sport, entertainment and business on Early Edition with Ryan Bridge, on Newstalk ZB.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Camilla Belich: Labour Justice Spokesperson on consent laws

topicRelevance.rationale: Directly covers a specific New Zealand electoral policy dispute — National's consent law reform announcement versus Labour's bipartisan bill effort — with clear legislative and campaign stakes.

contentStructure.rationale: Short interview format with a clear question-and-answer throughline, though the segment ends abruptly and lacks a formal close beyond a sign-off.

clipAbility.rationale: The exchange where Belich argues passing the bill now guarantees it happens regardless of election outcome is a self-contained, quotable moment that works without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Full Show Podcast: 13 July 2026

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers multiple timely political and economic stories — New Zealand's India FTA controversy, UK government transition, Middle East ceasefire collapse and oil markets — making it substantively news-driven, though the breadth dilutes depth on any single topic.

contentStructure.rationale: The show follows a recognizable morning radio format with distinct segments (news, employment law interview, business with Vicky Price), but transitions are abrupt and pacing is driven by clock breaks rather than editorial throughline.

clipAbility.rationale: A few quotable moments exist — notably the John Bolton framing on peace deal futility and the AI-vs-coding curriculum observation — but most require surrounding context from the broadcast to land properly.

scored.description:
Join Todd Herman on The Todd Herman Show offering unfiltered commentary on today’s critical issues. A former Seattle radio host and frequent Rush Limbaugh fill-in, Todd delivers sharp Christian conservative perspectives on politics and more. With a background at Microsoft and as a startup CEO, he blends real-world experience with fearless analysis. Subscribe for daily episodes, exclusive blogs, and show notes via Substack. Perfect for listeners seeking truth in a complex world.


For more information, visit radioamerica.com/todd-herman-show/

scored.description: An archive of each full weekday episode of the award-winning program presented by Stephen Grootes.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The Best of the Money Show: The art of betting big & working less

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a business/entrepreneurship and workplace productivity show — a 'best of' compilation covering haircare entrepreneurship, four-day workweek research, and a fintech CEO's origin story, with no substantive connection to current news or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode is a loosely stitched 'best of' compilation with brief host transitions between segments, but the clips feel editorially disconnected and pacing is uneven across the sampled excerpt.

clipAbility.rationale: The Henry Ford/40-hour workweek passage and the 90% retention stat for four-day workweek trials are genuinely self-contained and quotable without much surrounding context.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: The Best of the Money Show: Why cellphone bills jump after contracts end & the business of fixing SA's cars

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode covers car repair economics and cellphone billing practices in South Africa — consumer finance content with no substantive connection to news or political events.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode has a discernible interview format with a caller Q&A segment, but pacing is loose and transitions between topics are conversational rather than deliberate.

clipAbility.rationale: The anecdote about the woman demanding to see the replaced car part is a usable standalone moment, but most exchanges require surrounding context to land.

latestEpisode.title: The Best of the Money Show: The art of betting big & working less

scored.description: Real America’s Voice: Real News, Honest Views - Dive into a world of unfiltered news, in-depth analysis, and thought-provoking discussions featuring some of your favorite personalities. At Real America’s Voice, we cover a wide array of topics—politics, culture, and current events—through the lens of American values and freedom. We pride ourselves on delivering honest content that challenges mainstream narratives. Our passionate hosts are experts and commentators who offer unique insights on the issues that matter most to you. Whether you start your day with American Sunrise, follow breaking news in the War Room with Steve Bannon, gain political insights from Charlie Kirk, explore cultural commentary with Jack Posobiec, or tune-in for the latest with investigative reporter John Solomon and Just The News, you’ll find all your favorite programs on RAV right here on the RAV podcast, including coverage of our live events featured on the network. Join the Real America’s Voice community to stay informed, engaged, and inspired. Subscribe now and never miss an episode!

episodeScores.episodeTitle: MEDIA EXPOSED WITH ADAM WEISS - JULY 19, 2026

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages directly with current political dynamics — Democratic candidate vetting failures, the 'Grand Platina' controversy, male voter realignment, and a Library of Congress America 250 gala — making it substantively tied to live political developments.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode shifts between a behavioral scientist interview, photo-narration of a gala, and media criticism without clear signposting or transitions, creating a loose, meandering feel.

clipAbility.rationale: The 'toxic masculinity has no scientific basis' exchange and the 'grown in vats' strategist quote are usable standalone moments, but most segments require surrounding context to land.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: WITCHES & DEMONS CLASH WITH LAW ENFORCEMENT, PNW BORDER UPDATES | LAW & BORDER

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly covers current immigration enforcement, state-level legislation, and northern border security — timely political subject matter with specific bills and legislative actors named, though the framing is advocacy rather than news reporting.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through identifiable segments (Portland protest footage, Idaho border interview, upcoming musical tribute) with clear transitions, but pacing is uneven and the segment breaks feel loosely stitched rather than deliberately structured.

clipAbility.rationale: The Idaho legislator's 'seventy percent of the dairy industry would fail an E-Verify audit' claim and the 'modern day slavery' exchange are self-contained and punchy enough to stand alone as preview clips without much setup.

card.tagline: Did Democrats vet their working-class candidate — or manufacture him?
card.description: A behavioral scientist and media critic dissect the Democratic Party's failed attempt to win back male voters by casting a blue-collar candidate with a Nazi tattoo and controversial Reddit history.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a live Democratic candidate controversy making headlines

scored.feedTitle: The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs
scored.description:
The Daily Mojo with Brad Staggs and Producer Ron Phillips is your first look each weekday, at the news, politics, faith, and absurdity that America is fast becoming. Brad and Producer Ron Phillips lens it all through a filter of truth, cynicism, and laughter (that’s getting tougher by the minute).

Our core value – We The People are at the heart of these United States and WE need to wrest our power back from the DC swamp dwellers. If you believe in freedom, personal responsibility, and individual rights, you may have found soulmates.

The Daily Mojo is life in the Conservatarian lane each weekday from 8-10a ET at TheDailyMojo.com, and wherever you get your favorite podcasts! Follow @RealBradStaggs @RealRonPhillips

Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-daily-mojo-with-brad-staggs--3085897/support.

card.tagline: Did Democratic politicians' bad judgment just hand Trump a weapon?
card.description: Brad Staggs and co-hosts dissect alleged misconduct by Swalwell and Ruben Diego, arguing poor personal judgment disqualifies them from office.
card.reason: Because this episode covers alleged misconduct by sitting Democratic lawmakers

scored.description: Your Source for Free Speech, Talk Radio, Podcasts, and News.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: When politics trumps the law

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages directly with current political developments — local data center moratoriums, a named Texas Senate candidate's public statements, and Second Amendment constitutional debate — making it substantively on-topic for a News/Politics audience.

contentStructure.rationale: The excerpt shows a clear pivot from data center policy to electoral politics and constitutional law, suggesting a segmented structure, though pacing is conversational and transitions are loose rather than deliberately signposted.

clipAbility.rationale: The Second Amendment breakdown — calling out a Senate candidate for misreading the very amendment he cited — is a genuinely self-contained, quotable moment that would work as a standalone preview without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Fighting back the Bolshevik Revolution of 2026

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode directly engages current geopolitical developments around Iran, IRGC threats against Trump, and U.S. foreign policy strategy, making it substantively tied to live news events as of mid-2026.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation meanders between Iran regime-change strategy, Trump security concerns, and guest self-promotion without clear segmentation or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: A few quotable moments exist — the '92% dead' regime claim and the Iranian constitution chapters calling for U.S. destruction — but most require surrounding context to land.

scored.description: The Moon Griffon Podcast offers insightful commentary on political and social issues with a blend of humor and wit. Moon Griffon frequently interviews local elected officials, state government watchdogs, and political analysts to provide listeners with truthful and engaging content. Listen his daily radio show Monday-Friday 9am-11am on Talk 107.3!

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Speaker Mike Johnson Sounds the Alarm on Election Security

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode centers on Speaker Mike Johnson, Republican vote margins, the 'big beautiful bill,' and election security — specific, current political content, though the transcript excerpt leans more toward general conservative commentary than a tightly focused policy analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode meanders from a Johnson interview segment into a loosely connected monologue about Republican defectors, Senate margins, and Democratic socialism without clear transitions or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: A few punchy lines land — the 'Boudreau's butt paste' quip and the 'palms greased' riff are colorful and self-contained — but most moments require surrounding context to land with full force.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Is China Trying to Sabotage America's AI Boom? The Data Center Battle Heating Up Now

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode addresses timely geopolitical and policy concerns — Chinese infrastructure infiltration, AI data center security, EV trade competition, and Texas energy legislation — with specific current examples, though the conversation is discursive rather than rigorously reported.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode meanders through data centers, grid security, Chinese EVs, and carbon lifestyle without clear segmentation or deliberate pacing, held together loosely by interview format rather than intentional structure.

clipAbility.rationale: The grid-spoofing explanation — where a guest describes how Chinese entities could fake power output to collapse the Texas grid — is a genuinely self-contained, alarming moment that would work as a standalone preview without much setup.

card.tagline: Speaker Johnson warns on elections while his own party keeps blocking him

card.reason: Because this episode covers Speaker Johnson's election security alarm and GOP vote margins

scored.description: Jim Webb Podcast—where real conversations meet sharp commentary. We dive into the latest trending topics, viral clips, and cultural debates, breaking them down with insight, honesty, and a touch of entertainment. Our goal is to cut through the noise, spark thought, and keep you engaged every step of the way. Hit that subscribe button and join the conversation today!

episodeScores.episodeTitle: DARRYL COOPER -  THE MINE WARS - America's Forgotten Civil War

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a historical narrative podcast about early 20th-century labor conflicts in West Virginia — analytically rich but not tied to any current news event, legislation, or political development.

contentStructure.rationale: The excerpt follows a clear narrative throughline moving from injunction enforcement to the Stanford Mountain killings to Mother Jones's organizing, with identifiable storytelling beats and a guest-host dynamic that provides pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: Mother Jones's direct quote — 'I'm not only advised the miners to buy rifles, but personally raise the money which paid for them' — is a genuinely standalone, punchy moment that needs minimal setup to land.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: DARRYL COOPER - Appalachia Culture pre-1900.

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is sharply focused on active U.S.-Iran military escalation, sanctions policy, the Strait of Hormuz, and Ukraine weapons manufacturing — all timely, specific geopolitical developments with direct policy implications.

contentStructure.rationale: There is a discernible throughline connecting multiple current events, but the host's commentary meanders between clips and topics without tight signposting or clear segment breaks.

clipAbility.rationale: The host's analysis of Trump's 'mob-style' negotiating logic and the laws of armed conflict around civilian targeting are quotable standalone moments, though most require at least minimal setup from the surrounding clip.

card.tagline: Six miners shot in their beds — and nothing was ever done about it
card.description: Darryl Cooper narrates the forgotten West Virginia Mine Wars, where coal operators deployed machine guns and bought judges to crush labor organizing by force.
card.reason: Because this episode recovers a violent chapter of American history most listeners have never heard

scored.feedTitle: The Right Stuff: A Blood Bowl Podcast
scored.description:
Yannie & Nathan talk about our in-store Blood Bowl League, Secret League! They go through each division, pick their favorites, and preview the week's matchups!

Hosts - David Yannarella & Nathan Starwalt
Audio Engineer - Scott Jensen 
Executive Producer - Wayne Knox

Don't forget to Like & Subscribe! Find all of our links here! https://linktr.ee/empiregamessc

scored.description:
Join the journey with David Knight, a true veteran in the realm of news and analysis, and become part of a community that values historical context, critical think, and spiritual insight in navigating the challenges of our era.  
LIVE every weekday morning at 9am Eastern where you'll find an honest, fresh perspective, not partisan cheerleading.  WIth a background as an electrical engineer, David is focused on the interplay of technology & liberty from a Christian perspective.

David has had decades of experience in the trenches of politics as an activist, organizer, candidate, reporter, analyst, and news anchor









Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-real-david-knight-show--5282736/support.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Fri Episode #2309:  Freedom Dies When Good Men Stay Silent

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode engages substantively with current surveillance technology policy concerns — Flock cameras, AI monitoring, police misconduct — anchored to specific recent incidents, making it clearly relevant to civil liberties and political commentary even if loosely structured around a news hook.

contentStructure.rationale: The excerpt flows as a loosely connected conversation between two hosts, moving from car surveillance to humanoid robots to Flock camera abuse without clear segmentation or deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: The Florida cop stalking story and the 'it's not that it can be abused, it's inherently abusive' line are genuinely self-contained and quotable moments that would work as standalone previews.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Fri Episode #2304:  — The UK Government Protected Child Predators for Decades

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode title references a specific UK government scandal, but the sampled transcript is dominated by religious-cultural commentary, immigration grievance framing, DEI criticism, and a book promotion — loosely political but without substantive reporting on specific current events or legislation.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation meanders between cultural commentary, biblical analogies, immigration rhetoric, and a book interview segment without clear transitions or deliberate pacing, though a loose guest-interview throughline is discernible.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context to land — the biblical leadership framework and immigration arguments are embedded in a flowing conversation with no punchy standalone hooks that would work as a 30-45 second preview.

card.tagline: Cops using Flock cameras to stalk women — and it's getting worse
card.description: David Knight breaks down real cases of police abusing license plate surveillance tech, humanoid home robots as spy tools, and the collapse of constitutional checks on power.
card.reason: Because this episode covers active surveillance abuses happening right now

scored.description: Welcome to Furniture Industry News the premier podcast dedicated to executives navigating the ever-evolving world of furniture. In a landscape where the latest trends, market shifts, and innovative technologies can redefine success overnight, our podcast stands as your authoritative source for everything you need to stay ahead. Tune in to stay informed, inspired, and in step with the future of furniture. Furniture Industry News – where leaders come to learn, share, and shape the future of the furniture world.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Washington's Billion-Dollar Warning to Furniture

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a furniture industry trade podcast covering import enforcement, sales data, freight rates, and retail trends — industry/commodity content that scores in the 0-20 range despite referencing federal enforcement actions, as the episode's core purpose is furniture business intelligence, not political or news analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode opens with a clear agenda, moves through distinct segments in the promised order (enforcement, design sentiment, sales data, freight, product news), with explicit transitions and a consistent format throughout.

clipAbility.rationale: The closing line of the enforcement segment — 'the cheapest container on the market might turn out to be the most expensive one you ever bought' — and the importer-of-record liability explanation are genuinely self-contained and quotable without much setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Everyone's Waiting. Buffett's Successor Isn't

topicRelevance.rationale: This is a furniture industry trade podcast covering supply chain, retail trends, and sector M&A — not a News/Politics show, despite incidental references to tariffs and trade policy.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode moves through clearly delineated segments — Berkshire/housing, Sleep Number bankruptcy, consumer behavior data, port volumes, supplier restructuring, and a compliance warning — each with a distinct setup and actionable close.

clipAbility.rationale: Several segments open with self-contained hooks (the Sleep Number auction deadline, the customs seizure reveal, the record import forecast) that could stand alone as 30-45 second previews without much setup.

00:01:40,390 misclassifying finished goods as parts and evading tariffs and 42 00:01:40,390 --> 00:01:41,390 anti dumping duties. 43 00:01:41,790 --> 00:01:43,870 We have already seen this play out in our space. 44 00:01:44,190 --> 00:01:46,750 In one case, an outdoor furniture producer agreed to pay 45 00:01:46,750 --> 00:01:50,230 $4.9 million to resolve allegations that it mischaracterized 46 00:01:50,230 --> 00:01:52,430 imported aluminum components to avoid duties. 47 00:01:52,880 --> 00:01:55,200 In another, a building products company pleaded guilty and 48 00:01:55,200 --> 00:01:58,200 was fined more than $6.3 million for its role in a scheme 49 00:01:58,200 --> 00:02:00,640 to route plywood through a third country to dodge duties on 50 00:02:00,640 --> 00:02:01,440 Chinese goods. 51 00:02:01,680 --> 00:02:04,000 Now here is why this reaches your desk. 52 00:02:04,000 --> 00:02:07,560 Even if you think your house is in order, the government has said 53 00:02:07,560 --> 00:02:11,160 plainly that the importer of record is responsible for the accuracy 54 00:02:11,160 --> 00:02:11,920 of an entry. 55 00:02:12,080 --> 00:02:15,520 Even when a broker or an overseas factory prepared the paperwork, 56 00:02:15,760 --> 00:02:18,640 you cannot sign that responsibility away in a contract. 57 00:02:18,880 --> 00:02:22,320 And saying the factory told you so may not be much of a defense. 58 00:02:22,810 --> 00:02:25,810 The guidance is that companies are expected to audit their supply 59 00:02:25,810 --> 00:02:28,970 chains and to avoid deliberately ignoring warning signs. 60 00:02:29,210 --> 00:02:32,610 So when a supplier tells you production moved from China to Vietnam 61 00:02:32,610 --> 00:02:33,450 or Cambodia. 62 00:02:33,530 --> 00:02:36,890 The question is whether real manufacturing actually moved or whether 63 00:02:36,890 --> 00:02:38,730 the product is just being relabeled. 64 00:02:38,810 --> 00:02:42,050 And when one supplier's landed price is dramatically below what 65 00:02:42,050 --> 00:02:43,130 everyone else can achieve,. 66 00:02:43,290 --> 00:02:44,650 Someone needs to ask why. 67 00:02:45,050 --> 00:02:47,010 For the honest operators out there, this. 68 00:02:47,010 --> 00:02:48,250 Is genuinely good news. 69 00:02:48,650 --> 00:02:50,290 If you pay the correct duties, test. 70 00:02:50,290 --> 00:02:52,450 Your products and document your sourcing, you. 71 00:02:52,450 --> 00:02:54,130 Should not have to compete against sellers. 72 00:02:54,130 --> 00:02:56,410 Whose low prices are built on false invoices. 73 00:02:56,570 --> 00:02:59,370 This enforcement push levels a field that has been tilted. 74 00:02:59,530 --> 00:03:03,210 The practical move this week is know where your product came from, 75 00:03:03,290 --> 00:03:06,570 know what was declared, and know what was paid, because in this 76 00:03:06,570 --> 00:03:09,170 environment, the cheapest container on the market might turn 77 00:03:09,170 --> 00:03:11,370 out to be the most expensive one you ever bought. 78 00:03:11,850 --> 00:03:15,570 Let's shift to something more upbeat Design and construction professionals 79 00:03:15,570 --> 00:03:18,050 are heading into the third quarter feeling more confident than 80 00:03:18,050 --> 00:03:21,290 they have all year, According to the latest Houzz Pro Industry 81 00:03:21,290 --> 00:03:24,730 barometer, the design sector is showing especially strong momentum, 82 00:03:24,730 --> 00:03:27,610 driven by more project inquiries and new committed work. 83 00:03:27,850 --> 00:03:30,530 The measure that tracks expected business activity for the 84 00:03:30,530 --> 00:03:34,330 design sector rose to 68 for the third quarter, up 8 points from 85 00:03:34,330 --> 00:03:37,570 60 in the second quarter, and the measure of recent activity jumped 86 00:03:37,570 --> 00:03:41,050 to 62, up 14 points from just 48 in the first quarter. 87 00:03:41,210 --> 00:03:43,490 That is a real swing in mood now. 88 00:03:43,490 --> 00:03:45,290 It is not all smooth sailing. 89 00:03:45,450 --> 00:03:49,290 Project backlogs actually slipped a bit, down to about 4.7 90 00:03:49,290 --> 00:03:53,030 weeks, and interior designers running closer to 3.9 weeks. 91 00:03:53,190 --> 00:03:55,270 And the pressures are still very real. 92 00:03:55,670 --> 00:03:58,990 Roughly half of design firms point to rising product and material 93 00:03:58,990 --> 00:04:02,390 costs, and about half point to client financial uncertainty. 94 00:04:02,950 --> 00:04:05,750 Here's the part worth noting for how you work with the trade. 95 00:04:06,070 --> 00:04:09,430 When these pros feel cost pressure, their most common response 96 00:04:09,430 --> 00:04:12,750 is to raise their prices, and their second response is to get more 97 00:04:12,750 --> 00:04:14,870 selective about which projects they take on. 98 00:04:15,430 --> 00:04:18,870 Designers are also leaning harder on repeat clients and referrals. 99 00:04:19,290 --> 00:04:22,650 So if you sell into or alongside the design community, this 100 00:04:22,650 --> 00:04:26,050 is your signal to make your value obvious and your relationship 101 00:04:26,050 --> 00:04:29,450 sticky because pros are consciously narrowing down who they 102 00:04:29,450 --> 00:04:30,250 say yes to. 103 00:04:30,810 --> 00:04:33,970 That optimism in the design channel makes an interesting contrast 104 00:04:33,970 --> 00:04:37,290 with our next story, which is the hard numbers on furniture sales. 105 00:04:37,610 --> 00:04:41,450 After seven straight months of year over year declines, the furniture 106 00:04:41,450 --> 00:04:44,490 and home furnishings category finally pulled even in June. 107 00:04:45,070 --> 00:04:49,270 Store sales came in at about $11.3 billion, which was essentially 108 00:04:49,270 --> 00:04:52,270 flat compared to May and essentially flat compared to June 109 00:04:52,270 --> 00:04:53,070 of last year. 110 00:04:53,470 --> 00:04:56,710 Year to date, the category is running about two points behind where 111 00:04:56,710 --> 00:04:58,030 it was in 2025. 112 00:04:58,590 --> 00:05:01,990 Now, flat is better than falling, so this counts as stabilizing. 113 00:05:01,990 --> 00:05:05,150 But flat is not growth and the context matters. 114 00:05:05,470 --> 00:05:09,190 Total retail across all categories rose in June and no major 115 00:05:09,190 --> 00:05:11,070 category posted an annual decline. 116 00:05:11,610 --> 00:05:15,090 Auto dealers were strong, E commerce was strong and even sporting 117 00:05:15,090 --> 00:05:16,410 goods and hobby stores were up. 118 00:05:16,810 --> 00:05:20,570 So the takeaway is shoppers have money and they are spending 119 00:05:20,570 --> 00:05:20,810 it. 120 00:05:20,890 --> 00:05:23,890 They are just choosing not to spend it on big ticket home goods 121 00:05:23,890 --> 00:05:24,330 right now. 122 00:05:24,650 --> 00:05:27,850 That makes value and a clear reason to buy the whole game this 123 00:05:27,850 --> 00:05:28,330 summer. 124 00:05:28,570 --> 00:05:31,610 And keep an eye on the strength of online retail because 125 00:05:31,610 --> 00:05:35,210 even the buyer who ultimately wants to sit on a sofa in your showroom 126 00:05:35,210 --> 00:05:38,250 is very likely researching and comparing online first. 127 00:05:38,920 --> 00:05:42,320 Your digital presence is doing sales work long before anyone walks 128 00:05:42,320 --> 00:05:42,920 through the door. 129 00:05:43,400 --> 00:05:44,520 On the supply chain. 130 00:05:44,520 --> 00:05:47,400 There is a little relief and a lot of uncertainty. 131 00:05:47,720 --> 00:05:50,920 Container freight rates edged lower this week as the early peak 132 00:05:50,920 --> 00:05:52,440 season started to lose steam. 133 00:05:52,680 --> 00:05:59,080 Drury's World Container Index fell 2% to about $4,547 per container, 134 00:05:59,240 --> 00:06:01,880 coming down from a nearly two year high the week before. 135 00:06:02,360 --> 00:06:04,480 The route from Shanghai to Los Angeles. 136 00:06:04,480 --> 00:06:07,350 Slipped a bit with while Shanghai to New York held steady. 137 00:06:07,670 --> 00:06:10,030 Carriers are trimming sailings to keep supply. 138 00:06:10,030 --> 00:06:12,030 In line with demand and they tried. 139 00:06:12,030 --> 00:06:13,830 To push through some big rate increases. 140 00:06:13,830 --> 00:06:15,830 In mid July that simply did not hold. 141 00:06:16,230 --> 00:06:18,230 But here is the cloud over all of it. 142 00:06:18,470 --> 00:06:22,230 Tensions involving Iran are escalating again, and that puts pressure 143 00:06:22,230 --> 00:06:24,950 on some of the most important shipping chokepoints in the world. 144 00:06:25,270 --> 00:06:28,190 There are real concerns about vessels transiting the Strait of 145 00:06:28,190 --> 00:06:31,190 Hormuz and about continued threats in the Red Sea. 146 00:06:31,750 --> 00:06:34,870 On top of that, current tariffs are scheduled to expire on 147 00:06:34,870 --> 00:06:38,470 July 24, with additional tariffs expected in early August. 148 00:06:38,710 --> 00:06:42,270 So the front loading rush that inflated rates is fading, which helps 149 00:06:42,270 --> 00:06:43,830 your landed costs in the near term. 150 00:06:43,990 --> 00:06:47,669 But you have two known dates on the calendar and a security situation 151 00:06:47,670 --> 00:06:48,790 that just got worse. 152 00:06:49,030 --> 00:06:52,230 If you haven't done it yet, this is a good time to model your 153 00:06:52,230 --> 00:06:55,030 fall ordering against a scenario where rates spike again 154 00:06:55,030 --> 00:06:55,990 on a disruption. 155 00:06:56,230 --> 00:06:58,470 The value here is not this week's number. 156 00:06:58,860 --> 00:07:01,660 It is not getting caught flat footed if the risk turns real. 157 00:07:02,300 --> 00:07:04,460 Now for a story that is a little unusual. 158 00:07:04,780 --> 00:07:08,300 Moto Motion, the fast growing maker of Smart Motion furniture, 159 00:07:08,380 --> 00:07:11,860 had to correct one of

card.tagline: DOJ's billion-dollar trade fraud crackdown just became furniture's problem
card.description: A new federal enforcement section is targeting false country-of-origin claims and transshipment schemes — and furniture importers are squarely in the crosshairs.
card.reason: Because this episode breaks down a federal crackdown affecting furniture importers directly

scored.description:
Join the journey with David Knight, a true veteran in the realm of news and analysis, and become part of a community that values historical context, critical think, and spiritual insight in navigating the challenges of our era.  
LIVE every weekday morning at 9am Eastern where you'll find an honest, fresh perspective, not partisan cheerleading.  WIth a background as an electrical engineer, David is focused on the interplay of technology & liberty from a Christian perspective.

David has had decades of experience in the trenches of politics as an activist, organizer, candidate, reporter, analyst, and news anchor









Become a supporter of this podcast: https://www.spreaker.com/podcast/the-david-knight-show--2653468/support.

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode is squarely focused on current U.S. political dynamics — 2028 presidential field speculation, Trump's second-term political engagement, congressional budget reconciliation battles, and assassination threat concerns — all timely and substantive.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation follows a loose but identifiable throughline moving from Democratic 2028 contenders to Republican field to Trump's political activity to congressional dynamics, though transitions are conversational rather than deliberately signposted.

clipAbility.rationale: Several self-contained moments work as standalone clips — notably the 'Democrat socialist movement is nationally unelectable' take, the 'three assassination attempts' exchange, and the 'my party has become fiscally irresponsible' admission — each punchy enough to preview without heavy setup.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Contact Politics #51-US Congressman Darin LaHood (R-IL)

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode covers timely 2026 midterm election dynamics, NRCC strategy, Democratic Party internal tensions, and specific races in New York, Colorado, and Maine, giving it clear news relevance, though the sports diplomacy opening dilutes focus.

contentStructure.rationale: The conversation meanders between sports diplomacy, political analysis, and candidate-specific commentary without clear segmentation, and the hosts frequently interrupt or redirect without deliberate pacing.

clipAbility.rationale: LaHood's three-point Republican majority argument (candidate quality, resources, Democratic radicalism) is the most self-contained passage, but most exchanges require surrounding context to land and the crosstalk reduces standalone usability.

card.tagline: GOP strategist calls out his own party's fiscal recklessness on live air
card.description: Republican insider Mike Dennehy breaks down the 2028 field, Trump's rally absence, assassination threat concerns, and why he thinks the GOP has abandoned fiscal responsibility.
card.reason: Because this episode maps the emerging 2028 presidential race in real time

scored.description: After many years on national television as a broadcaster for The Shopping Channel, Norm Murray has turned his attention to the people, events, and news from his own backyard – Peel Region. A Mississauga resident, Norm travels to various happenings across the GTA to find out – what IS “The Norm” in Peel? On-air, Norm invites conversation, as he engages in thorough discussion through live interviews. Notable interviews include: performers of The Living Arts Centre, Paramount Fine Foods Centre, and The International Centre – everyday people who entertain the community, specifically those who shy away from the “norm,” subsequently establishing a new “norm.” As he likes to say, “we’ve got a lot to talk about.”

episodeScores.episodeTitle: It’s Wild In The West.

topicRelevance.rationale: The episode touches on real news items — a Mississippi Medicaid fraud indictment, a foiled mass shooting, and a criminal case update — but coverage is anecdotal, reactive, and loosely structured around personal commentary rather than substantive political or policy analysis.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode meanders between multiple unrelated stories with frequent personal digressions (watching TV with dad, cooking dinner) and no clear segmentation or throughline connecting the topics.

clipAbility.rationale: Most moments require surrounding context to land, and the host's frequent filler phrases and self-interruptions make it difficult to isolate a clean, standalone 30-45 second excerpt.

latestEpisode.title: It’s Wild In The West.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Are Extraterrestrials Real or Are They Us in the Future

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is a speculative UFO/extraterrestrial discussion with no substantive grounding in current news, legislation, or political developments — it is fringe entertainment content, not News/Politics.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode meanders through loosely connected ideas — government denial, time travel theory, movie references — without clear segments, signposting, or a disciplined throughline.

clipAbility.rationale: A few quotable lines exist (e.g., 'it's either extraterrestrial or it's us in the future') but most moments require surrounding context and the rambling delivery undermines standalone usability.

episodeScores.episodeTitle: Autopsy - The Death of Local Terrestrial Radio

topicRelevance.rationale: This episode is a media industry retrospective about radio consolidation and commercial load strategy — entertainment/media business content with no substantive political, legislative, or policy angle at its core.

contentStructure.rationale: The episode follows a clear analytical throughline — consolidation causes, commercial load failures, technology response — with identifiable segments, though pacing is conversational and occasionally repetitive.

clipAbility.rationale: The 'listeners view a 10-second commercial the same as a 60-second commercial' insight and the '13-element break' explanation are quotable, but most moments require the surrounding buildup to land with full impact.

the years and the decades to know something is going on. 00:23:05.920 --> 00:23:08.799 And our government is well aware of it. 00:23:08.960 --> 00:23:34.079 But something that I have been thinking about for a long time, I've read a couple of books, and I'm wondering, especially when you hear reports of people saying, yeah, our government actually works with these beings, and that's how we have been able to create some of our technology, like our stealth bomber technology, something that can fly and not be detected by radar. 00:23:34.240 --> 00:23:44.079 A lot of people believe that is the result of technology that was taught to us by extraterrestrial beings. 00:23:44.480 --> 00:23:51.519 But what if, if that's true, why does it have to be extraterrestrial? 00:23:52.000 --> 00:24:16.240 And when you look at movies like Signs, for example, you know, the Mel Gibson movie where you actually see these beings and they actually come into his home and they have to try that they're all portrayed the same way as being human-like in that they walk upright, they have arms, they have legs, they have a head, they have these large eyes. 00:24:16.480 --> 00:24:21.119 Um but we have seen this depiction. 00:24:21.680 --> 00:24:32.880 So how do we know that those things are or are not extraterrestrial? 00:24:33.119 --> 00:24:49.039 Is it possible that these beings and these things that we're seeing are nothing more than our own technology from the future? 00:24:49.200 --> 00:24:56.160 I mean, it very advanced technology that we were able to create in the future. 00:24:56.720 --> 00:25:16.640 And the way these beings physically looked was the way that they adapted to maybe a new atmosphere or any changes that happened in the atmosphere, and that that is simply the way humans evolve in the future in order to sustain life here. 00:25:17.279 --> 00:25:23.519 And the reason that they keep coming here is because they know what's going on down here. 00:25:23.680 --> 00:25:32.559 They know what we're capable of, they know about the nuclear, the nuclear technologies, they know about the bombs, they know about the weapons, they know about the wars. 00:25:32.720 --> 00:25:43.920 And who's to say that they are not so incredibly afraid of losing their society way into the future that that's why they're coming here? 00:25:44.160 --> 00:26:02.079 So they can try and prevent things or report on things, or at the very least, just keep a watchful eye over all these things to make sure that we don't do anything crazy, like start a nuclear war and destroy the whole thing, which might endanger their existence later in the future. 00:26:02.240 --> 00:26:08.960 And honestly, that makes much more sense to me than anything else. 00:26:09.200 --> 00:26:13.599 But I also happen to believe that are we alone? 00:26:14.720 --> 00:26:16.079 I don't see how. 00:26:16.400 --> 00:26:31.839 In this vast universe, and there are still galaxies out there that we don't know anything about yet, but in this vast universe, I find it hard to believe that we could be the only intelligent life out there. 00:26:32.319 --> 00:26:40.960 And underline that word intelligent, because if we are, then that speaks volumes about the universe. 00:26:41.119 --> 00:26:48.240 If we are the only intelligent life out there, the future's doomed. 00:26:48.799 --> 00:27:09.119 But if it's simply us in the future evolving both physically and technologically to the point where in the future we have the capability of going back in time, time travel, through some kind of a portal. 00:27:09.200 --> 00:27:15.519 And again, there's no scientific evidence to prove these portals exist, but a lot of people do believe that they exist. 00:27:16.960 --> 00:27:23.119 And that there are many different dimensions, and it just depends on which one you go to. 00:27:23.279 --> 00:27:29.200 So there's there's so much for the believer, and there's an equal amount even for the non-believer. 00:27:29.279 --> 00:27:42.480 I'm not saying that that's what I believe these things are, us in the future, and the crafts that we see is the result of our enhanced technology thousands of years into the future. 00:27:42.640 --> 00:27:43.839 I'm not saying that is. 00:27:43.920 --> 00:27:50.240 I'm also not saying it isn't, and I'm also not discounting the idea of extraterrestrials. 00:27:50.640 --> 00:27:54.720 I mean, we we we've only scratched the surface of what's out there. 00:27:54.880 --> 00:28:16.240 They're already learning with the Mars rover and other telescopes that they have used over the years, that Mars, for example, there is evidence to indicate that there might have been some kind of life on Mars way back when. 00:28:17.200 --> 00:28:24.160 I mean, what, you know, like Star Wars, you know, millions and millions of years ago, uh, that is possible. 00:28:24.319 --> 00:28:26.400 They're finding strange things on Mars. 00:28:26.480 --> 00:28:35.759 They're finding strange things on Jupiter, they're finding strange things related to the moons that Jupiter orbits. 00:28:36.640 --> 00:28:39.920 So there's an awful lot out there. 00:28:40.160 --> 00:28:44.319 Whether or not they are extraterrestrial, I don't know. 00:28:45.119 --> 00:28:53.039 But the only reason I don't think that's necessarily true is why have they never attacked? 00:28:53.200 --> 00:28:59.039 Why have they never shown any kind of aggression, like they're gonna take over the planet? 00:28:59.200 --> 00:29:12.799 Which is why, in my mind, it makes more sense that maybe this is nothing more than us in the future using the technology that we have developed over the decades and the centuries. 00:29:12.960 --> 00:29:22.720 And they're trying to maintain their society, and it requires them to be here to try and put a stop to anything that could jeopardize their future. 00:29:23.440 --> 00:29:37.599 I know that's a little deep, but that does make sense to me when you wonder and you see movies like War of the Worlds, where these things attack because uh, you know, they've used up all the resources and and and they move from planet to planet. 00:29:37.759 --> 00:29:42.480 And as soon as all the resources have been consumed, they move on to the next planet. 00:29:42.640 --> 00:29:43.920 I mean, that's possible. 00:29:44.079 --> 00:29:50.480 You know, Independence Day, that that that is possible, but it hasn't happened yet. 00:29:50.640 --> 00:30:04.559 We haven't seen anything, even with these stories of people seeing these beings coming out of these crafts that are almost always described as very oddly shaped. 00:30:04.880 --> 00:30:12.720 And the most important characteristic is the fact there's no noise whatsoever. 00:30:13.519 --> 00:30:16.559 Absolute dead silence. 00:30:17.599 --> 00:30:19.440 There are no chemtrails. 00:30:20.079 --> 00:30:32.160 Now, there is a video that's circulating from something that was spotted, I don't know if it was over Arizona, but it was some cylindrical thing that was vertical. 00:30:32.240 --> 00:30:37.359 You know, from it had a top and it had a bottom, and and and it was cylindrical. 00:30:37.440 --> 00:30:47.119 And as it moved, it almost looks like the air behind it is disturbed, meaning that it's leaving a trail of some kind. 00:30:47.680 --> 00:30:52.480 But if they have, it it it hasn't been detected yet. 00:30:52.559 --> 00:30:58.400 But people do report almost inevitably every time, these things do not make a sound. 00:30:58.640 --> 00:31:04.799 And they have different light patterns and things like that that again don't make any noise. 00:31:05.039 --> 00:31:08.400 But the patterns that they demonstrate don't really make any sense. 00:31:08.640 --> 00:31:28.000 And but anyway, it is it, I think it's a great thing that the government is finally coming out and at least open to the possibility of talking to the general public and saying, yeah, we we we we do still have a few cases that we really just don't know. 00:31:28.480 --> 00:31:32.000 And to their credit, they're not saying one way or the other. 00:31:32.160 --> 00:31:35.680 They're certainly not saying that, yeah, it's extraterrestrial. 00:31:35.839 --> 00:31:40.079 And they're certainly not saying it isn't extraterrestrial. 00:31:40.319 --> 00:31:44.960 The fact is, they're saying they really just don't know that it is unusual. 00:31:45.119 --> 00:31:51.200 And yes, these things have happened in front of hundreds and thousands of people in places all over the world. 00:31:51.279 --> 00:31:58.799 I mean, not just here in the United States, but there are sightings all over the world of these things. 00:31:59.839 --> 00:32:23.519 So, but the fact that nothing aggressive has happened, at least to our knowledge, I find it hard to believe unless it is a race of extraterrestrials who are just so curious about what we're doing down here that they are they're fascinated. 00:32:24.160 --> 00:32:28.799 And they are keeping an eye on things to make sure that maybe we don't go too far. 00:32:29.759 --> 00:32:34.640 Um, but it's it's not that they're angry or anything like that. 00:32:34.799 --> 00:32:40.799 They're just fascinated by what we do here on Earth. 00:32:41.279 --> 00:32:43.279 And maybe they're trying to figure that out. 00:32:43.359 --> 00:32:46.640 They're trying to document it, they're trying to figure it all out. 00:32:46.880 --> 00:32:49.920 So it's either extraterrestrial or it's us in the future. 00:32:50.000 --> 00:32:51.599 I mean, I think it's one of the two. 00:32:51.759 --> 00:33:08.480 I think the days of denying that there are extraterrestrials or beings or strange ships out there that have come from other places, I think the days of denying that are over because I think enough has happened that certainly leaves the possibility open that anything is still possible. 00:33:08.720 --> 00:33:11.039 The extraterrestrials could be out there. 00:33:11.279 --> 00:33:25.279 We could have alien spacecraft out there, but it it's a very good thing that the government is now at least talking about it and they've established a formal way of being able to report. 00:33:25.440 --> 00:33:30.400 Again, the general public doesn't really have a way to do that, but they are working on that. 00:33:30.559 --> 00:33:42.880 I mean, the general public can call, you know, if if there's something near an Air Force base somewhere or some sort of military base and something is happening, more often than not, these military bases, their phones go absolutely nuts. 00:33:43.039 --> 00:33:45.759 And people are asking, what is that in the sky? 00:33:46.079 --> 00:33:50.079 And the only thing that they can really say is, well, we haven't seen anything yet. 00:33:50.240 --> 00:33:53.599 We're not seeing anything on our radar, but we're looking into it. 00:33:53.839 --> 00:33:55.359 But again, it's there. 00:33:55.519 --> 00:34:01.359 And they're getting hundreds of calls from people saying, What the hell is that up there? 00:34:01.680 --> 00:34:06.480 And when you look at the way it's moving, something is wrong here. 00:34:06.640 --> 00:34:09.840 Something is not right about the way that thing is moving. 00:34:10.159 --> 00:34:12.320 The whole thing is just very fascinating. 00:34:12.480 --> 00:34:21.599 But Interstellar, the movie with Matthew McConaughey, does touch on that working theory, or I guess it really isn't a theory at this point. 00:34:21.679 --> 00:34:25.039 It's really more a hypothesis than it
